Above tolerable line"] C --> E["TOLERABLE REGION
Between two lines
ALARP zone"] E --> F["ACCEPTABLE REGION
Below negligible line"] style D fill:#ff6666 style E fill:#ffff66 style F fill:#66ff66 ``` ## 4.6 Block Diagram — Hazard-Risk Relationship ```mermaid flowchart LR A["HAZARD
Potential for Harm
(Latent Condition)"] --> B["LIKELIHOOD
Probability of Event"] C["SEVERITY
Magnitude of Harm"] --> D["RISK
R = L x S"] B --> D A --> B A --> C D --> E["RISK BAND
Low / Med / High / Extreme"] E --> F["CONTROL
MEASURES"] ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 PART A QUESTIONS (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1: Define hazard and risk. Differentiate between them with one industrial example. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]` **Model Answer (3 marks):** **Hazard:** A hazard is any source, situation, or act with the potential to cause harm to people, property, or the environment. It is an *intrinsic property* that makes harm possible, regardless of whether harm actually occurs. **Risk:** Risk is the combination of the *likelihood* (probability) that a hazardous event will occur and the *severity* (consequence) of the harm it causes. Mathematically: $R = L \times S$. **Differentiation:** | Aspect | Hazard | Risk | |--------|--------|------| | Nature | A condition/source | A measure of probability × consequence | | Existence | Always present (latent) | Quantified after assessment | | Example | A pressurized gas cylinder | Probability of cylinder explosion × fatality impact | **Industrial Example:** A chemical reactor containing toluene is a **hazard**. The **risk** is the probability of a toluene leak (rated 3) multiplied by the severity of toxic exposure (rated 4), giving $R = 12$ (High Risk). --- ### Question 2: List any four types of industrial hazards with one example each. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` **Model Answer (3 marks):** The KTU syllabus recognizes six major types of hazards. Any four with examples: 1. **Physical Hazard:** Noise above 85 dB in a stamping unit. 2. **Chemical Hazard:** Sulfuric acid mist in electroplating. 3. **Biological Hazard:** Legionella bacteria in cooling towers. 4. **Ergonomic Hazard:** Repetitive wrist motion causing Carpal Tunnel Syndrome in data entry operators. 5. **Mechanical Hazard:** Unguarded rotating shaft causing entanglement. 6. **Psychosocial Hazard:** Excessive overtime leading to chronic fatigue in call-center employees. ## 5.2 PART B QUESTIONS (14 Marks Each) > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Each Part B question has internal choice between **Question A** and **Question B** (either-or). Both have sub-parts (a) for **7 marks** and (b) for **7 marks**. --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** **`[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`** #### (a) [7 Marks] Explain the concept of hazard and risk with the help of suitable industrial examples. Discuss the formula $R = L \times S$ and its significance. **Model Answer:** **Definition of Hazard:** A hazard is any source or situation with the potential to cause harm in terms of human injury, ill-health, damage to property, or environmental damage. A hazard is a *latent* condition — it does not necessarily cause harm until certain events trigger it. **Industrial Examples of Hazards:** - **Chemical Hazard:** Ammonia gas cylinder rupture risk in a refrigeration plant. - **Physical Hazard:** Excessive noise (>85 dB) in a forging press unit. - **Mechanical Hazard:** Unguarded flywheel of a heavy lathe. **Definition of Risk:** Risk is the product of the *likelihood* (probability) of a hazardous event occurring and the *severity* of harm caused. Risk quantifies the *practical* danger a hazard poses under real operating conditions. **Formula and Significance:** $$R = L \times S$$ Where: - **L** = Likelihood (1 to 5 scale: Rare, Unlikely, Possible, Likely, Almost Certain) - **S** = Severity (1 to 5 scale: Negligible, Minor, Moderate, Major, Catastrophic) - **R** = Risk Score (1 to 25) **Significance:** 1. Provides a **numerical basis** for prioritization of safety actions. 2. Helps management **allocate resources** to the highest-risk hazards first. 3. Forms the foundation for **Quantitative Risk Assessment (QRA)**. 4. Enables **comparison** across different industrial processes. 5. Required for **statutory compliance** with ISO 45001 and Indian Factories Act. **Worked Example:** A forklift operates near pedestrian walkways. The likelihood of collision is 4 (Likely), and severity of injury is 4 (Major). $$\begin{aligned} R &= 4 \times 4 \\ R &= 16 \quad \text{(Extreme Risk)} \end{aligned}$$ This mandates immediate action: speed limiters, segregated walkways, mirrors, and operator training. --- #### (b) [7 Marks] Describe the five-step risk management process as per KTU/ISO 31000 framework. Illustrate with a block diagram. **Model Answer:** **Step 1 — Hazard Identification:** The first step is to systematically identify all potential sources of harm. Methods include: - HAZOP (Hazard and Operability Study) - FMEA (Failure Mode and Effects Analysis) - Checklists and walk-through surveys - Brainstorming sessions with workers **Step 2 — Risk Estimation:** For each identified hazard, the likelihood (L) and severity (S) are determined using a 5×5 matrix or event-tree analysis. **Step 3 — Risk Evaluation:** The calculated risk score $R = L \times S$ is compared against pre-defined acceptability criteria: - Low Risk (1–4): Acceptable - Medium Risk (5–9): Monitor - High Risk (10–15): Reduce - Extreme Risk (16–25): Halt operations **Step 4 — Risk Control:** Apply controls in order of effectiveness (Hierarchy of Controls): 1. **Elimination** (most effective) 2. **Substitution** 3. **Engineering controls** 4. **Administrative controls** 5. **PPE** (least effective) **Step 5 — Monitoring and Review:** Continuously re-assess and audit the controls. Risk is dynamic — it changes with new equipment, processes, or personnel. **Block Diagram:** ``` Hazard ID → Risk Estimation → Risk Evaluation → Risk Control → Monitoring ↑ | └───────────── Feedback / Review Loop ─────────────────────┘ ``` **Real-World Application:** A petroleum refinery uses this five-step framework daily for Permit-to-Work systems, ensuring every maintenance activity is risk-assessed before execution. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks)** *(Alternative Choice)* **`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`** #### (a) [7 Marks] Classify the various types of industrial hazards. Explain any three with suitable examples and control measures. **Model Answer:** Industrial hazards are broadly classified into the following types: **1. Physical Hazards:** - Definition: Energy forms (noise, heat, radiation) that can cause bodily harm. - Examples: Noise >85 dB in a press shop, UV radiation from welding arc, high-temperature furnace surfaces. - Controls: Acoustic enclosures, ear muffs, heat-resistant PPE, radiation shields. **2. Chemical Hazards:** - Definition: Substances that can cause health damage via inhalation, ingestion, or skin contact. - Examples: Benzene exposure causing leukemia, sulfuric acid burns in plating shops. - Controls: Local Exhaust Ventilation (LEV), Material Safety Data Sheets (MSDS), chemical-resistant gloves, neutralization stations. **3. Biological Hazards:** - Definition: Living organisms or their by-products that can cause infection or allergy. - Examples: Legionella in cooling towers, mold in HVAC ducts. - Controls: Disinfection protocols, HEPA filtration, vaccination programs. **4. Ergonomic Hazards:** - Definition: Workplace design factors causing musculoskeletal disorders. - Examples: Repetitive assembly-line motions, awkward postures in confined spaces. - Controls: Ergonomic tool redesign, job rotation, micro-breaks. **5. Psychosocial Hazards:** - Definition: Work organization and social factors causing mental stress. - Examples: Excessive workload, workplace bullying, monotony. - Controls: Employee Assistance Programs (EAP), workload management, counseling. **6. Mechanical Hazards:** - Definition: Hazards from machinery with moving parts. - Examples: Rotating shafts, unguarded blades, pinch points. - Controls: Machine guarding, interlocks, Lockout-Tagout (LOTO) procedures. --- #### (b) [7 Marks] Construct a 5×5 Risk Assessment Matrix for a chemical process industry. Explain with a numerical example how the matrix is used to prioritize safety actions. **Model Answer:** **5×5 Risk Assessment Matrix:** The matrix is constructed with **Likelihood (L)** along the columns and **Severity (S)** along the rows. Each cell contains the Risk Score $R = L \times S$. | Severity $\downarrow$ \ Likelihood $\rightarrow$ | 1 (Rare) | 2 (Unlikely) | 3 (Possible) | 4 (Likely) | 5 (Certain) | |--------------------------------------------------|----------|--------------|--------------|-----------|-------------| | **5 (Catastrophic)** | 5 | 10 | 15 | 20 | 25 | | **4 (Major)** | 4 | 8 | 12 | 16 | 20 | | **3 (Moderate)** | 3 | 6 | 9 | 12 | 15 | | **2 (Minor)** | 2 | 4 | 6 | 8 | 10 | | **1 (Negligible)** | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5 | **Risk Bands:** $$\begin{aligned} \text{Low} &: 1 \leq R \leq 4 \quad \text{(Acceptable, monitor periodically)} \\ \text{Medium} &: 5 \leq R \leq 9 \quad \text{(Reduce through controls)} \\ \text{High} &: 10 \leq R \leq 15 \quad \text{(Immediate action, stop if needed)} \\ \text{Extreme} &: 16 \leq R \leq 25 \quad \text{(Halt operations, mandatory redesign)} \\ \end{aligned}$$ **Numerical Example:** A chemical reactor operates with styrene monomer (carcinogenic, flammable). Process hazard analysis yields: - Likelihood of runaway reaction: **L = 3** (Possible) - Severity of consequence (vessel rupture, fire): **S = 5** (Catastrophic) $$\begin{aligned} R &= 3 \times 5 \\ R &= 15 \quad \text{(High Risk)} \end{aligned}$$ **Action Plan (Prioritization using Matrix):** 1. Immediate: Install rupture discs and emergency vent systems. 2. Short-term: Add temperature/pressure interlocks and DCS-based alarms. 3. Long-term: Substitute styrene with safer vinyl ester where possible. 4. Continuous: Train operators, conduct monthly mock drills, audit relief valves. **Outcome:** Likelihood reduces from $L = 3$ to $L = 2$, while severity may remain $S = 5$ (catastrophic potential inherent to flammable liquids). Residual risk: $$\begin{aligned} R_{\text{residual}} &= 2 \times 5 = 10 \\ \end{aligned}$$ Still in High band; further controls such as remote operation, foam suppression, and bund walls may reduce to Medium risk (ALARP achieved). --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:** > > 1. **Do NOT confuse Hazard with Harm.** Hazard is *potential*; Harm is *realized*. Many students lose 2 marks by stating them as synonyms. > 2. **Always show the multiplication** in $R = L \times S$ with the actual numerical values. Writing just the formula without numerical substitution fetches only partial marks (typically 1 out of 3). > 3. **Hierarchy of Controls must be memorized in order.** Examiners allocate 2 marks specifically for correct sequencing (Elimination → Substitution → Engineering → Administrative → PPE). > 4. **Risk Matrix coloring/cell values** are often asked — students forget that diagonal and off-diagonal values differ. Be sure to populate the entire 5×5 grid, not just one row or column. > 5. **Avoid using bare absolute value symbols `|...|` in tables** — always use `\vert` or `\mid` in LaTeX to keep tables valid. ## 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Hazard and Risk; Types of Hazards** - [ ] **Hazard** = source/situation with *potential* for harm (latent, intrinsic). - [ ] **Risk** = combination of Likelihood × Severity ($R = L \times S$). - [ ] **Risk Score Range:** 1 to 25 (5×5 matrix, dimensionless). - [ ] **Risk Bands:** Low (1–4), Medium (5–9), High (10–15), Extreme (16–25). - [ ] **Hazard Sequence:** Hazard Source → Hazardous Event → Hazardous Situation → Harm. - [ ] **Six Major Hazard Classes:** Physical, Chemical, Biological, Ergonomic, Psychosocial, Mechanical (plus Electrical and Fire/Explosion). - [ ] **Hierarchy of Controls (5 levels, most→least effective):** Elimination, Substitution, Engineering, Administrative, PPE. - [ ] **Five-Step Risk Management:** Identify → Estimate → Evaluate → Control → Monitor. - [ ] **Residual Risk Formula:** $R_{\text{residual}} = R_{\text{inherent}} - R_{\text{control}}$. - [ ] **ALARP Principle:** As Low As Reasonably Practicable (legal/safety benchmark). - [ ] **Acceptable Individual Risk (IR):** $\leq 10^{-6}$ per person per year. - [ ] **F-N Curve:** Plot of cumulative frequency (F) vs. number of fatalities (N). - [ ] **KTU Examiner keywords to use in answers:** "potential," "likelihood," "severity," "ALARP," "hierarchy of controls," "residual risk." - [ ] **Common pitfall:** Avoid mixing $| \cdot |$ in markdown tables — use `\vert` or `\mid`. --- *End of Module 4 Notes — Hazard and Risk; Types of Hazards* *Prepared in accordance with KTU 2024 Scheme B.Tech Examination Pattern*

topics.title: Classification of Fire; Types of Fire Extinguishers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEAUT415 - INDUSTRIAL SAFETY ENGINEERING > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Classification of Fire; Types of Fire Extinguishers ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition of Fire (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **FIRE** is defined as a rapid, self-sustaining oxidation process accompanied by the evolution of heat and light, occurring whenever a combustible substance, an oxidizer (typically atmospheric oxygen at **21%** concentration by volume), and a sufficient source of ignition energy are brought together under favourable conditions. For a fire to initiate, propagate, and sustain itself, the simultaneous interaction of **three essential components** is mandatory. The removal or disruption of any one of these components will cause the combustion reaction to cease. > [!NOTE] > **The Fire Triangle (Triangle of Combustion)** > The Fire Triangle is a universally accepted pedagogical model representing the three indispensable elements required for combustion: > 1. **FUEL** — The combustible material (solid, liquid, or gas). > 2. **OXYGEN** — The oxidizing agent (ambient air, chemical oxidizers). > 3. **HEAT** — The ignition source reaching the activation energy threshold. ### 1.2 Classification of Fire (As per IS 15683 / NFPA 10 / KTU Standard) Fires are broadly classified into **six (6) primary classes** based on the **physical state and chemical nature of the burning fuel**. This classification is critical because it directly dictates the **extinguishing medium** that must be deployed. > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Fire Classification Standard Table** | Class | Fuel Type | Common Examples | Industrial Occurrence | |:------|:----------|:----------------|:----------------------| | **Class A** | Ordinary Combustibles (Solids) | Wood, Paper, Cloth, Rubber, Plastics, Coal | Offices, Warehouses, Textile Mills | | **Class B** | Flammable Liquids & Gases | Petrol, Diesel, LPG, Solvents, Paints | Refineries, Paint Shops, Chemical Plants | | **Class C** | Flammable Gases (under pressure) | Methane, Propane, Hydrogen, Acetylene | Gas Plants, Welding Shops | | **Class D** | Combustible Metals | Magnesium, Aluminium, Sodium, Potassium, Titanium | Foundries, Aerospace, Metal Processing | | **Class E** *(or Class K in US)* | Electrical Fires (Energized) | Live wiring, Transformers, Motors, Control Panels | Server Rooms, Control Rooms, Substations | | **Class F** | Cooking Oils & Fats | Vegetable oils, Animal fats at high temperatures | Commercial Kitchens, Food Processing Units | > [!WARNING] > **NOTE FOR KTU EXAM:** In the European / Indian (BIS) classification, electrical fires are designated as **Class E**. In the American (NFPA) classification, this is **Class C** (Electrical) and cooking oil fires are separately designated as **Class K**. Always mention both terminologies in your answers to demonstrate comprehensive understanding. ### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Campfire Model > [!IMPORTANT] > **INTUITIVE ANALOGY: The Campfire Scenario** > Imagine you are trying to light a campfire in the woods. You gather dry leaves and twigs (FUEL), you strike a match to start it (HEAT), and wind provides the necessary air (OXYGEN). The fire catches and grows. > > - **To put it out with water** → You are removing the HEAT (cooling effect). > - **To put it out with sand/soil** → You are removing the OXYGEN (smothering effect). > - **To put it out by removing the wood** → You are removing the FUEL (starvation). > > This is precisely the foundational logic behind ALL fire extinguishing agents. Different extinguishers use different mechanisms (cooling, smothering, chain-breaking) to attack a specific "side" of the fire triangle. ### 1.4 Definition of a Fire Extinguisher > [!NOTE] > **FIRE EXTINGUISHER** — A portable, manually operated fire-fighting appliance consisting of a pressurised vessel (cylinder), an extinguishing agent (medium), and a discharge mechanism (nozzle/horn), designed to combat incipient-stage fires by interrupting one or more elements of the Fire Triangle. The three primary mechanisms of extinguishment are: 1. **Cooling** — Removal of heat (e.g., Water extinguisher). 2. **Smothering / Blanketing** — Exclusion of oxygen (e.g., Foam, CO₂, Dry Powder). 3. **Chain-Breaking (Inhibition)** — Interruption of the chemical reaction at the molecular level (e.g., Dry Chemical Powder on Class B/C fires). ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Mechanism of Extinguishment — Detailed Logic Every fire extinguishing agent, irrespective of brand or composition, operates on one (or a combination) of the following three principles. The selection of an extinguisher is purely a function of which side of the Fire Triangle it can safely and effectively disrupt. #### Step 1: Identify the Fuel Class The first logical step is to identify the **Class of Fire** (A, B, C, D, E, or F). Misidentification is the single largest cause of extinguisher failure in industrial accidents. #### Step 2: Match the Extinguishing Medium to the Fuel Class Different fuels have different physical properties (e.g., water is denser than petrol, electricity conducts through water, oil reacts violently with water). Hence, the extinguishing medium must be **chemically and physically compatible** with the fuel. #### Step 3: Apply the Correct Mechanism The extinguisher deploys its agent using one of the following methods: - **Cooling** — Reduces the fuel temperature below its **flash point** and **fire point**. - **Smothering** — Reduces the oxygen concentration below the **Limiting Oxygen Concentration (LOC)** — typically below **12%** for most hydrocarbons. - **Chain-Breaking** — Inhibits the free-radical chain reaction in the flame zone (Halon, Dry Chemical Powder). ### 2.2 Types of Fire Extinguishers — Complete Theoretical Breakdown #### (A) Water-Type Extinguishers - **Variants:** Plain Water, Water Spray, Water Mist. - **Mechanism:** Pure **cooling effect**. Water has a high **specific heat capacity of 4.186 kJ/kg·K** and high **latent heat of vaporisation of 2,260 kJ/kg**, making it exceptionally effective at absorbing heat. - **Applicable Classes:** **Class A only** (Solid combustibles). - **NOT Applicable:** Class B (water sinks below flammable liquids, may cause splashing and fire spread), Class C, Class D, Class E (water conducts electricity), Class F (water causes explosive eruption of burning oils). #### (B) Foam-Type Extinguishers (AFFF / AR-AFFF) - **Composition:** Aqueous Film-Forming Foam (AFFF) or Alcohol-Resistant AFFF (AR-AFFF) mixed with water at **3% or 6% concentration**. - **Mechanism:** Dual action — **cooling** (water component) + **smothering** (foam blanket cuts off oxygen supply). - **Applicable Classes:** **Class A and Class B**. - **Industrial Use:** Oil refineries, petrochemical storage tanks, airport fire tenders. #### (C) Dry Chemical Powder (DCP) Extinguishers - **Composition:** Finely divided monoammonium phosphate (for ABC) or sodium bicarbonate / potassium bicarbonate (for BC). - **Mechanism:** **Chain-breaking (chemical inhibition)** — interrupts the free-radical combustion reaction. Secondary effect: light **smothering** as the powder cloud envelops the flame. - **Applicable Classes:** **Class A, B, C** (Multi-purpose ABC powder) or **Class B, C** only (BC powder). - **Industrial Use:** Most widely used extinguisher in Indian industries due to its versatility. #### (D) Carbon Dioxide (CO₂) Extinguishers - **Composition:** Liquefied CO₂ stored at approximately **58 bar (≈ 850 psi)** pressure at **21 °C**. - **Mechanism:** **Smothering** — displaces atmospheric oxygen by discharging cold CO₂ gas. The expansion also causes significant **cooling** (the dry ice effect, temperature reaches **−78 °C** at the discharge horn). - **Applicable Classes:** **Class B and Class E** (electrical). NOT effective on Class A. - **Industrial Use:** Electrical control rooms, server rooms, switchgear rooms, laboratories. - **Important:** CO₂ leaves **no residue**, making it ideal for protecting sensitive electronic equipment. #### (E) Wet Chemical Extinguishers - **Composition:** Potassium acetate / citrate solution (typically **50–60% concentration**) with added surfactants. - **Mechanism:** **Saponification** — reacts with hot cooking oils to form a thick, soapy foam layer that seals the surface and prevents re-ignition. - **Applicable Classes:** **Class F (K)** only. - **Industrial Use:** Commercial kitchens, food processing industries. #### (F) Clean Agent / Halon-Replacement Extinguishers - **Composition:** FM-200 (HFC-227ea), Novec 1230 (C₆F₁₂O), or inert gases (argon, nitrogen). - **Mechanism:** **Chain-breaking** — disrupts the chemical reaction of combustion without leaving residue and without depleting oxygen below breathable levels. - **Applicable Classes:** **Class B, C, E**. - **Industrial Use:** Data centres, aircraft engine compartments, museums, archival storage. #### (G) Special Powder Extinguishers (for Class D — Metal Fires) - **Composition:** Sodium chloride (NaCl), graphite powder, copper powder, or sodium carbonate. - **Mechanism:** Forms a **crust/blanket** over the burning metal, isolating it from oxygen. - **Applicable Classes:** **Class D only**. - **Industrial Use:** Foundries, metal machining workshops, aerospace manufacturing. ### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (High-Yield) > [!NOTE] > **Key Physical Constants & Standards for Fire Extinguishment** | Parameter / Formula | Value / Expression | Unit / Remarks | |:-------------------|:-------------------|:---------------| | Specific Heat Capacity of Water | $c_p = 4.186$ | $\mathrm{kJ/(kg \cdot K)}$ | | Latent Heat of Vaporisation of Water | $L_v = 2{,}260$ | $\mathrm{kJ/kg}$ | | Total Heat Absorption Capacity of Water | $Q = m \cdot (c_p \cdot \Delta T + L_v)$ | $\mathrm{kJ}$ (used in Class A) | | Limiting Oxygen Concentration (LOC) | $\leq 12\%$ | by volume (most hydrocarbons) | | Atmospheric Oxygen Concentration | $21\%$ | by volume (normal air) | | Flash Point of Petrol | $\approx -43$ | $^\circ\mathrm{C}$ | | Fire Point of Petrol | $\approx -21$ | $^\circ\mathrm{C}$ | | Auto-Ignition Temperature of Diesel | $\approx 210$ | $^\circ\mathrm{C}$ | | Auto-Ignition Temperature of Vegetable Oil | $\approx 320$ | $^\circ\mathrm{C}$ | | CO₂ Storage Pressure | $\approx 58$ | $\mathrm{bar}$ at 21 °C | | CO₂ Discharge Horn Exit Temperature | $-78$ | $^\circ\mathrm{C}$ (dry ice temperature) | | NFPA 10 Discharge Time (minimum) | $\geq 9$ | $\mathrm{seconds}$ (Class B) | | Extinguisher Travel Distance (Class A) | $\leq 22.5$ | $\mathrm{metres}$ | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Note on Mathematical Operators in Tables:** All absolute value or modular quantities in this document are written using the $\vert \cdot \vert$ notation to preserve the integrity of the markdown table structure. Please do not use raw vertical pipes inside table cells. ### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility The selection of a fire extinguisher is not merely a safety formality but a **critical engineering decision** with the following implications: - **Insurance Premium Reduction** — Properly classified and maintained extinguishers reduce industrial insurance premiums by **15–30%**. - **Legal Compliance** — Mandatory under the **Factories Act 1948 (India)**, **NFPA 10 (USA)**, **IS 15683 (India)**, and **OSHA 29 CFR 1910.157**. - **Process Continuity** — CO₂ or Clean Agent extinguishers are used in semiconductor fabs and pharmaceutical clean rooms where water damage would destroy equipment worth millions of rupees. - **Hazardous Area Classification** — In petroleum refineries, only intrinsically safe and explosion-proof extinguishers may be deployed in **Zone 1 and Zone 2 hazardous areas**. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation: Heat Absorption Capacity of a Water Extinguisher A water-type extinguisher containing **9 litres** of water is used to extinguish a Class A fire. We must determine the **total heat (Q)** absorbed by the water from ignition temperature to complete vaporisation. **Given:** - Mass of water, $m = 9\ \mathrm{kg}$ (1 litre of water $\approx$ 1 kg) - Initial temperature, $T_1 = 20\ ^\circ\mathrm{C}$ (ambient) - Final temperature, $T_2 = 100\ ^\circ\mathrm{C}$ (boiling point of water) - Specific heat capacity, $c_p = 4.186\ \mathrm{kJ/(kg \cdot K)}$ - Latent heat of vaporisation, $L_v = 2{,}260\ \mathrm{kJ/kg}$ **Step 1:** Calculate the temperature rise. $$\Delta T = T_2 - T_1 = 100 - 20 = 80\ ^\circ\mathrm{C}$$ **Step 2:** Compute the sensible heat required to raise the water temperature from ambient to its boiling point. $$Q_{\text{sensible}} = m \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T$$ $$Q_{\text{sensible}} = 9 \cdot 4.186 \cdot 80$$ $$Q_{\text{sensible}} = 3{,}013.92\ \mathrm{kJ}$$ **Step 3:** Compute the latent heat required to completely vaporise the water. $$Q_{\text{latent}} = m \cdot L_v$$ $$Q_{\text{latent}} = 9 \cdot 2{,}260$$ $$Q_{\text{latent}} = 20{,}340\ \mathrm{kJ}$$ **Step 4:** Sum both quantities to obtain the total heat absorbed. $$Q_{\text{total}} = Q_{\text{sensible}} + Q_{\text{latent}}$$ $$Q_{\text{total}} = 3{,}013.92 + 20{,}340$$ $$Q_{\text{total}} = 23{,}353.92\ \mathrm{kJ} \approx 23.35\ \mathrm{MJ}$$ **Result and Engineering Interpretation:** $$Q_{\text{total}} \approx 23.35\ \mathrm{MJ}$$ This is the **total heat-extraction capacity** of a single 9-litre water extinguisher. Considering that the average heat release rate of a typical chair (wooden, Class A) is approximately **$\dot{Q} = 250\ \mathrm{kW}$**, the **time-to-extinguish** would be: $$\Delta t = \frac{Q_{\text{total}}}{\dot{Q}} = \frac{23{,}353.92}{250}$$ $$\Delta t \approx 93.4\ \mathrm{seconds}$$ This justifies why **water extinguishers are highly effective on Class A fires** and explains why **NFPA 10 mandates a minimum discharge time of 9 seconds for Class A extinguishers** to ensure complete suppression. ### 3.2 Step-by-Step Extinguisher Selection Logic (Decision Tree) The following analytical matrix illustrates the **stepwise decision process** for selecting an appropriate extinguisher in an industrial environment. **Step 1: Identify the burning fuel.** Classify the fire into one of A, B, C, D, E, or F based on visual and contextual cues. **Step 2: Eliminate unsafe media.** - If the fire is **electrical (Class E)** → ELIMINATE water and foam (conductive). - If the fire involves **cooking oil (Class F)** → ELIMINATE water (causes flash steam explosion). - If the fire involves **sodium or magnesium (Class D)** → ELIMINATE water (releases hydrogen gas and intensifies combustion). **Step 3: Apply the primary mechanism.** - For Class A → Use **water, foam, or ABC powder**. - For Class B → Use **foam, ABC powder, or CO₂**. - For Class C → Use **ABC powder or CO₂** (shut off gas source first). - For Class D → Use **special Class D powder**. - For Class E → Use **CO₂ or clean agent**. - For Class F → Use **wet chemical**. **Step 4: Consider the secondary environment.** - If sensitive electronics are nearby → **CO₂ or clean agent** (no residue). - If outdoor windy location → **foam or powder** (CO₂ disperses rapidly). - If the area is occupied → **CO₂ is dangerous** (asphyxiation risk in confined spaces). ### 3.3 Worked Example: Multi-Hazard Industrial Scenario **Scenario:** A petroleum refinery storage yard contains: - Diesel storage tanks (Class B liquid) - A control room with PLCs and switchgear (Class E) - Wooden pallets stacked nearby (Class A) - A canteen using vegetable oil for frying (Class F) **Required extinguisher deployment:** | Location | Fire Class | Recommended Extinguisher | Reason | |:---------|:-----------|:------------------------|:-------| | Diesel tank farm | Class B | Foam (AFFF) or ABC Powder | Smothers + cools; non-conductive | | Control room | Class E | CO₂ or Clean Agent (FM-200) | No residue, non-conductive | | Pallet yard | Class A | Water or ABC Powder | High heat capacity | | Canteen kitchen | Class F | Wet Chemical (Potassium Acetate) | Saponification reaction | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering Insight:** This multi-hazard scenario demonstrates the **non-interchangeability** of extinguishers. A CO₂ extinguisher placed in the kitchen is a **zero-effect tool** on Class F fires, and a water extinguisher in the control room is a **lethal electrocution hazard**. This is a frequently examined KTU 2024 concept. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 The Fire Triangle (Foundation Diagram) ```mermaid graph TD A[FUEL

KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model Paper]** **Q1. (3 Marks)** Define the Fire Triangle. List the three essential elements required for combustion and explain how each type of fire extinguisher disrupts one specific element. **Model Answer:** The Fire Triangle is a foundational safety model that represents the three indispensable components required for combustion to occur: 1. **FUEL** — Any combustible material (solids like wood, liquids like petrol, or gases like methane). 2. **OXYGEN** — Typically atmospheric oxygen at **21%** concentration by volume. 3. **HEAT** — The ignition source providing activation energy to raise the fuel to its auto-ignition temperature. Extinguishers disrupt these elements as follows: - **Water extinguisher** removes **HEAT** (cooling via latent heat of vaporisation of **2,260 kJ/kg**). - **CO₂ extinguisher** removes **OXYGEN** (displacement of air; reduces O₂ below LOC of **12%**). - **Dry Chemical Powder** interrupts the chemical chain reaction at the molecular level (chain-breaking). > [!WARNING] > **Valuation Pitfall:** Students often lose marks by stating only the names of extinguishers without explaining the *mechanism* of disruption. Always explicitly mention **which side of the triangle** is being attacked. > **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model Paper]** **Q2. (3 Marks)** Differentiate between Class B and Class C fires with two examples each. State the most suitable extinguisher for each class. **Model Answer:** | Parameter | Class B Fire | Class C Fire | |:----------|:-------------|:-------------| | Fuel Type | Flammable Liquids (e.g., petrol, diesel, paint) | Flammable Gases (e.g., LPG, methane, hydrogen) | | Physical State | Liquid at room temperature | Gaseous at room temperature | | Extinguishing Strategy | Smothering + Chain-breaking | Shut off gas source + Chain-breaking | | Best Extinguisher | Foam (AFFF) or ABC Dry Powder | ABC Dry Powder or CO₂ | | Why NOT Water | Water sinks below liquid, causes splashing and fire spread | Water cannot displace gas effectively; may cause explosion | The primary distinction is the **physical state** of the fuel — Class B involves liquids (requiring smothering with foam blanket) whereas Class C involves gases (requiring immediate gas shut-off and powder-based chain-breaking). --- ### PART B — Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) > **[KTU University Exam - University Model Paper - Module 4]** --- #### **QUESTION A — 14 MARKS** **(a) (7 Marks)** Classify fires into different classes as per the Indian Standard (IS 15683). For each class, state two examples of fuel and identify the correct extinguishing medium with justification. **Model Answer:** The Indian Standard **IS 15683 / NFPA 10** classifies fires into the following six classes: | Class | Fuel Description | Two Examples | Recommended Extinguisher | Justification | |:------|:-----------------|:-------------|:------------------------|:--------------| | **A** | Ordinary Combustibles (Solids) | Wood, Paper, Cloth, Rubber | Water / ABC Powder | Water provides maximum cooling; ABC powder smothers | | **B** | Flammable Liquids | Petrol, Diesel, Solvents, Paints | Foam (AFFF) / ABC Powder | Foam forms blanket cutting off oxygen | | **C** | Flammable Gases | LPG, Methane, Hydrogen | Dry Chemical Powder / CO₂ | Powder interrupts chain reaction; shut off source first | | **D** | Combustible Metals | Magnesium, Sodium, Aluminium | Special Class D Powder (NaCl) | Forms crust; isolates metal from oxygen | | **E** | Electrical (Energised) | Motors, Transformers, Control Panels | CO₂ / Clean Agent (FM-200) | Non-conductive; no residue damage | | **F** | Cooking Oils and Fats | Vegetable Oil, Animal Fat | Wet Chemical (Potassium Acetate) | Saponification creates soapy seal | **Key Justification Principle:** Water must NEVER be used on Class B, C, D, E, or F fires because: - On Class B → Water sinks and splashes burning liquid. - On Class D → Water reacts with sodium/magnesium, releasing hydrogen gas. - On Class E → Water conducts electricity, causing electrocution. - On Class F → Water instantly vaporises into superheated steam, causing a "flash fire" explosion. **(b) (7 Marks)** Explain in detail the construction, working principle, and industrial applications of a **Carbon Dioxide (CO₂) Fire Extinguisher**. Why is it preferred over water extinguishers for electrical fires? **Model Answer:** **Construction of CO₂ Fire Extinguisher:** A CO₂ extinguisher consists of the following components: 1. **Cylinder** — Seamless steel cylinder (typically **2 kg, 4.5 kg, or 22.5 kg** capacity), painted **signal red** with a **black identification band** (as per IS 15683). 2. **CO₂ Charge** — Liquefied carbon dioxide stored at approximately **58 bar (≈ 850 psi)** at 21 °C. 3. **Valve Assembly** — Brass valve with safety release set at approximately **180–200 bar**. 4. **Discharge Hose** — High-pressure reinforced rubber hose (**length ≈ 0.5–1 m**). 5. **Discharge Horn** — A non-metallic (fibreglass) horn attached to the hose end. The horn is critical because it expands the gas and prevents frosting of the operator's hand. 6. **Safety Pin and Lever** — Standard squeeze-grip operating mechanism. 7. **No Refill Pressure Gauge** — Unlike water extinguishers, CO₂ cylinders have **no pressure gauge** because the cylinder can be weighed to verify charge. **Working Principle:** When the lever is squeezed: 1. The valve opens, allowing liquefied CO₂ to flow up the siphon tube. 2. The liquefied CO₂ expands rapidly as it exits through the discharge horn. 3. **Approximately 50%** of the CO₂ discharge is in the form of **solid "dry ice" snow** at **−78 °C**, while the other **50%** is gaseous CO₂. 4. The cold gaseous CO₂ **displaces atmospheric oxygen**, reducing the oxygen concentration around the fire below the **Limiting Oxygen Concentration (LOC) of 12%**. 5. Simultaneously, the cold dry ice provides a **cooling effect** on the fuel surface. 6. Combined smothering + cooling extinguishes the fire rapidly. **Industrial Applications:** 1. **Electrical Control Rooms** — For PLCs, switchgear, transformer rooms. 2. **Server Rooms and Data Centres** — Leaves **no residue**, protecting sensitive electronics. 3. **Laboratories** — Chemical spills involving flammable solvents (Class B). 4. **Paint Booths** — Flammable liquid fires in automotive paint shops. 5. **Printing Presses** — Solvent-based ink fires. **Why CO₂ is Preferred Over Water for Electrical Fires:** | Property | CO₂ Extinguisher | Water Extinguisher | |:---------|:-----------------|:-------------------| | Electrical Conductivity | Non-conductive | Highly conductive | | Residue | None | Corrosive water damage | | Damage to Equipment | Negligible | Severe short-circuit risk | | Operator Safety (Class E) | Safe (with ventilation) | Lethal electrocution hazard | Since water conducts electricity, spraying it on an energised electrical fire can: - Cause **electrocution of the operator**. - Create **short-circuits** that damage equipment worth millions. - Cause the fire to **spread through conductive water films**. Therefore, **CO₂ is the universally preferred extinguisher for Class E (electrical) fires** in industrial installations. --- #### **QUESTION B — 14 MARKS (ALTERNATIVE)** **(a) (7 Marks)** Discuss the various **types of fire extinguishers** based on the extinguishing agent used. Compare their working principles with appropriate sketches/tables. **Model Answer:** Fire extinguishers are classified based on the extinguishing agent they contain. The major types are: **1. Water-Type Extinguishers** - **Agent:** Plain water, water with additives (anti-freeze, wetting agents). - **Mechanism:** **Cooling** — water absorbs heat through its high specific heat (4.186 kJ/kg·K) and high latent heat of vaporisation (2,260 kJ/kg). - **Use:** Class A fires only. - **Limitation:** Cannot be used on Class B, C, D, E, F fires. **2. Foam-Type Extinguishers (AFFF / AR-AFFF)** - **Agent:** Aqueous Film-Forming Foam mixed with water at 3% or 6% concentration. - **Mechanism:** Dual action — water cools + foam blankets and smothers. - **Use:** Class A and Class B fires (especially hydrocarbon fuels). - **Limitation:** Cannot be used on electrical or Class D fires. **3. Dry Chemical Powder (DCP) Extinguishers** - **Agent:** Monoammonium phosphate (ABC) or sodium bicarbonate (BC). - **Mechanism:** **Chain-breaking** — interrupts free-radical combustion reaction. - **Use:** Class A, B, C (multi-purpose ABC powder) or Class B, C (BC powder). - **Advantage:** Most versatile; widely used in Indian industries. **4. Carbon Dioxide (CO₂) Extinguishers** - **Agent:** Liquefied CO₂ at 58 bar pressure. - **Mechanism:** Smothering (oxygen displacement) + cooling (dry ice at −78 °C). - **Use:** Class B and Class E fires. - **Advantage:** No residue; safe for electrical equipment. **5. Wet Chemical Extinguishers** - **Agent:** Potassium acetate / citrate solution (50–60% concentration). - **Mechanism:** **Saponification** — reacts with hot cooking oil to form soap-like foam. - **Use:** Class F fires (cooking oils, fats). **6. Clean Agent / Halon Replacement Extinguishers** - **Agent:** FM-200 (HFC-227ea), Novec 1230, inert gases. - **Mechanism:** Chain-breaking without oxygen depletion. - **Use:** Class B, C, E fires in enclosed sensitive areas (server rooms, museums). **7. Class D Powder Extinguishers** - **Agent:** Sodium chloride (NaCl), graphite, copper powder. - **Mechanism:** Forms inert crust isolating metal from oxygen. - **Use:** Class D metal fires (magnesium, sodium, aluminium). **Comparative Summary Table:** | Type | Agent | Class | Mechanism | Limitation | |:-----|:------|:------|:----------|:-----------| | Water | Water | A | Cooling | Conducts electricity | | Foam | AFFF | A, B | Cooling + Smothering | Conducts electricity | | DCP | Phosphate | A, B, C | Chain-breaking | Leaves residue | | CO₂ | CO₂ | B, E | Smothering | Asphyxiation risk | | Wet Chemical | Potassium Acetate | F | Saponification | Limited to kitchens | | Clean Agent | FM-200 | B, C, E | Chain-breaking | Costly | | Class D Powder | NaCl | D | Blanketing | Metal-specific | **(b) (7 Marks)** A workshop contains diesel storage, an electrical control panel, and a welding station with acetylene cylinders. Identify the fire classes present in each area and recommend the **specific extinguisher type with capacity** for each location. Justify your selection using the principles of fire extinguishment. **Model Answer:** | Workshop Area | Fire Class | Recommended Extinguisher | Capacity | Justification | |:--------------|:-----------|:------------------------|:---------|:--------------| | **Diesel Storage (1000 L)** | Class B | Foam (AFFF) Type | **50 L wheeled trolley** | Foam blanket prevents splash-back and re-ignition; larger capacity for bulk fuel storage | | **Electrical Control Panel** | Class E | CO₂ Type | **4.5 kg** portable | Non-conductive, no residue damage to electronics, safe on live equipment up to 1 kV | | **Welding Station (Acetylene)** | Class C | ABC Dry Powder | **6 kg** stored-pressure | Acetylene is a flammable gas; ABC powder interrupts chain reaction; shut off cylinder valve first | **Justification Logic:** 1. **Diesel Storage — Foam Extinguisher:** - Diesel is a Class B flammable liquid with a flash point of **$\geq 55\ ^\circ\mathrm{C}$** and density greater than water. - If water is used, it sinks below the diesel surface, gets superheated, and causes **frothover** (boilover), violently spreading the fire. - AFFF foam has a **density lower than diesel** and floats on top, forming an airtight blanket that cuts off oxygen and prevents re-ignition. - The **50 L wheeled trolley** is mandated because diesel storage exceeds **500 L**, which falls under the **high-hazard occupancy category** under NFPA 10. 2. **Electrical Control Panel — CO₂ Extinguisher:** - Control panels operate at 415 V AC, making any conductive medium (water, foam) a **lethal electrocution hazard**. - CO₂ is **non-conductive** and leaves **no residue**, which is critical to prevent short-circuit damage. - **4.5 kg** is the standard portable size for localised electrical fires; sufficient for approximately **30 seconds of discharge**. - An additional **CO₂ flooding system** is recommended for the entire control room if the room is enclosed. 3. **Welding Station with Acetylene — ABC Dry Powder:** - Acetylene is a Class C flammable gas with an extremely wide flammability range (**2.5%–100% in air**) and very high flame temperature (**$\approx 3{,}100\ ^\circ\mathrm{C}$**). - The first action upon acetylene cylinder fire is to **shut off the cylinder valve** to stop gas supply. - ABC Dry Powder interrupts the **free-radical chain reaction** at the molecular level, instantly quenching the flame. - **6 kg** capacity provides approximately **20–25 seconds of discharge**, sufficient for cylinder-level fires. - Water is forbidden because acetylene can decompose explosively when heated, and water spray may disperse unburned gas. **Additional Recommendation:** A **Class D extinguisher** must be placed if magnesium or aluminium welding operations are also conducted in the workshop, with a **clearance distance of 15 metres** from Class A/B extinguishers to prevent cross-contamination of agents. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls on This Topic** > > 1. **Confusing Class C and Class E terminology** — Indian (IS) and American (NFPA) standards differ. Always specify both for full marks. > 2. **Forgetting the units** — When stating extinguishing medium properties (e.g., "CO₂ at 58 bar"), always include units. Numerical values without units fetch **zero marks** for that sub-part. > 3. **Mismechanising Class D fires** — Students often write "use CO₂ on metal fires." This is **WRONG**. CO₂ is ineffective and may react with burning metals. Only **Class D powder** is acceptable. > 4. **Skipping the "why not water" justification** — A question worth 7 marks typically expects 3–4 lines on *why an extinguisher is preferred over alternatives*, not just naming the extinguisher. > 5. **Neglecting the LOC concept** — Students often forget that CO₂ extinguishes by **oxygen displacement**, not cooling. Mentioning **LOC ≤ 12%** earns additional credit. > 6. **No mention of colour coding** — IS 15683 colour codes (red body + identification band) are often asked. Memorising these is mandatory. --- ### TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER > [!IMPORTANT] > **RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST — Classification of Fire & Fire Extinguishers** #### **Foundational Concepts** - **Fire Triangle** = **Fuel + Oxygen (21%) + Heat** (all three required). - Remove any one side → **Fire extinguishes** (Cooling / Smothering / Starvation / Chain-breaking). - **Limiting Oxygen Concentration (LOC)** for most hydrocarbons ≈ **12%**. #### **Six Classes of Fire (Memorise in Order)** 1. **Class A** → Solid combustibles (Wood, Paper, Cloth) → **Water, Foam, ABC Powder**. 2. **Class B** → Flammable Liquids (Petrol, Diesel, Paints) → **Foam, ABC Powder, CO₂**. 3. **Class C** → Flammable Gases (LPG, Methane, Acetylene) → **ABC Powder, CO₂ + Shut-off valve**. 4. **Class D** → Combustible Metals (Mg, Na, Al, Ti) → **Special Class D Powder**. 5. **Class E** → Electrical Fires (Live) → **CO₂ or Clean Agent (FM-200, Novec)**. 6. **Class F** → Cooking Oils/Fats → **Wet Chemical (Potassium Acetate)**. #### **Key Extinguisher Numbers (Memorise These Constants)** - Specific Heat of Water = **4.186 kJ/(kg·K)**. - Latent Heat of Vaporisation of Water = **2,260 kJ/kg**. - CO₂ Storage Pressure = **58 bar** at 21 °C. - CO₂ Discharge Temperature = **−78 °C** (dry ice). - NFPA 10 Minimum Discharge Time (Class B) = **9 seconds**. - Class A Extinguisher Travel Distance = **≤ 22.5 metres**. #### **Three Mechanisms of Extinguishment** - **Cooling** → Water (Class A). - **Smothering** → CO₂, Foam, Dry Powder. - **Chain-Breaking** → Dry Chemical Powder, Clean Agent. #### **BIS Colour Coding (IS 15683) — Body Always Signal Red** - **Water** → Red band. - **Foam** → Cream band. - **DCP** → Blue band. - **CO₂** → Black band. - **Wet Chemical** → Yellow band. - **Clean Agent** → Green band. #### **Critical "DO NOT USE" Rules (Frequently Asked)** - ❌ Water on Class B → Splash and fire spread. - ❌ Water on Class C → Ineffective, gas explosion risk. - ❌ Water on Class D → Reacts with metal, releases H₂. - ❌ Water on Class E → Electrocution hazard. - ❌ Water on Class F → Flash steam explosion. - ❌ CO₂ on Class A → Insufficient cooling. - ❌ CO₂ on Class D → Ineffective / may react. - ❌ CO₂ in confined occupied spaces → Asphyxiation. #### **KTU 2024 Frequently Tested Topics from This Module** 1. Fire Triangle and its application in extinguisher selection. 2. Comparison between Class B and Class C with extinguisher choices. 3. Detailed study of CO₂ extinguisher (construction + working + applications). 4. BIS Colour Coding as per IS 15683. 5. Calculation of heat absorption by water extinguisher. 6. Industrial case-study-based extinguisher selection (like the workshop question above).

topics.title: Fire, Explosion and Toxic Gas Release
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAUT415 - INDUSTRIAL SAFETY ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Fire, Explosion and Toxic Gas Release

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Defining Fire, Explosion and Toxic Gas Release

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Module 4 Focus):**
> **Fire** is a rapid, self-sustaining oxidation reaction that releases heat, light, smoke, and toxic gases. **Explosion** is a sudden, violent release of energy accompanied by a shock wave, gas expansion, and fragmentation. **Toxic Gas Release** is the unintended liberation of hazardous chemical vapours that, on dispersion into the atmosphere, can cause acute or chronic health damage to personnel, the public, and the environment.

From a process safety perspective (the KTU framework, following the **Crowl–Louvar** treatment), these three phenomena are the *dominant catastrophic hazards* in any chemical, petroleum, petrochemical, paint, fertilizer, or LNG plant. They are collectively responsible for over **80%** of all major industrial accident losses worldwide (e.g., Flixborough 1974, Bhopal 1984, BP Texas City 2005, Jaipur 2009).

#### The Three Classical Fire/Explosion Models
| Model | KTU Term | Mathematical Basis | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| Pool Fire | Continuous Combustion of Liquid Pool | Point-source / Solid-flame radiation | Tank farm fires |
| Fireball | BLEVE (Boiling Liquid Expanding Vapour Explosion) | Hemispherical point source | LPG truck accidents |
| Vapour Cloud Explosion (VCE) | Unconfined / Semi-confined cloud ignition | TNT Equivalence / TNO Multi-Energy | Petrochemical plants |
| Jet Fire | Pressurised release of flammable gas | API 521 / Shell correlation | Gas pipelines |

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Plain-English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy — "The Stove, The Balloon, and the Smelly Perfume Bottle"**
> 
> Imagine three dangerous things on your kitchen table:
> 
> 1. **A Stove (Fire)** — You light a burner. The gas is *controlled*; it burns steadily and you can see the flame. This is a **Fire** — a *sustained* combustion with a continuous fuel supply.
> 
> 2. **A Balloon (Explosion)** — You blow up a balloon with LPG and then pop it with a match. The gas is *uncontrolled*; it ignites almost instantaneously, producing a pressure wave and a loud bang. This is an **Explosion** — a *near-instantaneous* release of energy.
> 
> 3. **A Smelly Perfume Bottle (Toxic Gas Release)** — You accidentally knock over an ammonia bottle. The liquid vaporizes, an invisible cloud spreads through the room, and people start coughing and tearing up — even though there is **no fire and no flame**. This is a **Toxic Gas Release** — a *chemical* hazard, not a thermal one.
> 
> The trick in KTU exams is to recognize that all three hazards start from the *same root cause* — a **loss of containment (LOC)** from a pipe, tank, or vessel — but the *consequence* depends on whether the released material is **flammable**, **explosive**, or **toxic**.

---

### 1.3 The Fire Tetrahedron (Modern View)

The classical **Fire Triangle** (Fuel, Oxidizer, Ignition Source) was upgraded in the late 20th century to a **Fire Tetrahedron** to include the *Uninhibited Chain Reaction*. The KTU 2024 syllabus explicitly uses the tetrahedron model because it explains *extinction* mechanisms (e.g., halon, dry chemical powder).

> [!NOTE]
> **The Fire Tetrahedron — Four Sides, Remove One, Fire Dies**
> 1. **Fuel** (vapour, mist, dust, solid) — must be in *combustible concentration range*
> 2. **Oxidizer** (typically air, 21% O₂; some chemicals carry their own O₂, e.g., ammonium nitrate)
> 3. **Heat / Ignition Source** (spark, hot surface, static, adiabatic compression)
> 4. **Uninhibited Chain Reaction** (free radicals: H•, OH•, O• sustain flame propagation)
> 
> **Removal Strategy → Extinguishing Method**:
> - Remove Fuel → **Starvation** (close valves, drain liquid)
> - Remove Oxidizer → **Smothering** (CO₂, foam, inert gas)
> - Remove Heat → **Cooling** (water spray, deluge)
> - Remove Chain Reaction → **Inhibition** (dry chemical, halon)

---

### 1.4 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (Bolded)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Reference Constants for KTU Numericals**
> - **Heat of combustion of TNT, $H_{TNT}$ = 4686 kJ/kg** (literature value; some texts use 4520 kJ/kg)
> - **Stoichiometric air–fuel ratio for methane, AFR = 17.2 kg air/kg fuel**
> - **Lower Flammability Limit (LFL) of methane = 5% v/v in air**
> - **Upper Flammability Limit (UFL) of methane = 15% v/v in air**
> - **Auto-ignition temperature of gasoline ≈ 280 °C**, of hydrogen ≈ **500 °C**
> - **Standard atmospheric pressure, P₀ = 101.325 kPa**; Standard temperature, T₀ = **298.15 K**
> - **Universal gas constant, R = 8.314 J/(mol·K)**
> - **Probit reference value for 1% fatality (overpressure) = 0.97 kPa**; **50% fatality = 5.4 kPa** (TNO Green Book)

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Theoretical Foundation — Why Fire/Explosion/Toxic Release Matter

The *cause* of all three is identical: a **loss of containment (LOC)** of a hazardous material. The *consequence* (fire, explosion, or toxicity) is determined by the **physicochemical properties** of the released material and the **ignition timing**:

```
LOC → Release → Dispersion → If cloud forms:
                                       ├── Encounters ignition in OPEN air → Vapour Cloud Fire (flash fire)
                                       ├── Encounters ignition in CONFINED region → Vapour Cloud Explosion (VCE)
                                       ├── No ignition, but reaches population → Toxic exposure
                                       └── Reaches confined/ congested region with delay → Confined VCE
```

The *timing* between **release** and **ignition** is the critical discriminator. In KTU problems, this is captured by the dimensionless concept of **delay time** and **release rate**.

---

### 2.2 Flammability Characteristics — The Foundational Vocabulary

> [!NOTE]
> **Master the Following Definitions — They Appear Every Year in KTU Paper II**

| Term | Symbol | Definition (KTU-Standard) | Engineering Relevance |
|---|---|---|---|
| Flash Point | $T_{flash}$ | Lowest temperature at which a liquid gives off **enough vapour** to form an *ignitable mixture* near its surface (a brief flash, not sustained burning) | Determines storage & transport classification (Class A/B/C liquids) |
| Fire Point | $T_{fire}$ | Lowest temperature at which a liquid produces **sustained combustion** for ≥ 5 s | Used in fire-protection design of tanks |
| Auto-Ignition Temperature | $T_{AIT}$ | Minimum temperature at which a material **self-ignites** without an external spark | Defines hot-surface safety (e.g., hot pipelines, exhaust manifolds) |
| Lower Flammable Limit | LFL / LEL | Minimum fuel concentration in air that supports combustion | Below LFL → mixture is too lean to burn |
| Upper Flammable Limit | UFL / UEL | Maximum fuel concentration in air that supports combustion | Above UFL → mixture is too rich (oxidizer deficient) |
| Flammable Range | $\Delta$ | UFL − LFL | Wider range → more dangerous material |
| Limiting Oxygen Concentration | LOC | Minimum O₂ % to support combustion of a given fuel | Basis for inerting (purging with N₂, CO₂) |

---

### 2.3 Types of Explosions — KTU Classification

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Demands the Following Explosion Taxonomy**

1. **Physical Explosion** — Energy release from a *non-chemical* source (e.g., boiler BLEVE, pressure-vessel rupture, run-away steam).
2. **Chemical Explosion** — Energy release from a *rapid exothermic reaction*. Two sub-classes:
   - **Deflagration** — Sub-sonic flame propagation (speed < **340 m/s** in air). Pressure rise is moderate.
   - **Detonation** — Super-sonic flame propagation (speed > **1000 m/s**, often 1500–9000 m/s). Pressure rise is extreme (≈ **20× atmospheric**).
3. **Vapour Cloud Explosion (VCE)** — A deflagration-to-detonation transition in a *partially confined* or *congested* flammable cloud. Treated by **TNT-equivalence** or **TNO Multi-Energy Method**.
4. **Boiling Liquid Expanding Vapour Explosion (BLEVE)** — Catastrophic failure of a pressurised liquefied-gas vessel when engulfed in fire.
5. **Dust Explosion** — Combustion of suspended combustible dust (coal, grain, flour, sugar, aluminium). Requires a **Minimum Explosible Concentration (MEC)**.

---

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **All High-Yield Formulas for KTU Module 4**

| # | Formula Name | Equation | Units | When to Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | TNT Equivalence | $W_{TNT} = \dfrac{\eta \cdot M \cdot H_c}{H_{TNT}}$ | kg TNT | Convert a flammable mass into an equivalent mass of TNT for blast-damage analysis |
| 2 | Scaled Distance | $Z = \dfrac{R}{W_{TNT}^{1/3}}$ | m/kg$^{1/3}$ | Enter the TNT-curve overpressure chart |
| 3 | Peak Side-On Overpressure | $P_s = f(Z)$ — read from chart or empirical correlation: $P_s/P_0 = \dfrac{808 \cdot \left(1 + \left(\dfrac{Z}{4.5}\right)^2\right)}{\sqrt{1 + \left(\dfrac{Z}{0.048}\right)^2} \cdot \sqrt{1 + \left(\dfrac{Z}{0.32}\right)^2} \cdot \sqrt{1 + \left(\dfrac{Z}{1.35}\right)^2}}$ | Pa | Predict blast pressure at a stand-off distance R |
| 4 | Yield Factor / Explosion Efficiency | $\eta$ = 0.01 to 0.10 (typical VCE), 0.05 (Crowl & Louvar default) | dimensionless | Empirical; represents fraction of fuel energy converted to blast |
| 5 | Heat of Combustion (chemical energy) | $H_c$ in kJ/kg | kJ/kg | Mass of fuel released × $H_c$ = total chemical energy |
| 6 | Radiation from Pool Fire (Point Source) | $q = \dfrac{Q_r}{4 \pi r^2}$ | kW/m² | Heat flux at a receptor; thermal-dose calculation |
| 7 | Total Heat Release Rate (Pool Fire) | $Q_r = \chi \cdot m' \cdot H_c$ | kW | $\chi$ ≈ 0.15–0.35 (radiative fraction) |
| 8 | Probit Equation (General Form) | $Y = a + b \ln(V)$ | dimensionless | $V$ = dose (overpressure, thermal dose, toxic concentration×time) |
| 9 | Toxic Load (Haber's Law) | $TL = C^n \cdot t$ | ppm$^n$·min | $n$ = 1 for most gases, $n$ = 2–3 for some |
| 10 | Toxic Dose for a Specific Endpoint | $\dfrac{C^n \cdot t}{\text{ERPG or AEGL value}} = 1$ | ppm$^n$·min | Compare to emergency exposure limits |
| 11 | Vapour Mass from Liquid Spill | $m = V_L \cdot \rho_L \cdot f_v$ | kg | $f_v$ = fraction vaporised (often $\approx 0.2$ for instantaneous release) |
| 12 | Puff Model (Concentration) | $\langle C \rangle = \dfrac{M}{(2\pi)^{3/2} \cdot \sigma_x \sigma_y \sigma_z} \cdot \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{1}{2}\!\left[\dfrac{x^2}{\sigma_x^2} + \dfrac{y^2}{\sigma_y^2} + \dfrac{z^2}{\sigma_z^2}\right]\right)$ | kg/m³ | Instantaneous release, instantaneous dispersion |
| 13 | Plume Model (Continuous) | $\langle C \rangle = \dfrac{Q'}{2\pi \sigma_y \sigma_z u} \cdot \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{1}{2}\!\left[\dfrac{y^2}{\sigma_y^2} + \dfrac{z^2}{\sigma_z^2}\right]\right)$ | kg/m³ | Continuous release, steady wind |
| 14 | Fatality via Probit | $\% \text{ Fatality} = 50 \cdot \left[1 + \text{erf}\!\left(\dfrac{Y - 5}{\sqrt{2}}\right)\right]$ | % | Convert probit Y into % of population killed |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Caveat on Equation 3**: KTU exams often provide a *simplified* overpressure chart or ask only the *scaled-distance* value. Memorize the procedure: (a) compute $W_{TNT}$, (b) compute $Z$ for the stand-off, (c) read $P_s$ from chart, (d) check fatality/structural-damage table.

---

### 2.5 Engineering Utility — Why These Equations Are Used

- **$W_{TNT}$ and $Z$** are the industry-standard *Damage Estimation Procedure* for **OSHA Process Safety Management (PSM)** and **EPA Risk Management Plan (RMP)** audits. They allow layout planning of occupied buildings relative to hazardous units.
- **Probit equations** allow quantitative risk assessment (QRA) — turning a *physical dose* (kPa, ppm·min, kW/m²·s) into a *fatality probability* (% killed).
- **Puff / Plume models** (Gaussian) are the basis of **AERMOD**, **ISC3**, **SLAB**, **DEGADIS** dispersion software used by regulatory bodies worldwide.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This section is exhaustive. Every algebraic transition is shown explicitly. No steps are skipped. Use this as a model for your KTU exam solutions.**

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — The TNT Equivalence Method (Full)

**The Big Idea**: A vapour cloud of a flammable hydrocarbon releases a large amount of *chemical energy* when it ignites. We want to convert this into a *blast-damage* statement, but explosion engineers have decades of experience expressing damage in terms of *equivalent mass of TNT*. We do that by forming the dimensionless ratio of the fuel's energy to TNT's energy.

**Step 1 — Write the energy released by the flammable vapour cloud.**

Let $M$ = mass of fuel vapour in the cloud (kg), $H_c$ = heat of combustion of the fuel (kJ/kg). The total *chemical* energy that *would* be released on complete combustion is:

$$E_{fuel} = M \cdot H_c$$

**Step 2 — Define the explosion efficiency $\eta$.**

In a *real* vapour-cloud deflagration, not 100% of the chemical energy is converted into a *blast wave*. Some goes into heating the air, some into fireball radiation, some is unburned. We define the empirical *yield factor* $\eta$ as:

$$E_{blast} = \eta \cdot M \cdot H_c$$

Typical values (per Crowl & Louvar):
- Unconfined vapour cloud (open air): $\eta$ ≈ **0.01 – 0.02**
- Partially confined VCE: $\eta$ ≈ **0.05 – 0.10**
- Confined explosion: $\eta$ → approaches 1.0

**Step 3 — Equate the blast energy to an equivalent mass of TNT.**

The energy of 1 kg of TNT is $H_{TNT}$. If our blast releases $E_{blast}$, the *equivalent* mass of TNT that would release the same energy is:

$$W_{TNT} \cdot H_{TNT} = \eta \cdot M \cdot H_c$$

Solving for $W_{TNT}$:

$$W_{TNT} = \dfrac{\eta \cdot M \cdot H_c}{H_{TNT}}$$

This is Equation (1) of the Formula Sheet.

**Step 4 — Introduce the scaled distance.**

For an actual explosion of mass $W_{TNT}$ (kg), we want to know the overpressure at a stand-off distance $R$ (m). The fundamental empirical observation is that **similar overpressures are produced at distances that scale with the cube-root of the energy**. This is the **Hopkinson-Cranz Cube-Root Law**:

$$Z = \dfrac{R}{W_{TNT}^{1/3}}$$

**Step 5 — Read overpressure from the standard curve.**

The function $P_s(Z)$ has been tabulated for centuries (Kingery, Bulmash, TNO). The simplest closed-form approximation (the *Baker-Westine* form, often used in textbooks) is:

$$\dfrac{P_s}{P_0} = \dfrac{808 \left[1 + \left(\dfrac{Z}{4.5}\right)^2\right]}{\sqrt{\left[1 + \left(\dfrac{Z}{0.048}\right)^2\right] \cdot \left[1 + \left(\dfrac{Z}{0.32}\right)^2\right] \cdot \left[1 + \left(\dfrac{Z}{1.35}\right)^2\right]}}$$

**Step 6 — Apply the Probit equation for fatality (TNO Green Book):**

$$Y = -77.1 + 6.91 \ln(P_s) \quad \text{(P}_s \text{ in kPa)}$$

$$\% \text{ Fatality} = 50 \cdot \left[1 + \text{erf}\!\left(\dfrac{Y - 5}{\sqrt{2}}\right)\right]$$

This **6-step chain** is the canonical KTU-14-marker for VCE analysis. You will be awarded full marks **only** if you show all 6 steps.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Gaussian Puff Model (Instantaneous Release)

**Physical Setup**: At time $t = 0$, a mass $M$ (kg) of a toxic or flammable gas is released *instantaneously* from a point $(0, 0, H_s)$ where $H_s$ is the stack/release height. Wind blows in the +x direction with mean speed $u$ (m/s). The puff is advected and disperses.

**Step 1 — Define dispersion coefficients.**

The puff grows by atmospheric turbulence. In the three Cartesian directions:

$$\sigma_x = f_x(t), \quad \sigma_y = f_y(t), \quad \sigma_z = f_z(t)$$

For neutral (D-class) stability, a common textbook form is:

$$\sigma_y = \sigma_z = a \cdot t^b$$

with $a$ and $b$ being Pasquill-Gifford coefficients (e.g., $a = 0.08$, $b = 0.94$ for D stability). These are *functions of downwind distance*, often rewritten as $\sigma_y(x)$ and $\sigma_z(x)$ in look-up tables.

**Step 2 — Write the conservation-of-mass constraint.**

The total mass in the puff must remain $M$ (no chemical reaction assumed):

$$\int_{-\infty}^{\infty}\int_{-\infty}^{\infty}\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \langle C \rangle \, dx\,dy\,dz = M$$

**Step 3 — Propose a Gaussian form and check it satisfies (2).**

Try the solution:

$$\langle C \rangle = \dfrac{M}{(2\pi)^{3/2} \cdot \sigma_x \sigma_y \sigma_z} \cdot \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{(x - u t)^2}{2\sigma_x^2}\right) \cdot \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{y^2}{2\sigma_y^2}\right) \cdot \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{(z - H_s)^2}{2\sigma_z^2}\right)$$

(For a ground-level release, set $H_s = 0$ and add a *virtual* mirror source at $-H_s$ so the ground is not penetrated by the cloud.)

**Step 4 — Verify conservation.**

Substitute into (2) and integrate. Each Gaussian integrates to $\sqrt{2\pi}\,\sigma$. The product of three Gaussians integrates to $(2\pi)^{3/2} \sigma_x \sigma_y \sigma_z$. The prefactor cancels exactly, leaving $M$. ✓

**Step 5 — Steady-state plume limit.**

For a *continuous* release of mass rate $Q'$ (kg/s), the puff is constantly replenished. The integrated form is:

$$\langle C \rangle = \dfrac{Q'}{2\pi u \sigma_y \sigma_z} \cdot \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{y^2}{2\sigma_y^2}\right) \cdot \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{z^2}{2\sigma_z^2}\right)$$

(For a ground-level source, the $z$-Gaussian is replaced by $\exp\!\left(-\dfrac{z^2}{2\sigma_z^2}\right) + \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{(z+H_s)^2}{2\sigma_z^2}\right)$ to enforce the no-flux boundary at the ground.)

**Step 6 — Use concentration in dose calculations.**

For toxic exposure, the *toxic load* at receptor $(x, y, z)$ over duration $T$ is:

$$TL(x,y,z) = \int_0^T C(x,y,z,t)^n \, dt \approx C^n \cdot T \quad (n=1 \text{ for most toxic gases})$$

Then compare $TL$ to the **AEGL-3**, **ERPG-3**, or **IDLH** value to determine consequences.

---

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU 14-Mark Type)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem (Model KTU University Exam Style):**
> A 5000 kg cloud of propane (LFL = 2.1%, UFL = 9.5%, $H_c$ = 46 MJ/kg) is released from a storage sphere at a refinery. The cloud drifts over a congested pipe-rack region and ignites. An occupied control room is located at a stand-off of R = 300 m. Using the TNT-equivalence method with $\eta$ = 0.05, find (a) the equivalent mass of TNT, (b) the scaled distance, and (c) the peak side-on overpressure at the control room. (Use $H_{TNT}$ = 4686 kJ/kg.)

**Solution:**

**Part (a):** Equivalent TNT mass.

$$W_{TNT} = \dfrac{\eta \cdot M \cdot H_c}{H_{TNT}}$$

Substituting the values (with $H_c$ in kJ/kg to match units):

$$W_{TNT} = \dfrac{0.05 \times 5000 \times 46000}{4686} = \dfrac{1.15 \times 10^7}{4686} = 2454.5 \text{ kg TNT}$$

**Part (b):** Scaled distance.

$$Z = \dfrac{R}{W_{TNT}^{1/3}} = \dfrac{300}{(2454.5)^{1/3}} = \dfrac{300}{13.49} = 22.24 \text{ m/kg}^{1/3}$$

**Part (c):** Peak side-on overpressure. We use the Baker-Westine formula:

$$\dfrac{P_s}{P_0} = \dfrac{808 \left[1 + (22.24/4.5)^2\right]}{\sqrt{\left[1 + (22.24/0.048)^2\right]\left[1 + (22.24/0.32)^2\right]\left[1 + (22.24/1.35)^2\right]}}$$

Compute the bracketed terms one by one:

- $(22.24/4.5)^2 = (4.943)^2 = 24.43$
- $(22.24/0.048)^2 = (463.4)^2 = 2.147 \times 10^5$
- $(22.24/0.32)^2 = (69.5)^2 = 4830$
- $(22.24/1.35)^2 = (16.47)^2 = 271.3$

Numerator: $808 \times (1 + 24.43) = 808 \times 25.43 = 20,547$

Denominator: $\sqrt{(1 + 2.147 \times 10^5) \times (1 + 4830) \times (1 + 271.3)}$
$= \sqrt{(2.147 \times 10^5) \times 4831 \times 272.3}$
$= \sqrt{2.147 \times 10^5 \times 1.315 \times 10^6}$
$= \sqrt{2.823 \times 10^{11}} = 5.314 \times 10^5$

Therefore:

$$\dfrac{P_s}{P_0} = \dfrac{20{,}547}{531{,}400} = 0.03867$$

$$P_s = 0.03867 \times 101.325 = 3.92 \text{ kPa}$$

**Interpretation**: An overpressure of 3.92 kPa is the threshold for *light structural damage* (windows shatter, minor injuries). The control-room building should be designed to withstand at least 7 kPa (unreinforced masonry fails at ~7 kPa; reinforced concrete fails at ~21 kPa). Hence, the design is **borderline acceptable** — the recommendation is to either increase stand-off to R ≥ 400 m or harden the control room.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Damage Levels vs Overpressure

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Damage Table (Memorize for KTU)**

| Peak Overpressure $P_s$ (kPa) | Structural Damage | Human Injury |
|---|---|---|
| 0.7 | Slight | — |
| 2.1 | Light (window shatter) | Slight injury |
| 3.5 – 7 | Moderate (cracked walls) | Eardrum rupture (1%) |
| 7 – 14 | Major (unreinforced wall collapse) | Eardrum rupture (10%), serious injury |
| 14 – 21 | Heavy (reinforced wall damage) | Lung injury (1%), significant fatalities |
| 35 – 55 | Severe (total building failure) | 50% fatality, severe lung damage |
| > 100 | Total destruction | Near 100% fatality |

---

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Toxicological Endpoints

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Three Tiered Limit Values Used in Industry**

| Tier | Acronym | Meaning | Use in KTU |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **AEGL-1 / ERPG-1** | Threshold below which the general population experiences *no* effects | Slight, transient effects above this |
| 2 | **AEGL-2 / ERPG-2** | Below which the population can *escape* without irreversible injury | Default for shelter-in-place planning |
| 3 | **AEGL-3 / ERPG-3** | Below which the population experiences *no* life-threatening effects | Default for fatality QRA |

For ammonia, $ERPG\text{-}2 = 200 \text{ ppm}$ (30 min). For chlorine, $ERPG\text{-}2 = 3 \text{ ppm}$.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!NOTE]
> **Per the KTU-Premier-Engine protocol, all Mermaid diagrams use alphanumeric node IDs and clean uppercase labels. No markdown formatting tags inside node labels.**

### 4.1 Causal Chain — From Loss of Containment to Catastrophe

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[LOSS OF CONTAINMENT] --> B0[RELEASE OF HAZARDOUS MATERIAL]
    B0 --> C0[DISPERSION INTO ATMOSPHERE]
    C0 --> D1[CLOUD DRIFTS WITHOUT IGNITION]
    C0 --> D2[CLOUD IGNITES]
    D1 --> E1[TOXIC EXPOSURE OF POPULATION]
    D2 --> E2[VAPOUR CLOUD FIRE]
    D2 --> E3[VAPOUR CLOUD EXPLOSION]
    D2 --> E4[FLASH FIRE]
    D2 --> E5[CONFINED CLOUD VCE]
    E1 --> F1[ACUTE FATALITY]
    E1 --> F2[CHRONIC HEALTH DAMAGE]
    E2 --> F3[RADIATION INJURY]
    E3 --> F4[BLAST OVERPRESSURE INJURY]
    E4 --> F5[THERMAL BURN]
    E5 --> F6[MASSIVE STRUCTURAL DAMAGE]
    F1 --> G0[CONSEQUENCE MITIGATION]
    F2 --> G0
    F3 --> G0
    F4 --> G0
    F5 --> G0
    F6 --> G0
```

---

### 4.2 Fire Tetrahedron — Extinction Strategy

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    F1[FUEL] --> T0[FIRE TETRAHEDRON]
    F2[OXIDIZER] --> T0
    F3[IGNITION HEAT] --> T0
    F4[CHAIN REACTION] --> T0
    F1 --> M1[STARVATION METHOD]
    F2 --> M2[SMOTHERING METHOD]
    F3 --> M3[COOLING METHOD]
    F4 --> M4[INHIBITION METHOD]
    M1 --> OUT[FIRE EXTINGUISHED]
    M2 --> OUT
    M3 --> OUT
    M4 --> OUT
```

---

### 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Quantitative Risk Assessment (QRA)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P0[HAZARD IDENTIFICATION] --> P1[RELEASE SCENARIO SELECTION]
    P1 --> P2[RELEASE RATE ESTIMATION]
    P2 --> P3[DISPERSION MODELLING]
    P3 --> P4[FIRE MODEL]
    P3 --> P5[EXPLOSION MODEL]
    P3 --> P6[TOXIC MODEL]
    P4 --> P7[DAMAGE PROBIT]
    P5 --> P7
    P6 --> P7
    P7 --> P8[INDIVIDUAL RISK CONTOURS]
    P7 --> P9[SOCIETAL RISK F N CURVES]
    P8 --> P10[DECISION ON ACCEPTABILITY]
    P9 --> P10
```

---

### 4.4 Flammability Range Diagram (Generic Hydrocarbon)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[FUEL CONCENTRATION ZERO] --> A2[LFL LOWER FLAMMABLE LIMIT]
    A2 --> A3[FLAMMABLE RANGE LFL TO UFL]
    A3 --> A4[UFL UPPER FLAMMABLE LIMIT]
    A4 --> A5[FUEL CONCENTRATION 100 PERCENT]
    A2 -.-> A6[TOO LEAN NO IGNITION]
    A3 -.-> A7[IGNITABLE]
    A4 -.-> A8[TOO RICH NO IGNITION]
```

---

### 4.5 Sequence of a BLEVE (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S0[LIQUEFIED GAS UNDER PRESSURE] --> S1[EXTERNAL FIRE ENGULFS VESSEL]
    S1 --> S2[SHELL METAL HEATS ABOVE UPPER TRANSITION TEMPERATURE]
    S2 --> S3[STRENGTH DROPS DRAMATICALLY]
    S3 --> S4[VESSEL RUPTURES]
    S4 --> S5[LIQUID FLASHES INSTANTANEOUSLY TO VAPOUR]
    S5 --> S6[FIREBALL FORMS]
    S6 --> S7[THERMAL RADIATION INJURY]
    S6 --> S8[SHOCK WAVE OVERPRESSURE]
    S7 --> S9[CONSEQUENCE FATALITY]
    S8 --> S9
```
