<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 📘 PART A — 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions (Module 4)

#### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024] Define: (i) LFL, (ii) Flash Point, (iii) Fire Point. Give one example value for each.**

**Model Answer:**

> (i) **Lower Flammable Limit (LFL):** The minimum concentration of a flammable vapour in air below which propagation of a flame does not occur on contact with a source of ignition. *Example: Methane, LFL = 5% v/v.*
> 
> (ii) **Flash Point:** The lowest temperature at which a liquid gives off sufficient vapour to form a momentarily ignitable mixture with air near its surface (a brief flash, not sustained combustion). *Example: Gasoline, $T_{flash} \approx -43 \text{ °C}$.*
> 
> (iii) **Fire Point:** The lowest temperature at which a liquid produces sustained combustion for at least 5 seconds. *Example: Kerosene, $T_{fire} \approx 65 \text{ °C}$.*
> 
> The fire point is always *higher* than the flash point (typically by 5–15 °C), because the liquid must vaporise faster than the flash point rate to sustain combustion.

---

#### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] Distinguish between deflagration and detonation. Why is this distinction important for blast-damage estimation?**

**Model Answer:**

> | Feature | Deflagration | Detonation |
> |---|---|---|
> | Flame speed | Sub-sonic, < 340 m/s | Super-sonic, 1500–9000 m/s |
> | Pressure ratio | ≈ 7–10:1 (unconfined), up to 20:1 (confined) | ≈ 20:1 or higher |
> | Energy transmission | Mostly by heat conduction & convection | Shock wave with chemical reaction in the wave-front |
> | Damage pattern | Quasi-static pressure, large displacement | Localised, high-impulse shattering |
> 
> **Why it matters in blast-damage estimation:** The **TNT-equivalence** method assumes a *detonation-type* pressure profile. If the actual event is a slow deflagration, applying the TNT method overestimates the impulse and underestimates the duration; one should use a **multi-energy** (TNO ME) model with a *lower flame speed* and *lower energy yield* $\eta$ to capture the true overpressure-time profile.

---

### 📗 PART B — 14-Mark ESE Module Internal Choice

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Always state the value of $H_{TNT}$ you are using** (4686 kJ/kg or 4520 kJ/kg). If you change it between sub-parts, you lose 1 mark for "internal inconsistency."
> - In $W_{TNT}$ numericals, **do not confuse units of $H_c$** (kJ/kg vs MJ/kg). A factor-of-1000 error is the most common KTU pitfall.
> - In Probit problems, **always mention** that you are using the "Crowl & Louvar Table 2-8" correlation or the "TNO Green Book" — credit is given for *naming* the source.
> - In dispersion problems, **state the Pasquill stability class** (A, B, C, D, E, or F) you are assuming. Examiners deduct 2 marks for "unspecified stability."
> - Always include the **boundary condition** (ground reflection, virtual source) when sketching Gaussian dispersion.

---

#### **QUESTION A (14 Marks) — Vapour Cloud Explosion via TNT Equivalence**

**[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 4]**

> (a) **Derive** the TNT-equivalence formula $W_{TNT} = \dfrac{\eta \cdot M \cdot H_c}{H_{TNT}}$ starting from the energy balance of a vapour cloud. **\[7 marks\]**
> 
> (b) A 10,000 kg cloud of cyclohexane ($H_c$ = 43.4 MJ/kg) drifts into a congested region and undergoes a VCE with $\eta = 0.08$. The site control room is at R = 250 m. Compute (i) $W_{TNT}$, (ii) the scaled distance $Z$, (iii) the peak side-on overpressure using the Baker-Westine correlation, and (iv) the % fatality using the TNO probit for overpressure ($Y = -77.1 + 6.91 \ln P_s$ with $P_s$ in kPa). Use $H_{TNT}$ = 4686 kJ/kg. **\[7 marks\]**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation (7 marks):**

Step 1: Total chemical energy of the vapour cloud = $E_{fuel} = M \cdot H_c$ — **1 mark**
Step 2: Real-world conversion efficiency factor $\eta$ = $E_{blast}/E_{fuel}$, so $E_{blast} = \eta \cdot M \cdot H_c$ — **2 marks**
Step 3: TNT equivalence: 1 kg of TNT releases $H_{TNT}$ kJ; to release $E_{blast}$ we need $W_{TNT}$ such that $W_{TNT} \cdot H_{TNT} = \eta \cdot M \cdot H_c$ — **2 marks**
Step 4: Rearranging gives $W_{TNT} = \dfrac{\eta M H_c}{H_{TNT}}$ — **1 mark**
Step 5: Hopkinson-Cranz cube-root law, $Z = R/W_{TNT}^{1/3}$ — **1 mark** (for "scaling law" credit).

**(b) Numerical (7 marks):**

**(i) Equivalent TNT mass — 2 marks**

$$W_{TNT} = \dfrac{0.08 \times 10{,}000 \times 43{,}400}{4686} = \dfrac{3.472 \times 10^7}{4686} = 7409 \text{ kg TNT}$$

**(ii) Scaled distance — 1 mark**

$$Z = \dfrac{250}{(7409)^{1/3}} = \dfrac{250}{19.49} = 12.83 \text{ m/kg}^{1/3}$$

**(iii) Peak overpressure — 3 marks**

Compute ratios:
- $(Z/4.5)^2 = (12.83/4.5)^2 = (2.851)^2 = 8.128$
- $(Z/0.048)^2 = (267.3)^2 = 7.145 \times 10^4$
- $(Z/0.32)^2 = (40.09)^2 = 1607$
- $(Z/1.35)^2 = (9.504)^2 = 90.32$

Numerator: $808 \times (1 + 8.128) = 808 \times 9.128 = 7375$
Denominator: $\sqrt{(1 + 7.145 \times 10^4) \times (1 + 1607) \times (1 + 90.32)}$
$= \sqrt{7.145 \times 10^4 \times 1608 \times 91.32}$
$= \sqrt{1.049 \times 10^{11}} = 3.239 \times 10^5$

$$\dfrac{P_s}{P_0} = \dfrac{7375}{3.239 \times 10^5} = 0.02277$$

$$P_s = 0.02277 \times 101.325 = 2.31 \text{ kPa}$$

**(iv) % Fatality — 1 mark**

$$Y = -77.1 + 6.91 \ln(2.31) = -77.1 + 6.91 \times 0.837 = -77.1 + 5.78 = -71.32$$

$$\% \text{ Fatality} = 50 \times \left[1 + \text{erf}\!\left(\dfrac{-71.32 - 5}{\sqrt{2}}\right)\right] = 50 \times \left[1 + \text{erf}(-53.92)\right] \approx 0\%$$

(The erf of a large negative argument is ≈ -1, so fatality is ≈ 0%. This is correct: 2.31 kPa is below the 3.5 kPa eardrum-rupture threshold.)

**Conclusion**: At R = 250 m, the overpressure produces no fatalities but minor structural damage. To eliminate *all* structural damage ($P_s$ < 0.7 kPa), R should be > 800 m.

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Toxic Gas Release and Gaussian Plume**

**[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 4]**

> (a) **Explain** the Gaussian Puff and Plume models. State the key assumptions. **\[7 marks\]**
> 
> (b) A refinery releases chlorine at a steady rate $Q'$ = 0.5 kg/s from a stack of height $H_s$ = 30 m. Wind speed $u$ = 4 m/s, Pasquill class D. At a downwind distance x = 1000 m, the dispersion coefficients are $\sigma_y$ = 38 m and $\sigma_z$ = 21 m. A populated village is located at a ground-level crosswind distance y = 50 m. Estimate (i) the ground-level centreline concentration, (ii) the ground-level concentration at y = 50 m, and (iii) the maximum concentration over the downwind reach. **\[7 marks\]**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Theory (7 marks):**

- **Puff Model (Instantaneous Release):** Describes a *single pulse* of gas released at $t = 0$, advected by wind and growing in three dimensions due to turbulence. Concentration field is a 3-D Gaussian. **2 marks** for stating the form:
$$\langle C \rangle = \dfrac{M}{(2\pi)^{3/2} \sigma_x \sigma_y \sigma_z} \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{(x-ut)^2}{2\sigma_x^2} - \dfrac{y^2}{2\sigma_y^2} - \dfrac{(z-H_s)^2}{2\sigma_z^2}\right)$$
- **Plume Model (Continuous Release):** Describes a *steady* release; plume is advected but its concentration field is *statistically stationary* in a moving frame. **2 marks** for stating the form:
$$\langle C \rangle = \dfrac{Q'}{2\pi u \sigma_y \sigma_z} \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{y^2}{2\sigma_y^2}\right) \left[\exp\!\left(-\dfrac{(z-H_s)^2}{2\sigma_z^2}\right) + \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{(z+H_s)^2}{2\sigma_z^2}\right)\right]$$
- **Key Assumptions (3 marks):** (1) Steady, homogeneous wind field; (2) Fickian diffusion (turbulent diffusion parameterised as eddy diffusivity); (3) No chemical reaction in the cloud; (4) Ground is a reflecting boundary; (5) Constant emission rate.

**(b) Numerical (7 marks):**

**Given:** $Q' = 0.5$ kg/s, $H_s = 30$ m, $u = 4$ m/s, $\sigma_y = 38$ m, $\sigma_z = 21$ m, $x = 1000$ m, $y = 50$ m, $z = 0$ (ground-level).

**(i) Ground-level centreline concentration (y = 0, z = 0) — 2 marks**

The two exponential terms in $z$ (at z = 0) become:
$\exp(0) + \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{H_s^2}{2\sigma_z^2}\right) = 1 + \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{900}{2 \times 441}\right) = 1 + \exp(-1.020) = 1 + 0.360 = 1.360$

$$C(1000, 0, 0) = \dfrac{0.5}{2\pi \times 4 \times 38 \times 21} \times 1 \times 1.360$$

Denominator: $2\pi \times 4 \times 38 \times 21 = 2\pi \times 3192 = 20{,}058$

$$C = \dfrac{0.5 \times 1.360}{20{,}058} = \dfrac{0.680}{20{,}058} = 3.39 \times 10^{-5} \text{ kg/m}^3 = 0.0339 \text{ mg/m}^3$$

In ppm for Cl₂ (M = 70.9 g/mol, 24.45 L/mol at 25°C, 1 atm): 

$$0.0339 \text{ mg/m}^3 = \dfrac{0.0339}{70.9/24.45} = \dfrac{0.0339}{2.899} = 0.0117 \text{ ppm}$$

**(ii) Ground-level concentration at y = 50 m — 2 marks**

The crosswind attenuation: $\exp\!\left(-\dfrac{y^2}{2\sigma_y^2}\right) = \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{2500}{2 \times 1444}\right) = \exp(-0.866) = 0.421$

$$C(1000, 50, 0) = 0.0117 \times 0.421 = 0.00493 \text{ ppm}$$

**(iii) Maximum concentration over the downwind reach — 3 marks**

Maximum ground-level concentration occurs at the downwind distance where the term $\sigma_y \sigma_z$ first dominates the dispersion. Empirical formula (Briggs):

$$x_{max} = \dfrac{u \cdot H_s^2}{2 \sigma_z^2 / x}$$

A simpler rule: for elevated releases, the maximum ground-level concentration occurs at approximately $x \approx 10 H_s$ to $20 H_s$ for neutral stability. For $H_s = 30$ m, $x_{max} \approx 300$–$600$ m.

At $x = x_{max}$, $\sigma_y$ and $\sigma_z$ are read from Pasquill-Gifford tables; let's say $\sigma_y \approx 22$ m, $\sigma_z \approx 18$ m:

$C_{max} \approx \dfrac{0.5 \times 1.5}{2\pi \times 4 \times 22 \times 18} = \dfrac{0.75}{9951} = 7.5 \times 10^{-5}$ kg/m³ = **0.075 mg/m³** = **0.026 ppm**

(The exact value depends on the lookup; the methodology is what earns marks.)

**Conclusion**: The maximum ground-level concentration of 0.026 ppm is well below the chlorine ERPG-2 of 3 ppm. The village is safe, but the *intermediate* calculation at 1000 m shows how the cloud thins with distance.

---

### 🔥 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Pin This List to Your Wall)**

- ✅ **Fire Tetrahedron**: Fuel + Oxidizer + Heat + Chain Reaction — *remove one, fire dies.*
- ✅ **Fire Triangle → Tetrahedron**: Modern view includes the *uninhibited chain reaction*; this explains *dry chemical* and *halon* extinguishing.
- ✅ **Flash Point < Fire Point < Auto-Ignition Temperature** (always in that order for liquids).
- ✅ **LFL < UFL**: Below LFL → too lean; Above UFL → too rich; between LFL & UFL → ignitable.
- ✅ **Deflagration** = sub-sonic flame; **Detonation** = super-sonic flame with shock-wave.
- ✅ **BLEVE** = Boiling Liquid Expanding Vapour Explosion; caused by external fire heating a pressurised liquefied-gas vessel.
- ✅ **VCE** = Vapour Cloud Explosion; requires a *congested* or *partially confined* region to amplify the blast.
- ✅ **TNT Equivalence** is a *simplified* model — for a more accurate treatment use the **TNO Multi-Energy Method**.
- ✅ **$H_{TNT} = 4686$ kJ/kg** (most KTU papers); the alternative 4520 kJ/kg is acceptable but must be stated.
- ✅ **Cube-root law** $Z = R/W^{1/3}$ is the foundation of every blast-damage chart.
- ✅ **Probit equations** convert physical dose (kPa, kW/m², ppm·min) into % fatality.
- ✅ **Gaussian Puff** is for *instantaneous* releases; **Gaussian Plume** is for *continuous* releases.
- ✅ **Pasquill Stability Classes A (very unstable) → F (very stable)** determine $\sigma_y$ and $\sigma_z$.
- ✅ **Toxic Load** $TL = C^n \cdot t$; for most gases $n = 1$.
- ✅ **Damage thresholds** (memorize): 3.5 kPa → eardrum rupture, 7 kPa → wall failure, 14 kPa → lung injury, 35 kPa → 50% fatality, 100 kPa → total destruction.
- ✅ **In the KTU exam**, always (1) state assumptions, (2) state the empirical correlation being used, (3) show units, (4) give a final numerical answer with units, and (5) interpret the result in *engineering* terms.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Study Tip**: For Module 4, draw the Fire Tetrahedron and the LOC → Consequence flowchart from memory. If you can sketch them cold, you have 70% of the marks already. The numerical problems are *templated* — once you know the 6-step chain in Section 3.1, you can solve any KTU question on VCE.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Structure of Hazard Identification and Risk Assessment
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAUT415 - INDUSTRIAL SAFETY ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Structure of Hazard Identification and Risk Assessment

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Hazard Identification and Risk Assessment (HIRA)** is a systematic, structured, and iterative engineering methodology used to identify potential hazards in a workplace, process, or system, and to evaluate the associated risks by analyzing the **consequences** (severity) and **likelihood** (probability) of harm occurring. Under the KTU 2024 Scheme and aligned with international standards such as **ISO 31000:2018** and **IEC 31010:2019**, HIRA forms the foundation of the **Plan-Do-Check-Act (PDCA)** safety management cycle.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> *HIRA is the overall process of hazard identification followed by risk evaluation, which combines the severity of harm and the probability of its occurrence to determine the risk level and decide whether the risk is tolerable or acceptable.*

Mathematically, the fundamental relationship is expressed as:

$$R = f(S \times L)$$

Where:
- **R** = Risk Level (dimensionless score)
- **S** = Severity of Consequence
- **L** = Likelihood (Probability) of Occurrence

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: The Driving Decision Matrix**
> 
> Imagine you are driving a car approaching an intersection with a malfunctioning traffic signal. Before crossing, your brain automatically performs HIRA:
> - **Hazard Identification:** "A red light is broken — a vehicle may come from the side."
> - **Severity (S):** "If a car hits mine, the injury could be severe (hospitalization)." → Rating: **High (4)**
> - **Likelihood (L):** "It's 2 AM, traffic is sparse." → Rating: **Low (1)**
> - **Risk (R):** Moderate. You slow down, look both ways, and proceed cautiously.
> 
> This mental Risk = Severity × Likelihood calculation is exactly what HIRA formalizes for industrial plants handling chemicals, machinery, and high-energy systems.

In an industrial plant (refinery, chemical factory, manufacturing unit), HIRA is performed *before* commissioning and *periodically during operation* to ensure that every credible hazard — fire, explosion, toxic release, mechanical failure, electrical shock — has been catalogued, evaluated, and reduced to **As Low As Reasonably Practicable (ALARP)** levels.

---

## 1.3 Core Terminology — KTU Board-Critical Vocabulary

| Term | Formal Definition | Example |
|------|------------------|---------|
| **Hazard** | An intrinsic physical or chemical property with the potential to cause harm | Stored LPG, High voltage busbar |
| **Risk** | The combination of the probability of an event and the severity of its consequences | Explosion during maintenance |
| **Hazard Identification (HAZID)** | The process of recognizing which hazards exist | HAZOP, JSA, What-If Analysis |
| **Risk Assessment** | The overall process of risk analysis and risk evaluation | Using a Risk Matrix |
| **Risk Acceptance Criteria** | The predefined threshold of tolerable risk | Risk Score ≤ 4 is acceptable |
| **ALARP** | As Low As Reasonably Practicable | Cost-benefit justified mitigation |
| **Top Event** | The central undesired event in a fault tree | Vessel rupture |
| **Residual Risk** | Risk remaining after control measures | PPE-protected worker still faces low risk |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Distinction:** A **Hazard** is *what can cause harm*. A **Risk** is *how likely it is to cause harm and how serious the harm would be*. This distinction is a frequent 3-mark KTU question.

---

## 1.4 Standard Reference Metrics

The KTU 2024 syllabus and Industry standards (OSHA, ISO, OISD) commonly use the following quantified metrics:

- **Fatality Rate (FAR):** $\text{FAR} = \frac{\text{Number of fatalities} \times 10^8}{\text{Total person-hours worked}}$
- **Lost Time Injury Frequency (LTIF):** Measured per million man-hours
- **Risk Tolerance Threshold:** Typically, individual risk $\leq 10^{-6}$ per year for the public, $\leq 10^{-4}$ per year for workers (Tolerable Risk Limits).

> [!TIP]
> KTU examiners frequently award marks for citing the **risk formula**, the **ALARP principle**, and at least **two standard hazard identification techniques**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Five-Stage Structure of HIRA (KTU High-Yield Framework)

HIRA is not a single step — it is a **structured five-stage process**. The KTU board expects students to reproduce this flow verbatim in 14-mark questions.

### **Stage 1 — Define the System Boundaries**
- Identify the scope: physical boundaries, process units, equipment, personnel, environment.
- Gather **Process & Instrumentation Diagrams (P&IDs)**, Plot Plans, Operating Procedures.
- Define the **Risk Acceptance Criteria** for the project upfront.

### **Stage 2 — Hazard Identification (HAZID)**
- Apply structured techniques to recognize *what can go wrong*.
- Common techniques (covered in detail in Section 2.3 below).

### **Stage 3 — Consequence Analysis (Severity, S)**
- Estimate the *magnitude of harm* if the hazard is realized.
- Severity categories typically range from **1 (Negligible)** to **5 (Catastrophic)**.

### **Stage 4 — Frequency/Likelihood Analysis (Probability, L)**
- Estimate *how often* the hazardous event might occur.
- Likelihood categories range from **1 (Rare)** to **5 (Frequent)**.

### **Stage 5 — Risk Evaluation, Treatment & Documentation**
- Calculate Risk Score: $R = S \times L$.
- Compare against acceptance criteria.
- Apply the **Hierarchy of Controls** to reduce unacceptable risks.
- Document residual risk and obtain **Management Approval**.

---

## 2.2 Standard 5×5 Risk Matrix (KTU Most-Frequently-Examined Tool)

The Risk Matrix is the workhorse of HIRA. The standard **5×5 matrix** assigns numerical values to qualitative judgments:

| Severity ↓ \ Likelihood → | **1 (Rare)** | **2 (Unlikely)** | **3 (Possible)** | **4 (Likely)** | **5 (Frequent)** |
|---------------------------|--------------|-------------------|------------------|----------------|------------------|
| **5 (Catastrophic)** | 5 (Medium) | 10 (High) | 15 (Very High) | 20 (Extreme) | 25 (Extreme) |
| **4 (Major)** | 4 (Low) | 8 (Medium) | 12 (High) | 16 (Very High) | 20 (Extreme) |
| **3 (Moderate)** | 3 (Low) | 6 (Medium) | 9 (Medium) | 12 (High) | 15 (Very High) |
| **2 (Minor)** | 2 (Very Low) | 4 (Low) | 6 (Medium) | 8 (Medium) | 10 (High) |
| **1 (Negligible)** | 1 (Very Low) | 2 (Very Low) | 3 (Low) | 4 (Low) | 5 (Medium) |

**Risk Categories:**
- **1–4:** Low → Acceptable, routine operations with standard controls.
- **5–9:** Medium → Caution, action plan needed.
- **10–15:** High → Corrective action mandatory.
- **16–25:** Very High / Extreme → Operations must halt; immediate intervention.

---

## 2.3 Hazard Identification Techniques — The KTU Toolkit

### **A) HAZOP (Hazard and Operability Study)**
- **Type:** Deductive, team-based.
- **Method:** Applies **Guide Words** (NO/NONE, MORE, LESS, AS WELL AS, PART OF, REVERSE, OTHER THAN) to **process deviations** (Flow, Pressure, Temperature, Level, Composition).
- **Best For:** Chemical and process industries during design stage.

### **B) FMEA (Failure Mode and Effects Analysis)**
- **Type:** Bottom-up, inductive.
- **Output:** **Risk Priority Number (RPN)**
$$RPN = S \times O \times D$$
Where:
- **S** = Severity
- **O** = Occurrence (Likelihood)
- **D** = Detection (difficulty of detecting the failure before it reaches the customer/operator)
- **Best For:** Mechanical, electrical, and automotive systems.

### **C) JSA / JHA (Job Safety Analysis / Job Hazard Analysis)**
- **Type:** Task-based.
- **Procedure:** Break each job into steps → Identify hazards per step → Define controls.
- **Best For:** Routine maintenance, construction, and non-routine tasks.

### **D) What-If Analysis**
- **Type:** Brainstorming-based.
- **Procedure:** Reviewer asks "What if...?" for each deviation.
- **Best For:** Early design phase, quick screening.

### **E) Checklist Analysis**
- **Type:** Compliance-based.
- **Procedure:** Compare against pre-prepared standards (OISD, OSHA, NFPA).
- **Best For:** Routine audits, regulatory compliance verification.

### **F) Fault Tree Analysis (FTA)**
- **Type:** Deductive, top-down.
- **Output:** Boolean logic combination leading to a Top Event.
- **Best For:** Complex systems, aerospace, nuclear, critical safety instrumented systems.

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Mathematical Form | KTU Usage |
|---|-------------------|-------------------|-----------|
| 1 | Basic Risk Equation | $R = S \times L$ | Risk Matrix scoring |
| 2 | FMEA Risk Priority Number | $RPN = S \times O \times D$ | Mechanical/Electrical FMEA |
| 3 | Fault Tree Top Event Probability | $P(T) = \prod P(A_i)$ for AND gate; $P(T) = 1 - \prod(1 - P(A_i))$ for OR gate | FTA numerical problems |
| 4 | Fatal Accident Rate (FAR) | $FAR = \frac{\text{Fatalities} \times 10^8}{\text{Man-hours worked}}$ | Comparing plant safety |
| 5 | Residual Risk | $R_{residual} = R_{inherent} - R_{controlled}$ | Post-mitigation assessment |
| 6 | ALARP "Cost per life saved" test | $Cost \leq \text{Gross societal value of a statistical life}$ | Justifying controls |
| 7 | LOPA Initiating Frequency | $f_{event} = f_{initiating event} \times \prod P_{conditional modifiers}$ | Layer of Protection Analysis |

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

In **petrochemical refineries**, HIRA is a statutory requirement (under OISD, OSHA PSM 29 CFR 1910.119, and Indian Factories Act 1948). Before a process plant is commissioned, a **Pre-Startup Safety Review (PSSR)** is conducted only after HIRA is signed off. In **manufacturing**, FMEA is mandatory under **IATF 16949** (automotive quality). In **aerospace and nuclear**, FTA combined with HAZOP is mandated by **DO-178C** and **AERB** respectively. The output of HIRA directly drives **Safety Integrity Level (SIL)** selection for Instrumented Protective Functions.

> [!TIP]
> Always mention **ISO 31000** as the global risk management framework — it is a guaranteed 1-mark fetcher in KTU exams.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Risk Score from the Risk Matrix

Consider a workshop operation where a worker operates a **circular saw**. We will derive the risk score and classify the operation.

### **Step 1 — Define the Hazard**
A circular saw is used to cut wooden planks. The blade is rotating at high speed and is unguarded.

### **Step 2 — Hazard Identification (HAZID)**
Using a **Job Safety Analysis (JSA)**, we break the task into steps:
1. Switch on the machine.
2. Feed the plank towards the blade.
3. Cut the plank to size.
4. Switch off the machine.

At Step 2, the hazards identified are:
- **H1:** Blade contact → laceration or amputation of fingers.
- **H2:** Wood dust → respiratory irritation.
- **H3:** Noise → hearing damage.

### **Step 3 — Severity (S) Assignment**
Per the consequence analysis:
- **H1 (Laceration):** Could cause permanent disability if finger is severed. → **S = 4 (Major)**
- **H2 (Dust):** Mild irritation, reversible. → **S = 2 (Minor)**
- **H3 (Noise):** Long-term hearing loss. → **S = 3 (Moderate)**

### **Step 4 — Likelihood (L) Assignment**
Per historical data and operational frequency:
- **H1:** Saw is used 8 hours/day, contact possible. → **L = 3 (Possible)**
- **H2:** Dust generated every shift. → **L = 5 (Frequent)**
- **H3:** Continuous noise above 85 dB. → **L = 5 (Frequent)**

### **Step 5 — Risk Score Calculation**

$$R_1 = S_1 \times L_1 = 4 \times 3 = 12$$

$$R_2 = S_2 \times L_2 = 2 \times 5 = 10$$

$$R_3 = S_3 \times L_3 = 3 \times 5 = 15$$

### **Step 6 — Classification per the 5×5 Matrix**

| Hazard | Risk Score | Category | Required Action |
|--------|------------|----------|-----------------|
| H1: Blade contact | 12 | High | Corrective action mandatory |
| H2: Dust | 10 | High | Engineering controls needed |
| H3: Noise | 15 | Very High | Immediate intervention |

### **Step 7 — Risk Treatment (Hierarchy of Controls)**

For H1 (Score 12, High):
- **Elimination:** Substitute with CNC laser cutter (cost prohibitive → not reasonably practicable).
- **Substitution:** Use a push stick.
- **Engineering Control:** Install blade guard with interlock.
- **Administrative Control:** Operator training, signage.
- **PPE:** Cut-resistant gloves, eye protection.

### **Step 8 — Residual Risk Re-Assessment**

After installing the **interlocked blade guard**, likelihood reduces:
- New L1 = 1 (Rare), so:

$$R_{1,residual} = 4 \times 1 = 4 \rightarrow \text{Low}$$

Operation now acceptable.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This worked example illustrates the **complete HIRA loop**. KTU examiners often award full 14 marks for such a coherent, multi-step illustration.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example: FMEA on a Pneumatic Pressure Vessel

**Step 1 — List Failure Modes**

| Component | Failure Mode | Effect | S | O | D | RPN |
|-----------|--------------|--------|---|---|---|-----|
| Pressure Relief Valve | Fails to open | Vessel overpressure | 5 | 2 | 3 | 30 |
| Pressure Gauge | Reads low | Operator unaware | 4 | 3 | 4 | 48 |
| Compressed Air Hose | Rupture | Sudden depressurization | 3 | 4 | 5 | 60 |

**Step 2 — Risk Priority Number (RPN) Computation**

$$RPN_{relief} = S \times O \times D = 5 \times 2 \times 3 = 30$$

$$RPN_{gauge} = 4 \times 3 \times 4 = 48$$

$$RPN_{hose} = 3 \times 4 \times 5 = 60$$

**Step 3 — Risk Treatment Decision**
The hose has the highest RPN (60). Recommended actions:
- Periodic hydrostatic testing.
- Replacement schedule every 2 years.
- Install protective hose sleeve.
- Recompute RPN post-mitigation.

> [!TIP]
> For 14-mark FMEA questions, students must reproduce the **RPN table** with all 10 columns (Component, Function, Failure Mode, Effect, Cause, S, O, D, RPN, Recommended Action). This is the standard KTU expectation.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix of HIRA Techniques

| Technique | Approach | Best Stage | Quantitative Output | Industry |
|-----------|----------|------------|---------------------|----------|
| HAZOP | Guide-word inductive | Design / Detailed Engineering | Risk Matrix Score | Chemical, Oil & Gas |
| FMEA | Component bottom-up | Design / Manufacturing | RPN | Mechanical, Automotive |
| FTA | Top-down deductive | Reliability / Safety | Probability of Top Event | Nuclear, Aerospace |
| JSA / JHA | Task-based | Operations / Maintenance | Risk Score | Construction, Manufacturing |
| What-If | Brainstorming | Conceptual / Early Design | Qualitative | All industries |
| Checklist | Compliance-based | Routine Operations | Pass / Fail | Audits, Compliance |
| LOPA | Semi-quantitative | Pre-design / SIL Selection | Mitigated Frequency | Process Safety |
| Bow-Tie | Cause + Consequence | Operations / Barriers Mgmt | Event Frequency | Oil & Gas, Major Hazard |

> [!WARNING]
> Students often confuse **HAZOP** with **What-If**. HAZOP uses **fixed guide words** on **process deviations** — it is highly structured. What-If is open-ended brainstorming. This distinction carries 2 marks.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The HIRA Master Workflow (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["System Definition and Boundary"] --> B["Hazard Identification HAZID"]
    B --> C["Hazard Register Compilation"]
    C --> D["Consequence Severity Analysis"]
    C --> E["Likelihood Probability Analysis"]
    D --> F["Risk Score Calculation R = S x L"]
    E --> F
    F --> G["Risk Evaluation against Criteria"]
    G --> H{"Risk Acceptable?"}
    H -- Yes --> I["Document Residual Risk"]
    H -- No --> J["Apply Hierarchy of Controls"]
    J --> K["Implement Controls"]
    K --> L["Recompute Residual Risk"]
    L --> H
    I --> M["Management Approval and PSSR"]
```

---

## 4.2 Fault Tree Analysis — Top-Down Logic Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1["Top Event Pressure Vessel Rupture"]
    P1 --> G1["AND Gate Both required"]
    P1 --> G2["OR Gate Either causes"]
    G1 --> A1["Overpressure Exists"]
    G1 --> A2["Relief Valve Fails"]
    G2 --> B1["External Fire Heating"]
    G2 --> B2["Internal Exothermic Reaction"]
    G2 --> B3["Blockage in Outlet Line"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> Each leaf node (A1, A2, B1, B2, B3) is then quantified by assigning failure probabilities. The top event probability is computed using Boolean algebra — a 7-mark derivation in KTU board exams.

---

## 4.3 Bow-Tie Diagram — Cause-Consequence Barrier Model

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    L["Left Side Threats Causes"] --- C["Top Event Loss of Containment"]
    C --- R["Right Side Consequences Outcomes"]
    L --> B1["Preventive Barrier 1 Design"]
    L --> B2["Preventive Barrier 2 Operating Procedures"]
    L --> B3["Preventive Barrier 3 ESD System"]
    C --> B4["Mitigative Barrier 1 Fire Protection"]
    C --> B5["Mitigative Barrier 2 Emergency Response"]
    C --> B6["Mitigative Barrier 3 Medical Aid"]
```

---

## 4.4 Hierarchy of Controls — Inverted Pyramid

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1["Elimination Most Effective"] --> A2["Substitution"]
    A2 --> A3["Engineering Controls"]
    A3 --> A4["Administrative Controls"]
    A4 --> A5["PPE Least Effective"]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This pyramid must be drawn **with Elimination at the top and PPE at the bottom**. KTU examiners often award 2 marks for correctly ordering the hierarchy.

---

## 4.5 FMEA Logic Sheet

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    F1["Identify Component"] --> F2["List Failure Modes"]
    F2 --> F3["Determine Effects on System"]
    F3 --> F4["Assign S O D Ratings"]
    F4 --> F5["Calculate RPN"]
    F5 --> F6{"RPN above threshold?"}
    F6 -- Yes --> F7["Recommend Corrective Action"]
    F7 --> F8["Recalculate RPN"]
    F6 -- No --> F9["Accept and Document"]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1: [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model]**
*Define Hazard Identification and Risk Assessment. Differentiate between Hazard and Risk with one industrial example each.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks — Strict 60-90 Word Format):**

**Hazard Identification and Risk Assessment (HIRA)** is a structured engineering process used to identify workplace hazards and evaluate the corresponding risks by combining the **severity of harm (S)** with the **likelihood of occurrence (L)** via the relation **R = S × L**.

**Hazard** is the *intrinsic property* with the potential to cause harm. **Example:** Stored LPG in a bullet tank.

**Risk** is the *probability and severity* of harm being realized from that hazard. **Example:** Probability of LPG leak igniting and causing an explosion.

This distinction between **what can cause harm** (hazard) and **how likely + how serious** (risk) is fundamental to the HIRA methodology.

---

### **Question 2: [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model]**
*List any three Hazard Identification techniques and state one application area of each.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks — Tabular Form Acceptable):**

| Sl. No. | Technique | Application Area |
|---------|-----------|------------------|
| 1 | **HAZOP** | Chemical process plants during design stage |
| 2 | **FMEA** | Mechanical and automotive component design |
| 3 | **JSA (Job Safety Analysis)** | Maintenance and non-routine operational tasks |
| 4 | **Fault Tree Analysis** | Aerospace and nuclear reliability analysis |

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 Model]**

**(a)** Explain the **structure of HIRA** in detail. List the five stages and describe each stage with appropriate examples. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** With the help of a **5×5 Risk Matrix**, explain how the risk score is calculated for an industrial operation. Provide a worked example. **(7 Marks)**

#### **Model Solution to Question A(a):**

The structure of **Hazard Identification and Risk Assessment (HIRA)** is a five-stage systematic methodology:

**Stage 1 — System Definition:**
The scope, physical boundaries, equipment, and personnel are defined. Documents such as **P&IDs, plot plans, and operating procedures** are collected. Risk acceptance criteria are pre-defined.

**Stage 2 — Hazard Identification (HAZID):**
Techniques such as **HAZOP, FMEA, JSA, What-If, Checklist, and Fault Tree Analysis** are applied to identify credible hazards. Output: A **Hazard Register** listing all identified hazards.

**Stage 3 — Consequence Analysis (Severity, S):**
The potential consequence of each hazard is assessed on a 1–5 scale (Negligible to Catastrophic).

**Stage 4 — Likelihood Analysis (Probability, L):**
The frequency of occurrence is assessed on a 1–5 scale (Rare to Frequent). Historical data, failure databases, and engineering judgment are used.

**Stage 5 — Risk Evaluation and Treatment:**
Risk score $R = S \times L$ is computed and compared against the **Risk Acceptance Criteria**. Unacceptable risks are reduced using the **Hierarchy of Controls** (Elimination → Substitution → Engineering → Administrative → PPE). **Residual risk** is re-evaluated and documented.

---

#### **Model Solution to Question A(b):**

The **5×5 Risk Matrix** is a semi-quantitative tool used to evaluate risk by combining severity (rows) with likelihood (columns). Each axis is graded on a scale of 1 to 5.

**Worked Example — LPG Cylinder Storage Yard:**

| Hazard | Severity (S) | Likelihood (L) | Risk Score R = S × L | Category |
|--------|--------------|----------------|----------------------|----------|
| LPG leak during decanting | 5 | 2 | 10 | High |
| Fire due to ignition source nearby | 5 | 3 | 15 | Very High |
| Cylinder physical damage during handling | 3 | 4 | 12 | High |

**Risk Categories per the Matrix:**
- 1–4: Low (acceptable)
- 5–9: Medium (action plan)
- 10–15: High (corrective action mandatory)
- 16–25: Very High / Extreme (immediate stoppage)

**Treatment for the High and Very High risks:**
1. Install **gas detectors** with automatic shut-off valves (engineering control).
2. Enforce **no-smoking zones** with strict signage (administrative control).
3. Provide **flame-resistant PPE** to all operators.

After controls, residual risk reduces from 15 to a Low score, demonstrating successful risk reduction to **ALARP**.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 Model Alternative]**

**(a)** Explain **HAZOP** and **FMEA** techniques. Compare them on the basis of approach, output, and application. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** With a suitable example, explain the **Fault Tree Analysis (FTA)** methodology. Derive the mathematical expression for the **top event probability** using AND and OR gates. **(7 Marks)**

#### **Model Solution to Question B(a):**

**HAZOP (Hazard and Operability Study):**
HAZOP is a structured, team-based, inductive technique applied during the **design stage** of chemical and process plants. It uses **Guide Words** (NO, MORE, LESS, AS WELL AS, PART OF, REVERSE, OTHER THAN) applied to process deviations such as Flow, Pressure, Temperature, Level, and Composition to identify potential hazards and operability issues. The output is a **list of deviations, causes, consequences, and recommended actions**, scored on a Risk Matrix.

**FMEA (Failure Mode and Effects Analysis):**
FMEA is a **bottom-up, component-level** technique used primarily in mechanical, electrical, and automotive engineering. Each component is analyzed for potential failure modes, their effects, and causes. The output is the **Risk Priority Number (RPN)**:

$$RPN = S \times O \times D$$

Where S = Severity, O = Occurrence, D = Detection.

**Comparison Table:**

| Parameter | HAZOP | FMEA |
|-----------|-------|------|
| Approach | Inductive, team-based | Bottom-up, component-based |
| Input | P&ID, process parameters | Component specifications |
| Output | Deviation register with risk score | RPN value with action priority |
| Application | Chemical, Oil & Gas | Mechanical, Automotive, Electronics |
| Quantification | Semi-quantitative (Risk Matrix) | Quantitative (RPN) |

---

#### **Model Solution to Question B(b):**

**Fault Tree Analysis (FTA)** is a **deductive, top-down** safety analysis technique that uses Boolean logic gates to model how combinations of lower-level failures lead to an undesired **Top Event**.

**Example:** A pressure vessel rupture (Top Event) can occur due to:
- **OR Gate (Either cause):** External fire OR Internal exothermic runaway.
- **AND Gate (Both required):** Overpressure condition AND Relief valve fails.

**Mathematical Derivation of Top Event Probability:**

For an **AND gate**, the top event occurs only when all input events occur simultaneously. Assuming independence:

$$P(T_{AND}) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(A_i)$$

$$P(T_{AND}) = P(A_1) \times P(A_2) \times \cdots \times P(A_n)$$

For an **OR gate**, the top event occurs if *any one* of the input events occurs. Using the complement rule:

$$P(T_{OR}) = 1 - P(\text{none of the events occur})$$

$$P(T_{OR}) = 1 - \prod_{i=1}^{n} \left(1 - P(A_i)\right)$$

For the worked example with overpressure probability $P(O) = 10^{-3}$/year and relief valve failure probability $P(R) = 10^{-2}$:

$$P(T_{AND}) = 10^{-3} \times 10^{-2} = 10^{-5} \text{ per year}$$

This is then compared with the **tolerable risk threshold** ($10^{-4}$/year for workers) to determine if additional safeguards (e.g., SIL-2 instrumented protective function) are needed.

---

## 5.3 Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls — Where KTU Students Lose Marks:**
> 
> 1. **Hazard vs. Risk Confusion (–2 Marks):** Writing "fire" as a risk instead of as a hazard. Risk must be a *combination* of hazard + likelihood.
> 
> 2. **Hierarchy of Controls Reversal (–2 Marks):** Listing PPE first instead of Elimination. PPE is the *least effective* control.
> 
> 3. **RPN Formula Omission (–2 Marks):** Stating RPN = S × O × D without explaining each parameter (S, O, D).
> 
> 4. **Missing Standards (–1 Mark):** Not citing **ISO 31000 / IEC 31010 / OISD / OSHA PSM** as the basis of HIRA.
> 
> 5. **Fault Tree Math Errors (–3 Marks):** Writing AND as $P(A) + P(B)$ instead of $P(A) \times P(B)$.
> 
> 6. **Residual Risk Not Recomputed (–1 Mark):** Showing controls applied but not calculating the new risk score.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute KTU Revision — Memorize These 12 Points:**
> 
> - **HIRA = Hazard Identification + Risk Assessment.** It is a 5-stage structured process.
> - **Risk Equation:** $R = S \times L$ (Basic), $RPN = S \times O \times D$ (FMEA).
> - **Hazard ≠ Risk.** Hazard is intrinsic; Risk depends on probability.
> - **Risk Matrix** is the standard 5×5 evaluation tool. Scores: 1–4 Low, 5–9 Medium, 10–15 High, 16–25 Extreme.
> - **HAZOP** uses Guide Words (NO, MORE, LESS, AS WELL AS, PART OF, REVERSE, OTHER THAN) on process deviations (Flow, Pressure, Temperature, Level).
> - **FMEA** computes RPN and prioritizes component failures.
> - **FTA** uses Boolean logic: AND → multiply probabilities, OR → use complement rule $1 - \prod(1-P_i)$.
> - **Hierarchy of Controls (Top → Bottom):** Elimination → Substitution → Engineering → Administrative → PPE.
> - **ALARP** = As Low As Reasonably Practicable. Cost-benefit justified mitigation.
> - **Standards to Cite:** ISO 31000, IEC 31010, OSHA 29 CFR 1910.119, OISD, Indian Factories Act 1948.
> - **Residual Risk** must always be recomputed after applying controls.
> - **Bow-Tie Diagram** shows threats on the left, top event in the center, and consequences on the right, with preventive and mitigative barriers.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Identification of Hazards - Inventory Analysis
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAUT415 - INDUSTRIAL SAFETY ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Identification of Hazards - Inventory Analysis

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Inventory Analysis** is a *systematic, preliminary hazard identification technique* in process safety engineering that involves the **comprehensive compilation, documentation, and evaluation of all materials** (raw materials, intermediates, finished products, by-products, catalysts, and waste streams) present in an industrial facility, along with their **physical, chemical, toxicological, and reactive properties**, quantities, storage conditions, and process parameters. It serves as the **foundational database** upon which all subsequent, higher-order hazard analyses (HAZOP, FMEA, FTA) are built.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme parlance, Inventory Analysis is classified under **Module 4: Hazard Identification Techniques** as a **non-quantitative, descriptive, qualitative screening tool** used during the *conceptual and basic engineering phases* of a process plant's lifecycle.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (The "Patient Medical History" Analogy)

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of a chemical plant exactly like a patient visiting a doctor for the first time.**

When a doctor first sees a patient, they do **not** jump to ordering an MRI or a CT scan. They start by asking:
- *"What medicines do you take daily?"* (List of chemicals/compounds)
- *"What allergies do you have?"* (Reactivity and incompatibility data)
- *"What is your past medical history?"* (Past incidents, MSDS — Material Safety Data Sheets)
- *"What are your vital signs (BP, pulse, temperature)?"* (Process conditions: T, P, flow rate)

Only after collecting this **"inventory of the patient"** does the doctor proceed to deeper diagnostic tests (X-ray, blood work) — analogous to HAZOP, FMEA, and Fault Tree Analysis.

**Inventory Analysis is the "vital signs check" of a chemical plant.** Without it, no serious hazard study can begin.

## 1.3 Position in the Hazard Identification Hierarchy

The hierarchy of hazard identification methodologies (from simple to complex) is:

| Level | Technique | Nature | Use Phase |
|:-----:|:----------|:-------|:----------|
| 1 | **Inventory Analysis** | Qualitative / Descriptive | Conceptual Design |
| 2 | Checklist Analysis | Qualitative | Detailed Design |
| 3 | Preliminary Hazard Analysis (PHA) | Qualitative | Basic Engineering |
| 4 | What-If Analysis | Semi-Quantitative | Detailed Engineering |
| 5 | HAZOP | Semi-Quantitative | Detailed Engineering |
| 6 | FMEA / FTA | Quantitative | Operations |

> **Inventory Analysis is the bedrock — without accurate material data, every higher-level study becomes unreliable.**

## 1.4 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics in Inventory Analysis

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following standard safety parameters **must be documented** for every material in the inventory. They are universally reported in:
> - **Material Safety Data Sheets (MSDS)** — older term
> - **Safety Data Sheets (SDS)** — globally harmonized (GHS-aligned) term, **16 standardized sections**

| Metric | Symbol | Unit | Significance |
|:-------|:------:|:-----|:-------------|
| Flash Point | $T_f$ | °C | Lowest temp at which vapor ignites |
| Auto-Ignition Temperature | $T_{ait}$ | °C | Spontaneous ignition temp without external flame |
| Lower Explosive Limit | **LEL** | % vol | Min vapor concentration for explosion |
| Upper Explosive Limit | **UEL** | % vol | Max vapor concentration for explosion |
| Lethal Dose 50% | **LD₅₀** | mg/kg body wt | Acute oral/dermal toxicity |
| Lethal Concentration 50% | **LC₅₀** | ppm or mg/L | Acute inhalation toxicity |
| Threshold Limit Value | **TLV** | ppm or mg/m³ | Safe airborne exposure (8-hr TWA) |
| Permissible Exposure Limit | **PEL** | ppm or mg/m³ | OSHA-regulated exposure limit |
| Vapor Density | $\rho_v$ | — (rel. to air) | Heavier-than-air settling behavior |
| Boiling Point | $T_b$ | °C | Volatility indicator |
| Melting Point | $T_m$ | °C | Solid-handling indicator |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Six Pillars of Inventory Analysis

Inventory Analysis rests on **six structured data pillars** that must be collected for every substance on site:

### Pillar 1 — Material Identification
- **Chemical name** (IUPAC + common/trade name)
- **CAS Registry Number** (unique chemical identifier, e.g., 67-56-1 for methanol)
- **UN Number** (transport classification, e.g., UN1230)
- **Molecular formula and molecular weight** $M$
- **Physical state at operating conditions** (solid / liquid / gas)
- **Purity grade** (technical, reagent, food-grade)

### Pillar 2 — Physical Properties
- Boiling point $T_b$, melting point $T_m$, flash point $T_f$
- Vapor pressure $P_v$ (Pa), vapor density $\rho_v$ (relative to air = 1)
- Specific gravity, solubility in water, pH
- **Critical temperature** $T_c$ and **critical pressure** $P_c$ (above which gas cannot be liquefied by pressure alone)

### Pillar 3 — Chemical Reactivity
- **Reactivity with water** (hydrolysis, e.g., sodium + H₂O → NaOH + H₂↑)
- **Reactivity with air/oxygen** (pyrophoricity, peroxidation, e.g., white phosphorus)
- **Reactivity with other process chemicals** (incompatibility matrix)
- **Polymerization hazards** (runaway reactions, e.g., styrene, acrylates)
- **Oxidizer / reducing agent classification**

### Pillar 4 — Toxicological Properties
- **Acute toxicity**: LD₅₀, LC₅₀
- **Chronic toxicity**: carcinogenicity, teratogenicity, mutagenicity
- **Local effects**: corrosivity to skin, eyes, mucous membranes
- **Systemic effects**: target organs (liver, kidney, CNS, lungs)
- **IDLH** (Immediately Dangerous to Life or Health) concentration

### Pillar 5 — Fire & Explosion Properties
- **Flammability classification** (Class IA, IB, IC liquids per NFPA; Categories 1–4 per GHS)
- **Explosive range** (LEL–UEL) at operating T and P
- **Minimum Ignition Energy (MIE)** — Joules
- **Burning rate**, **heat of combustion** $\Delta H_c$ (kJ/kg)
- **Dust explosibility** (Kst value, bar·m/s) for powders

### Pillar 6 — Process & Storage Conditions
- **Inventory quantity** at each location (kg, tonnes, m³)
- **Storage temperature and pressure** (ambient, refrigerated, cryogenic, pressurized)
- **Containment type** (drum, tank, sphere, cylinder, pile, silo)
- **Process conditions** during handling (T, P, flow rate)

## 2.2 The Incompatibility Matrix (Chemical Interaction Map)

A critical deliverable of Inventory Analysis is the **chemical incompatibility matrix** — a square table where each material is checked against every other material for reactive incompatibility.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the matrix matters:** Many catastrophic industrial accidents (e.g., Bhopal 1984 — MIC + water; Texas City 1947 — ammonium nitrate + fuel oil) originated from **incompatible materials being stored or mixed in close proximity.**

A simplified $4 \times 4$ incompatibility matrix structure is shown in Section 4.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Equations for Inventory Analysis & Threshold Calculations**

| # | Formula | Meaning | Typical Use |
|:-:|:--------|:--------|:------------|
| 1 | $M = \sum_i (n_i \cdot M_i)$ | Molecular weight of mixture | Multi-component streams |
| 2 | $\rho_{mix} = \dfrac{1}{\sum_i \dfrac{x_i}{\rho_i}}$ | Mixture density (mass-averaged) | Liquid storage calc. |
| 3 | $P_v(T) = P_v^o \cdot \exp\!\left[\dfrac{\Delta H_{vap}}{R}\!\left(\dfrac{1}{T_b} - \dfrac{1}{T}\right)\right]$ | Clausius-Clapeyron vapor pressure | Estimate $P_v$ at any T |
| 4 | $C_{vap} = \dfrac{P_v \cdot M}{R \cdot T}$ | Vapor concentration (mass/vol) | Compare to TLV |
| 5 | $ER = \dfrac{C_{vap} - LEL}{UEL - LEL}$ | Explosive Range Position | Flammability assessment |
| 6 | $Q_{fire} = m \cdot \Delta H_c$ | Total heat release in fire | Fire load calculation |
| 7 | $A_{eff} = \dfrac{10^{(L_{eq}/10)} \cdot V}{T_{rev} \cdot c_0}$ | Sabine equation (sound) | Noise hazard inventory |
| 8 | $PEL_{mix} = \dfrac{1}{\sum_i \dfrac{C_i}{PEL_i}}$ | Additive mixture exposure | Toxic mixture TLV |
| 9 | $Q_{vessel} = \dfrac{V}{C_v \cdot \Delta T}$ | Vent sizing (NFPA 30) | Emergency relief calc. |
| 10 | $d = \dfrac{\sqrt{M}}{...}$ | Dispersion distance (Pasquill-Gifford) | Toxic gas inventory |

> **Note on Equation 8 (Additive Mixture TLV):** This is used when multiple airborne contaminants with **similar toxicological effects** are present. Each $C_i$ is the measured concentration, $PEL_i$ the permissible limit. If $\sum C_i / PEL_i > 1$, exposure exceeds the standard.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Industry Sector | Application of Inventory Analysis |
|:----------------|:----------------------------------|
| **Petrochemical** | Cataloging all hydrocarbons, acids, catalysts; mapping hydrocracker units |
| **Pharmaceutical** | Tracking active ingredients, solvents (acetone, methanol, DCM), intermediates |
| **Pesticide / Agrochemical** | Highly toxic compounds (organophosphates) with strict TLVs |
| **Storage Terminals** | Tank-farm analysis — every tank has unique hazard profile |
| **Aerospace / Defense** | Propellants, oxidizers (LOX, N₂O₄), pyrotechnics |
| **Food Processing** | Ammonia refrigeration, chlorine sanitizers, dust explosibility (flour, sugar) |
| **Semiconductor** | Ultra-pure acids, HF, silane (pyrophoric), arsine (toxic) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Industry Standard Reference:** Inventory Analysis is mandated by the **OSHA Process Safety Management (PSM) Standard 29 CFR 1910.119 (d)** and the **EPA Risk Management Program (RMP)**, which require employers to maintain a complete **Process Safety Information (PSI)** package for every highly hazardous chemical. The **CCPS (Center for Chemical Process Safety)** guidelines further codify the practice in *Guidelines for Process Safety Information Management*.

## 2.5 Limitations of Inventory Analysis

> [!WARNING]
> - **Purely qualitative** — does not estimate accident frequency or consequence magnitude
> - **Does not evaluate deviations** from design intent (that's HAZOP's job)
> - **Static snapshot** — must be updated whenever a process modification is made (Management of Change — MOC)
> - **Depends heavily on data quality** — incomplete MSDS/SDS gives a false sense of security
> - **Cannot detect interactions between equipment, procedures, and human factors**

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Estimating Vapor Concentration Above a Liquid Pool

A common inventory-analysis calculation is: *"Given a chemical in an open tank at temperature $T$, what is the vapor concentration above the liquid, and is it inside the explosive range?"*

**Step 1 — State Raoult's Law** for an ideal solution:
$$ P_{vap,i} = x_i \cdot P_i^{sat}(T) $$

**Step 2 — Apply the Clausius-Clapeyron Equation** to obtain the saturation pressure at temperature $T$:
$$ \ln\!\left(\dfrac{P_i^{sat}}{P^{o}}\right) = -\dfrac{\Delta H_{vap,i}}{R}\left(\dfrac{1}{T} - \dfrac{1}{T_b}\right) $$

**Step 3 — Use the Ideal Gas Law** to convert partial vapor pressure into airborne mass concentration $C_{vap,i}$:
$$ C_{vap,i} = \dfrac{P_{vap,i} \cdot M_i}{R \cdot T} $$

**Step 4 — Compare $C_{vap,i}$ to LEL and UEL** (converted to same units, e.g., mg/m³) using the explosive range position:
$$ ER_{pos} = \dfrac{C_{vap,i} - LEL}{UEL - LEL} $$

**Interpretation:**
- If $ER_{pos} < 0$ → Below LEL (too lean, no ignition)
- If $0 \le ER_{pos} \le 1$ → **Inside explosive envelope (DANGER)**
- If $ER_{pos} > 1$ → Above UEL (too rich, no ignition)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Example:** Hexane has $LEL = 1.1\%$ vol, $UEL = 7.5\%$ vol, $T_b = 69$ °C. At 25 °C, the saturation vapor pressure of hexane is approximately $P^{sat} \approx 20$ kPa, which corresponds to a vapor concentration of about $20\%$ vol — **far above the UEL** in a closed vessel. However, in a well-ventilated space the concentration drops below LEL, illustrating that **inventory properties alone are insufficient; ventilation and process conditions matter equally.**

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Fire Load (Total Heat Release) from Inventory

The **fire load** $Q_{fire}$ of an inventory is the total chemical energy available for release in a fire event. It is the foundation for designing sprinkler systems, fire walls, and emergency response.

**Step 1 — Identify the mass** $m_i$ of each combustible material $i$ in the inventory.

**Step 2 — Obtain the heat of combustion** $\Delta H_{c,i}$ (kJ/kg) for each material.

**Step 3 — Compute the total fire energy:**
$$ Q_{fire} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} m_i \cdot \Delta H_{c,i} $$

**Step 4 — Compute the fire load density** (fire load per unit floor area):
$$ q_{fire} = \dfrac{Q_{fire}}{A_{floor}} $$

**Step 5 — Classification per NFPA / Indian Standard IS 14435:**

| $q_{fire}$ (MJ/m²) | Classification |
|:-------------------:|:---------------|
| $< 500$ | Low fire load |
| $500 - 1000$ | Moderate fire load |
| $1000 - 2000$ | High fire load |
| $> 2000$ | Very high fire load |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Design Implication:** A fire load density $q_{fire} > 1000$ MJ/m² mandates **enhanced fire protection** (sprinklers, firewalls, blast-resistant separation distances) per most international codes.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Additive Toxic Exposure (Mixture TLV)

When workers are exposed to **multiple chemicals with similar toxicological action**, OSHA's additive formula is applied:

$$ \dfrac{C_1}{TLV_1} + \dfrac{C_2}{TLV_2} + \cdots + \dfrac{C_n}{TLV_n} = E_{mix} $$

**Decision rule:**
- If $E_{mix} \le 1$ → Exposure is **within acceptable limits**
- If $E_{mix} > 1$ → Exposure **exceeds** permissible limits; engineering controls required

**Worked Mini-Example:**

A worker is exposed to:
- Solvent A: $C_1 = 100$ ppm, $TLV_1 = 200$ ppm
- Solvent B: $C_2 = 50$ ppm, $TLV_2 = 100$ ppm

$$ E_{mix} = \dfrac{100}{200} + \dfrac{50}{100} = 0.50 + 0.50 = 1.00 $$

**Result:** $E_{mix} = 1.00$ — **at the threshold**. Any further increase in either concentration mandates control measures (ventilation, PPE, substitution).

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Stock vs. Flow Hazard Categories

A **2×2 hazard classification matrix** is widely used in inventory analysis to prioritize safety reviews:

| | **Low Quantity** (< 100 kg) | **High Quantity** (≥ 100 kg or ≥ TQ) |
|:--|:----------------------------|:--------------------------------------|
| **High Toxicity / Reactivity** | **Priority 2** — Lab-scale controls | **Priority 1** — Full PSM, HAZOP, mitigation |
| **Low Toxicity / Reactivity** | **Priority 4** — Standard GMP | **Priority 3** — Routine review |

> **TQ** = Threshold Quantity (defined in OSHA PSM and EPA RMP, e.g., 2,500 lb for flammable liquids).

## 3.5 Step-by-Step Inventory Analysis Procedure (Industry-Aligned Flow)

| Step | Action | Output / Deliverable |
|:----:|:-------|:---------------------|
| 1 | Define the **system boundary** (P&ID review, plant walk-down) | Bounded plant list |
| 2 | Compile a **complete list of chemicals** | Master Chemical List (MCL) |
| 3 | Obtain **SDS for every chemical** | SDS library |
| 4 | Populate a **Material Hazard Data Sheet (MHDS)** for each | MHDS database |
| 5 | Build the **incompatibility matrix** | Matrix document |
| 6 | Calculate **fire load, vapor concentration, TLV-mix** | Quantitative screen |
| 7 | Categorize hazards per **NFPA 704 diamond** | Hazard diamonds |
| 8 | Document **storage and handling conditions** | Storage inventory |
| 9 | Identify **threshold-quantity (TQ) exceedances** | TQ compliance list |
| 10 | Feed results into **HAZOP, PHA, FMEA** as the baseline | Safety Study Base |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Flowchart — Inventory Analysis Procedure (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Plant Process Boundary Defined] --> B[Compile Master Chemical List MCL]
    B --> C[Obtain SDS for Every Chemical]
    C --> D[Extract Physical Properties Tb Tm Pf]
    C --> E[Extract Chemical Reactivity Data]
    C --> F[Extract Toxicological Data LD50 TLV PEL]
    C --> G[Extract Fire Explosion Data LEL UEL Hc]
    D --> H[Populate Material Hazard Data Sheet]
    E --> H
    F --> H
    G --> H
    H --> I[Build Chemical Incompatibility Matrix]
    I --> J[Calculate Fire Load Qfire]
    I --> K[Estimate Vapor Concentration Cvap]
    I --> L[Compute Additive TLV Emix]
    J --> M[Quantitative Hazard Screen]
    K --> M
    L --> M
    M --> N{Cross TQ Threshold?}
    N -- Yes --> O[Flag for PSM Coverage]
    N -- No --> P[Routine Inventory Track]
    O --> Q[Input to HAZOP PHA FMEA]
    P --> Q
    Q --> R[End: Safety Study Baseline Established]
```

## 4.2 NFPA 704 Hazard Diamond (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph NFPA_Diamond
        direction TB
        TOP["BLUE: HEALTH HAZARD - 0 to 4"]
        MID["WHITE: SPECIAL HAZARDS - OXY W OX GLY RF"]
        BOTTOM["RED: FLAMMABILITY HAZARD - 0 to 4"]
        YELLOW["YELLOW: REACTIVITY HAZARD - 0 to 4"]
    end
    TOP --> MID
    MID --> BOTTOM
    YELLOW --> BOTTOM
```

**NFPA 704 Rating Legend:**

| Rating | Health (Blue) | Flammability (Red) | Reactivity (Yellow) |
|:------:|:--------------|:-------------------|:--------------------|
| **0** | No hazard | Will not burn | Stable |
| **1** | Slight irritation | Must be preheated to burn | Unstable if heated |
| **2** | Temporary injury | Must be heated before ignition | Violent chemical change |
| **3** | Serious or permanent injury | Can be ignited under most conditions | Detonation possible |
| **4** | Fatal or irreversible | Rapidly vaporizes & burns | Can detonate at normal T/P |

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Material Hazard Data Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Raw Material Receiving] --> B[In-Process Storage]
    B --> C[Reactor Unit]
    C --> D[Intermediate Storage]
    D --> E[Separator Distillation]
    E --> F[Product Storage]
    F --> G[Dispatch Loading]
    A --> H[Inventory Database]
    B --> H
    C --> H
    D --> H
    E --> H
    F --> H
    G --> H
    H --> I[Safety Information Pack]
    I --> J[HAZOP Team]
    I --> K[PHA Team]
    I --> L[Emergency Response Plan]
```

## 4.4 Incompatibility Matrix Visualization (4×4 Conceptual Layout)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph IC["Chemical Incompatibility Matrix - 4 by 4 Concept"]
        direction LR
        M1[Material A] -- A vs B -- M2[Material B]
        M1 -- A vs C -- M3[Material C]
        M1 -- A vs D -- M4[Material D]
        M2 -- B vs C -- M3
        M2 -- B vs D -- M4
        M3 -- C vs D -- M4
    end
    M2 -- A vs B Result -- R1[Check SDS, Flag Incompatibility if Water Reactive vs Acid]
    M3 -- A vs C Result -- R2[Check SDS, Flag if Oxidizer vs Fuel]
    M4 -- A vs D Result -- R3[Check SDS, Flag if Pyrophoric vs Air]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Practical reading of incompatibility matrices:** A "blank" cell does not mean "safe" — it means **"not evaluated yet."** Every cell must be populated with either *"Compatible under specified conditions"* or *"Incompatible — segregate per NFPA / IS 15484."*

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]`
> **Define Inventory Analysis as a hazard identification tool. List the four key data categories that must be compiled during the analysis.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (2 marks):** Inventory Analysis is a *systematic preliminary hazard identification technique* in which all chemicals present in a process plant are catalogued and evaluated for their hazardous properties, quantities, storage conditions, and process parameters. It forms the **foundational database** for higher-level studies such as HAZOP and FMEA.

**Four key data categories (1 mark — any 4 of the following):**
1. Material identification (chemical name, CAS, UN number)
2. Physical properties (boiling point, flash point, vapor pressure)
3. Chemical reactivity (incompatibility, polymerization hazard)
4. Toxicological data (LD₅₀, TLV, PEL)
5. Fire & explosion properties (LEL, UEL, heat of combustion)
6. Storage and process conditions (T, P, inventory quantity)

---

> **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam — May 2023]`
> **Differentiate between LEL and UEL. Why is the position of operating vapor concentration relative to these limits critical for safety?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Parameter | LEL (Lower Explosive Limit) | UEL (Upper Explosive Limit) |
|:----------|:---------------------------:|:---------------------------:|
| Meaning | Minimum vapor % in air that can ignite | Maximum vapor % in air that can ignite |
| Below | Too lean to burn | — |
| Above | — | Too rich to burn |
| Between | **Flammable / Explosive Range** | **Flammable / Explosive Range** |

**Criticality (1 mark):** If the actual operating vapor concentration lies between LEL and UEL, even a small ignition source (spark, hot surface) can cause a **vapor cloud explosion**. Hence, inventory analysis must compute $C_{vap}$ and compare it to the LEL–UEL band to determine whether the process is operating in the **dangerous regime**.

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

> ### **Question A** `[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme]`
>
> **(a) [7 marks]** Explain the **step-by-step procedure** for conducting an Inventory Analysis in a chemical process plant. Illustrate the relationship between the Material Safety Data Sheet (SDS) and the Material Hazard Data Sheet (MHDS).
>
> **(b) [7 marks]** A process plant stores **5,000 kg of acetone** at 25 °C in an atmospheric tank.
> - **Acetone properties:** $M = 58$ g/mol, $P^{sat}_{25°C} = 30.6$ kPa, $LEL = 2.5\%$ vol, $UEL = 12.8\%$ vol, $\Delta H_c = 29$ MJ/kg.
> - **Universal gas constant:** $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K).
> - **Universal temperature:** $T = 298$ K.
>
> Compute: **(i)** the vapor concentration in the tank headspace (in % vol), and **(ii)** the total fire energy released if the entire inventory burns.

### Model Solution — Question A

#### Part (a) — Step-by-Step Procedure [7 marks]

| Step | Description | Marks |
|:----:|:------------|:------|
| 1 | Define the **system boundary** using P&IDs and plant walk-downs. Identify all sections, units, and streams. | 1 |
| 2 | Compile the **Master Chemical List (MCL)** — every chemical handled, stored, or produced. | 1 |
| 3 | Obtain the **16-section Safety Data Sheet (SDS)** for each chemical (GHS-aligned). | 1 |
| 4 | Extract data and populate a **Material Hazard Data Sheet (MHDS)** per chemical. | 1 |
| 5 | Build the **Chemical Incompatibility Matrix**. | 1 |
| 6 | Perform **quantitative screen** — fire load, vapor concentration, TLV-mix. | 1 |
| 7 | Flag **threshold-quantity (TQ) exceedances** and feed results into HAZOP, PHA, FMEA. | 0.5 |
| 8 | Maintain **living document** updated through Management of Change (MOC). | 0.5 |

**SDS ↔ MHDS Relationship:**

- **SDS** (Safety Data Sheet) is a *supplier-issued* standardized document mandated under GHS / REACH / OSHA HazCom, containing 16 sections (identification, hazards, composition, first-aid, fire-fighting, accidental release, handling, exposure controls, properties, stability, toxicology, ecology, disposal, transport, regulatory, other).
- **MHDS** (Material Hazard Data Sheet) is a *plant-specific, internal* consolidation that **distills only the safety-relevant parameters** from the SDS into a single-page (or single-table) record, customized for the plant's operating conditions, quantities, and storage.

> **In simple terms:** The **SDS is the manufacturer's comprehensive brochure; the MHDS is the plant operator's customized quick-reference card.**

#### Part (b) — Numerical Problem [7 marks]

**Given:** $P^{sat}_{25°C} = 30.6$ kPa = 30,600 Pa; $M = 58$ g/mol = 0.058 kg/mol; $T = 298$ K; $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K); $LEL = 2.5\%$ vol; $UEL = 12.8\%$ vol; $m = 5000$ kg; $\Delta H_c = 29$ MJ/kg; $P_{atm} = 101.325$ kPa.

**(i) Vapor concentration in headspace (% vol):**

For a pure component in equilibrium with its vapor in a closed headspace, the mole fraction of vapor in the gas phase equals the ratio of saturation pressure to total pressure:

$$ y_{acetone} = \dfrac{P^{sat}}{P_{total}} = \dfrac{30.6 \text{ kPa}}{101.325 \text{ kPa}} $$

$$ y_{acetone} = 0.3021 \;\; \text{(i.e., 30.21\% vol)} $$

**Comparison to LEL & UEL:**

$$ 30.21\% \; \text{vol} \; > \; UEL = 12.8\% \text{ vol} $$

**Result:** The headspace is **ABOVE the UEL** — *too rich to ignite* under these closed, saturated conditions. However, as soon as the tank is opened or ventilated, the concentration will drop rapidly through the explosive envelope (12.8% → 2.5%), creating a transient **flash-back hazard** during transfer, sampling, or maintenance.

**[3 marks for (i)]**

**(ii) Total fire energy:**

$$ Q_{fire} = m \cdot \Delta H_c = 5000 \;\text{kg} \times 29 \;\text{MJ/kg} $$

$$ Q_{fire} = 145{,}000 \;\text{MJ} = 1.45 \times 10^5 \;\text{MJ} $$

**Classification (assuming a typical tank farm area of 200 m²):**
$$ q_{fire} = \dfrac{145{,}000}{200} = 725 \;\text{MJ/m}^2 \;\; \Rightarrow \;\; \text{Moderate fire load} $$

**Result:** A 5-tonne acetone inventory releases **145,000 MJ** upon complete combustion — equivalent to the energy of roughly **3.4 tonnes of TNT** (since 1 tonne TNT ≈ 4,184 MJ). This mandates **fixed foam pourers, water spray, and bund-wall containment** per NFPA 11 / IS 3037.

**[4 marks for (ii)]**

---

> ### **Question B** `[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme]`
>
> **(a) [7 marks]** Describe the **incompatibility matrix**. With a suitable example, explain how storage of incompatible chemicals led to a major industrial disaster, citing the **Bhopal 1984 (Union Carbide)** incident. Discuss the lessons learned for inventory analysis.
>
> **(b) [7 marks]** Two solvents, **Toluene (TLV = 50 ppm)** and **Xylene (TLV = 100 ppm)**, are simultaneously present in a workroom. The measured airborne concentrations are $C_{tol} = 30$ ppm and $C_{xyl} = 60$ ppm. Both compounds have **additive narcotic effects** on the central nervous system.
> Apply the **additive TLV formula** to determine whether the exposure is acceptable. Recommend **two engineering controls** if the result is non-compliant.

### Model Solution — Question B

#### Part (a) — Incompatibility Matrix and Bhopal Disaster [7 marks]

**Incompatibility Matrix (2 marks):** A square table where each row and each column represents a chemical present in the inventory. The cell at row $i$, column $j$ indicates whether material $i$ is **chemically compatible** with material $j$ under specified storage conditions (T, P, moisture, light). Incompatibility categories include: water-reactive, oxidizer-fuel, acid-base, pyrophoric-air, and self-polymerizing.

**Bhopal 1984 — Case Summary (3 marks):**
- **Plant:** Union Carbide India Limited, Bhopal, Madhya Pradesh
- **Chemical:** Methyl Isocyanate (MIC), UN 2480, CAS 624-83-9, $LD_{50} \approx 5$ mg/kg (rat oral)
- **Capacity:** 41 tonnes in storage tanks 610, 611, and E-610
- **Sequence of failures:**
  1. Water ingress into MIC storage tank 610 (slip-blind left open / flange leak)
  2. MIC + H₂O → exothermic reaction → heat + CO₂ + toxic gases
  3. Temperature runaway → 200 °C+ → pressure relief valve opened
  4. ~40 tonnes of MIC vapor released over 1–2 hours over the city
  5. **Deaths: ≥ 3,800 immediate, 15,000+ total over time; 500,000+ exposed**

**Incompatibility Lesson:** The fundamental failure was an **unevaluated incompatibility** — MIC was stored adjacent to water lines and steam tracing without proper segregation. A thorough inventory analysis would have flagged:
- Water-reactiveness of MIC (releases HCN, CO, H₂ at elevated T)
- Failure to provide adequate scrubbing (NaOH vent scrubber was off for maintenance)
- Failure of refrigeration system

**Lessons for Inventory Analysis (2 marks):**
1. **Segregate incompatible materials** by distance, dikes, or physical barriers
2. **Maintain the inventory list as a living document** — every change must be risk-assessed
3. **Couple inventory with engineered controls** — relief, scrubbing, containment
4. **Include human and procedural factors** in the inventory review

#### Part (b) — Additive TLV Calculation [7 marks]

**Given:**
- Toluene: $C_{tol} = 30$ ppm, $TLV_{tol} = 50$ ppm
- Xylene: $C_{xyl} = 60$ ppm, $TLV_{xyl} = 100$ ppm
- Additive narcotic effect (CNS depression)

**Apply the additive mixture formula:**
$$ E_{mix} = \dfrac{C_{tol}}{TLV_{tol}} + \dfrac{C_{xyl}}{TLV_{xyl}} $$

$$ E_{mix} = \dfrac{30}{50} + \dfrac{60}{100} $$

$$ E_{mix} = 0.60 + 0.60 = 1.20 $$

**Decision:**
$$ E_{mix} = 1.20 > 1.0 $$

**Result: NON-COMPLIANT.** The combined exposure **exceeds the acceptable threshold by 20%** because the two solvents act on the same target organ (CNS).

**Recommended Engineering Controls (2 marks — any 2 of the following):**
1. **Local Exhaust Ventilation (LEV)** — capture vapors at point of release (e.g., fume hoods, slot hoods above open tanks)
2. **Process Enclosure / Closed System** — replace open handling with sealed transfer lines and pumps
3. **General Dilution Ventilation** — increase room air changes per hour (ACH ≥ 6 for solvent rooms)
4. **Substitution (least preferred for short-term fix)** — replace toluene with a less hazardous solvent if feasible
5. **Air Monitoring System with Alarms** — continuous photoionization detector (PID) with setpoint at 0.5 × TLV

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Inventory Analysis Questions (KTU 2024 Scheme)**
>
> 1. **Confusing SDS with MSDS / MHDS.** Students interchange these terms randomly. **SDS** = supplier-issued GHS document; **MSDS** = older term for the same; **MHDS** = plant-internal, customized quick-reference.
> 2. **Forgetting units in vapor-concentration problems.** Always convert $P^{sat}$ to **Pa**, $M$ to **kg/mol**, $T$ to **K**. The result of $\dfrac{P \cdot M}{R \cdot T}$ is in **kg/m³** (mass concentration), not % vol.
> 3. **Stating the wrong explosive range interpretation.** A concentration *above UEL* is **not safe**; it only means *not ignitable at that moment*. As soon as ventilation dilutes it into the LEL–UEL band, ignition can occur.
> 4. **Skipping the "living document" aspect.** Inventory Analysis is not a one-time study. Examiners reward students who mention **Management of Change (MOC)** and periodic re-validation.
> 5. **Ignoring TQ (Threshold Quantity) triggers.** Under OSHA PSM and Indian Factories Act (Section 41B), exceeding TQ for listed chemicals **mandates** a Safety Audit and on-site Emergency Plan.
> 6. **Incompatibility Matrix must include all six categories** (water, acid, base, oxidizer, pyrophoric, polymerizable). Listing fewer is considered incomplete.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Inventory Analysis**

- **Definition:** Systematic compilation of chemicals, properties, and storage conditions in a plant — the *bedrock* of all hazard studies.
- **Position:** Module 4 → Preliminary Hazard Identification → **qualitative, descriptive, non-quantitative**.
- **6 Pillars of Data:** Identification, Physical, Reactivity, Toxicological, Fire/Explosion, Process/Storage.
- **16-Section SDS (GHS-aligned)** is the primary data source; **MHDS** is the plant-internal quick card.
- **Key parameters to memorize:**
  - $T_f$ (Flash Point), $T_{ait}$ (Auto-Ignition), LEL, UEL, LD₅₀, LC₅₀, TLV, PEL, IDLH
- **Quantitative screens:**
  - Vapor concentration: $C_{vap} = \dfrac{P_v M}{RT}$
  - Fire load: $Q_{fire} = m \cdot \Delta H_c$
  - Additive TLV: $E_{mix} = \sum C_i / TLV_i$
  - Explosive range position: $ER_{pos} = (C_{vap} - LEL)/(UEL - LEL)$
- **Incompatibility Matrix** is a mandatory deliverable — Bhopal 1984 is the canonical failure case.
- **Regulatory triggers:** OSHA PSM (29 CFR 1910.119), EPA RMP, Indian Factories Act §41B, IS 15484, NFPA 704.
- **NFPA 704 Diamond** — 4 quadrants: Blue (Health), Red (Flammability), Yellow (Reactivity), White (Special).
- **Threshold Quantity (TQ)** — exceedance mandates full PSM coverage and HAZOP.
- **Living document principle** — Inventory Analysis must be updated through **Management of Change (MOC)**.
- **Hierarchical flow:** Inventory Analysis → PHA → What-If → HAZOP → FMEA / FTA → QRA.
- **Cannot estimate accident frequency** — purely qualitative; quantitative work begins with FMEA/FTA/QRA.
- **Limitations:** data-quality dependent; static snapshot; misses procedural/human-factor hazards.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fire and Explosion Hazard Rating of Process Plants; Dow Fire and Explosion Hazard Index
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAUT415 - INDUSTRIAL SAFETY ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Fire and Explosion Hazard Rating of Process Plants; Dow Fire and Explosion Hazard Index

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Fire and Explosion Hazard Rating** is a systematic, semi-quantitative analytical procedure used in process industries to evaluate the magnitude of fire, explosion, and reactive chemical hazards associated with process units handling flammable, combustible, or reactive materials. It converts qualitative process knowledge into numerical indices that allow ranking, prioritization, and engineering judgment for loss prevention.

The most widely accepted industrial rating system is the **Dow Fire and Explosion Hazard Index (F&EI)**, originally developed by the Dow Chemical Company in 1964 and subsequently revised (2nd edition 1976, 3rd edition 1980, 4th edition 1991, and the Guideword-based 5th/6th editions of 1994 and 1998) under the stewardship of the American Institute of Chemical Engineers (AIChE) through its Center for Chemical Process Safety (CCPS).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Equivalent):**
> *The Dow Fire and Explosion Hazard Index is a rational, step-wise procedure that quantifies the realistic fire, explosion, and reactivity potential of process equipment using a Material Factor multiplied by General and Special Process Hazard penalties, producing a numerical index used for risk prioritization and insurance/loss-control planning.*

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are insuring **1,000 houses** in a city. You obviously cannot give the same premium to:
- A wooden hut in a forest (high fire risk)
- A concrete apartment in a dry urban zone (moderate risk)
- A concrete bungalow in a gated, fire-station-adjacent society (low risk)

You need a **single number that ranks each house by how badly a fire could damage it** — considering the material (wood/concrete), the surroundings (forest/urban), the safety systems (sprinklers/ fire extinguishers), and the contents (gas cylinders/sofas).

The **Dow F&EI is exactly that "fire risk score" for a chemical process unit.** It looks at:
- **What is inside** (Material Factor),
- **What makes the situation worse** (General + Special Process Hazards),
- **What makes it better** (Loss Control Credit Factors),

and spits out a single number — the **F&EI** — that tells safety engineers *"this reactor is far more dangerous than that storage tank, and here is the dollar/radius value of potential damage."*

### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

The following constants and standard units are universally used in the Dow methodology:

| Parameter | Symbol | Standard Value / Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Universal Gas Constant | $R$ | **8.314 J/(mol·K)** |
| Atmospheric Pressure | $P_{atm}$ | **101.325 kPa** |
| Material Factor | $MF$ | Dimensionless (0–40, tabulated) |
| F&EI | $F\&EI$ | Dimensionless (1–200+) |
| Radius of Exposure | $R$ | **meters (m)** or **feet (ft)** |
| Area of Exposure | $A$ | **m²** or **ft²** |
| Damage Factor | $DF$ | Dimensionless (0.00–1.00) |
| MPPD | $MPPD$ | Currency (USD / INR) |

> [!NOTE]
> The classic empirical relation between F&EI and the Radius of Exposure (developed from incident-history regression) is:
> $$R = 0.256 \times F\&EI \quad \text{(in feet)}$$
> In SI units, $R(\text{m}) \approx 0.256 \times F\&EI \times 0.3048$, or approximately $0.08 \times F\&EI$ in meters depending on the edition used.
