## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Philosophy Behind the Dow Index

The Dow methodology rests on three engineering observations:

1. **A chemical is intrinsically dangerous or it is not** — quantified by the **Material Factor (MF)**, derived from the chemical's **NFPA 704** flammability (N\_F) and reactivity (N\_R) ratings:
$$MF = f(N_F, N_R)$$

2. **The way a chemical is processed multiplies its danger** — captured by **General Process Hazards (GPH)** and **Special Process Hazards (SPH)**, which are dimensionless *penalty factors* always $\geq 1.00$.

3. **Engineered safety layers reduce consequence** — represented by **Loss Control Credit Factors (LCCF)**, each $\leq 1.00$, that discount the loss estimate.

### 2.2 Step-Wise Logic Flow

The complete F&EI evaluation proceeds in **7 logical steps**:

- **Step 1 — Identify Process Unit:** Select the unit (reactor, distillation column, storage tank, dryer) whose loss potential is the highest.
- **Step 2 — Determine Material Factor (MF):** Read MF from tabulated values based on $N_F$ and $N_R$ (and vapor pressure for hydrocarbons).
- **Step 3 — List General Process Hazards:** Apply penalty factors to MF for general operational conditions (reactions, handling, access, drainage).
- **Step 4 — List Special Process Hazards:** Apply penalty factors for unit-specific high-risk conditions (toxicity, pressure, quantity, corrosion, leakage).
- **Step 5 — Calculate Process Unit Hazard Factor (PUHF) and F&EI:**
$$PUHF = GPH \times SPH$$
$$F\&EI = MF \times GPH \times SPH = MF \times PUHF$$

- **Step 6 — Damage Factor and MPPD:** Use the F&EI to read a **Damage Factor (DF)** from a standardized chart, then compute Maximum Probable Property Damage:
$$MPPD = \text{Replacement Value of Area of Exposure} \times DF$$

- **Step 7 — Apply Loss Control Credits:** Adjust MPPD and compute Business Interruption (BI) loss using LCCFs.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **minimum set of equations** a student must memorize for the KTU End Semester Examination (ESE).

| # | Formula | Description | Unit / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $F\&EI = MF \times GPH \times SPH$ | Master hazard index equation | Dimensionless |
| 2 | $PUHF = GPH \times SPH$ | Process Unit Hazard Factor | $\geq 1.00$ |
| 3 | $R = 0.256 \times F\&EI$ | Radius of Exposure (US units) | feet |
| 4 | $A = \pi R^{2}$ | Area of Exposure | ft² or m² |
| 5 | $DF = f(F\&EI)$ | Damage Factor (from chart) | 0.00 – 1.00 |
| 6 | $MPPD = V_{RA} \times DF$ | Maximum Probable Property Damage | Currency |
| 7 | $V_{RA} = A \times C_{unit}$ | Value of Area of Exposure | Currency |
| 8 | $MPPD_{adj} = MPPD \times LCCF_{process\ control} \times LCCF_{material\ isolation} \times \dots$ | Adjusted MPPD after loss control | Currency |
| 9 | $BI = f(MPPD_{adj},\ downtime)$ | Business Interruption loss | Currency / month |
| 10 | $MF = \frac{N_F + N_R}{2}\ (\text{simplified})$ | Approximate Material Factor (illustrative only) | Dimensionless |

> [!NOTE]
> **Caution on Table Usage:** Use `\vert` for absolute values to avoid breaking the markdown table. The Damage Factor equation $DF = f(F\&EI)$ is **graphical, not algebraic** — it must be read from the Dow F&EI nomograph (4th/5th edition) at the KTU lab exam.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Insurance Underwriting:** The F&EI is the de-facto standard for Property All-Risk (PAR) insurance in hydrocarbon, petrochemical, and pharmaceutical plants.
- **Plant Layout Decisions:** The Radius of Exposure is used as a **spacing criterion** between process units (API RP 752 / 753 / 753A) to prevent domino effects.
- **Regulatory Compliance:** OSHA PSM 29 CFR 1910.119 and Indian Factories Act (Section 41A, MSIHC Rules 1989) implicitly require quantitative hazard analysis.
- **Loss Prevention Auditing:** Setters like ICI, Mond, Dow Jones, and CEI (Chemical Exposure Index) evolved directly from this concept.

### 2.5 Comparison with Modern Indices

| Index | Year | Focus |
|---|---|---|
| Dow F&EI | 1964 → 1998 | Fire & Explosion severity |
| Mond Index | 1979 (Imperial Chemical Industries) | Adds toxicity and health |
| CEI (Chemical Exposure Index) | 1994 (Dow) | Toxic airborne release |
| FRED (Fire, Release, Explosion, Dispersion) | 1990s | Consequence-based integrated |
| Dow Jones Industrial Average | (unrelated) | Stock market – do not confuse! |

## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Master F&EI Equation from First Principles

The Dow F&EI is constructed under the principle that **the total process-unit hazard is the product of an intrinsic material factor and the cumulative process-condition penalties.** This is not derived from a single physical law but from **empirical multiplicative superposition** of dimensionless penalty factors.

$$\text{Hazard}_{\text{total}} = \text{Hazard}_{\text{intrinsic}} \times \sum_{i}\text{Penalty}_i$$

Since penalties are multiplicative (not additive) to capture the **synergistic amplification** of multiple simultaneous hazards (e.g., high pressure *and* toxic release *and* corrosion):

$$F\&EI = MF \times \left(1 + \sum_{i=1}^{n_G} g_i\right) \times \left(1 + \sum_{j=1}^{n_S} s_j\right)$$

where $g_i$ are the **GPH penalty increments** and $s_j$ are the **SPH penalty increments**, with the "1 +" term ensuring the baseline $\geq 1.00$. KTU accepted simplification:

$$\boxed{F\&EI = MF \times GPH \times SPH}$$

**Worked Numerical Example (KTU-style):**

> A storage sphere contains pressurized LPG with $MF = 21$. The unit is outdoors but in a congested area, has medium inventories, operates at 12 bar above ambient, and uses a pressure-relief valve set at 15 bar. Calculate F&EI.

| Factor | Value | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| $MF$ | 21 | LPG (NFPA: F=4, R=0) |
| $GPH$ | 1.40 | Outdoor (0) + access (0.35) + drainage (0.5) + handling (0.55) |
| $SPH$ | 2.20 | Toxic (0) + pressure 12 bar (1.20) + quantity LPG (0.50) + corrosion (0.20) + leak (0.30) |

$$F\&EI = 21 \times 1.40 \times 2.20 = 64.68 \approx 65$$

Interpretation: This unit falls in the **"Heavy"** hazard category (F&EI = 61–96).

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Radius of Exposure from Energy-Yield Regression

The empirical formula $R = 0.256 \times F\&EI$ (in feet) was developed by **The Dow Chemical Company** in 1976 by correlating historical incident data:

- 17 major hydrocarbon fires and explosions
- Yielding a least-squares regression of radius (in feet) vs. damage in USD/lb vs. mass released

The underlying logic: the area of exposure is approximated as the **circular footprint within which thermal radiation from a pool fire or overpressure from a vapor cloud explosion would cause significant secondary damage**.

$$R \propto \sqrt{E_{\text{released}}}$$
$$\text{Property Damage} \propto A = \pi R^2 \propto E_{\text{released}}$$

Since $F\&EI$ itself is proportional to $E_{\text{released}}$ through MF (heat of combustion) and SPH (quantity term $Q$):

$$R^2 \propto E_{\text{released}} \propto F\&EI \implies R = k \sqrt{F\&EI} \approx 0.256 \cdot F\&EI$$

Because regression of actual incident data gave a **near-linear** fit (not square-root), the constant 0.256 is empirical:

$$\boxed{R = 0.256 \times F\&EI \quad \text{[feet]}}$$

**SI conversion:**
$$R \,(\text{m}) = 0.256 \times F\&EI \times 0.3048 \approx 0.0780 \times F\&EI$$

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Damage Factor (DF) Functional Form

The Damage Factor is the **fraction of the exposed area expected to be destroyed**. It is read from a tabulated chart (Dow F&EI 4th edition, Table 4-1):

| F&EI Range | DF Range | Severity Degree |
|---|---|---|
| 1–50 | 0.00–0.10 | Light |
| 51–81 | 0.10–0.30 | Moderate |
| 82–95 | 0.30–0.50 | Heavy |
| 96–127 | 0.50–0.70 | Very Heavy |
| $\geq 128$ | 0.70–1.00 | Extreme |

An empirical fit (illustrative, not for KTU):
$$DF \approx 0.003906 \times (F\&EI - 1)^{1.789}$$

### 3.4 Derivation 4: MPPD and Loss Control Adjustment

Maximum Probable Property Damage before any loss control credits:

$$MPPD_0 = V_{RA} \times DF$$

where $V_{RA} = A \times c_{\text{unit}} = \pi R^2 \times c_{\text{unit}}$ and $c_{\text{unit}}$ is the **replacement cost per unit area** (USD/ft² or INR/m²).

After applying Loss Control Credit Factors (each ≤ 1.00, multiplied together):

$$MPPD_{adj} = MPPD_0 \times LCCF_{\text{process control}} \times LCCF_{\text{material isolation}} \times LCCF_{\text{fire protection}}$$

**Business Interruption (BI):**
$$BI = MPFF_{adj} \times \frac{\text{Outage (months)}}{12} \times \text{VP\%} \times \frac{1}{1 - \text{Tax\%}}$$

where $MPFF_{adj}$ = Material-on-Hand + Value-of-Production (adjusted); $VP\%$ = Variable Profit percentage.

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix: GPH and SPH Penalty Categories

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following matrices are the **core content** for KTU 14-mark questions. Reproduce the structure, do not memorize the entire numerical value list.

**General Process Hazards (GPH):**

| # | Category | Penalty Range |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Exothermic chemical reactions | 0.30 – 1.25 |
| 2 | Endothermic reactions | 0.20 – 0.40 |
| 3 | Material handling / transfer | 0.25 – 1.05 |
| 4 | Enclosed or indoor process units | 0.25 – 0.90 |
| 5 | Access of emergency equipment | 0.20 – 0.35 |
| 6 | Drainage / spill control | 0.25 – 0.50 |
| | **GPH Total** | $\geq 1.00$ |

**Special Process Hazards (SPH):**

| # | Category | Penalty Range |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Toxic materials | 0.20 – 0.80 |
| 2 | Sub-atmospheric pressure (< 500 mmHg) | 0.50 |
| 3 | Operation near flammable range (storage, blending) | 0.50 – 1.50 |
| 4 | Dust explosion potential | 0.25 – 2.00 |
| 5 | Pressure above atmospheric | 0.10 – 1.80 |
| 6 | Low temperature (embrittlement) | 0.20 – 0.30 |
| 7 | Quantity of flammable/unstable material | 0.10 – 3.00 |
| 8 | Corrosion / erosion | 0.10 – 0.75 |
| 9 | Joints / packing leakage | 0.10 – 1.50 |
| 10 | Fired heaters | 0.10 – 0.40 |
| 11 | Hot oil heat exchange | 0.15 – 0.50 |
| 12 | Rotating equipment | 0.10 – 0.30 |
| | **SPH Total** | $\geq 1.00$ |

## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the Dow F&EI Methodology

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[START: Identify Process Unit] --> B[Lookup Material Factor MF from NFPA table]
    B --> C{Is MF known?}
    C -- No --> D[Use Heat of Combustion and Reactivity to estimate MF]
    C -- Yes --> E[Compute GPH from 6 general categories]
    D --> E
    E --> F[Compute SPH from 12 special categories]
    F --> G[PUHF = GPH x SPH]
    G --> H[F&EI = MF x PUHF]
    H --> I{F&EI less than 128?}
    I -- Yes --> J[Read DF from Dow F&EI nomograph]
    I -- No --> K[Severe hazard: cap DF at 1.00]
    J --> L[Compute Radius of Exposure R]
    K --> L
    L --> M[Compute Area of Exposure A]
    M --> N[Compute Replacement Value of Area VRA]
    N --> O[Compute MPPD0 = VRA x DF]
    O --> P[Apply Loss Control Credit Factors]
    P --> Q[Compute Adjusted MPPD and BI]
    Q --> R[Final Output: Hazard Ranking and Loss Estimate]
```

## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. Define Material Factor (MF) and explain its role in the Dow Fire and Explosion Hazard Index.** `[KTU University Exam – May 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Material Factor (MF)** is a measure of the intrinsic energy release potential of a chemical substance under uncontrolled conditions. It is determined from the chemical's **NFPA 704 flammability rating ($N_F$) and reactivity rating ($N_R$)** — sometimes supplemented by vapor pressure for hydrocarbons.

In the Dow F&EI framework, the MF acts as the **multiplicative seed** of the entire index:

$$F\&EI = MF \times GPH \times SPH$$

The MF therefore fixes the **starting hazard level** before any process-specific penalties are applied. Chemicals with high flammability and high reactivity (e.g., acetylene: $N_F=4, N_R=4$) have $MF = 40$ (the maximum), while inert materials like water have $MF = 0$. It is a *dimensionless* index ranging from **0 to 40**.

*Allocation:* 1 mark definition, 1 mark NFPA basis, 1 mark role in F&EI equation.

---

**Q2. List any six categories of Special Process Hazards (SPH) used in the Dow F&EI method.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The six categories of **Special Process Hazards (SPH)** are:

1. Toxic materials (penalty 0.20 – 0.80)
2. Sub-atmospheric pressure operation (penalty 0.50)
3. Operation in or near the flammable range (penalty 0.50 – 1.50)
4. Dust explosion potential (penalty 0.25 – 2.00)
5. Pressure above atmospheric release pressure (penalty 0.10 – 1.80)
6. Quantity of flammable/unstable material present (penalty 0.10 – 3.00)

*(Other acceptable SPH categories: corrosion/erosion, joints/packing leakage, fired heaters, hot oil heat exchange, low temperature brittleness, rotating equipment.)*

---

### Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

---

**Q3(A). With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the step-by-step procedure for evaluating the Dow Fire and Explosion Hazard Index for a process plant. Also derive the relationship between F&EI and the Radius of Exposure.** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Step-by-Step Procedure (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Identify the Process Unit:**
Select the process unit (reactor, distillation column, storage tank, dryer, compressor, etc.) that presents the greatest loss potential. Plant is divided into logical units, and the most hazardous is chosen.

**Step 2 — Determine Material Factor (MF):**
The MF is obtained from standard Dow tables, indexed by NFPA 704 ratings:
- For a typical hydrocarbon (NFPA F=3, R=0): $MF \approx 16$
- For highly reactive materials (NFPA F=4, R=4): $MF = 40$ (acetylene)

**Step 3 — Calculate General Process Hazards (GPH):**
Apply penalties for:
1. Exothermic chemical reactions
2. Endothermic reactions
3. Material handling and transfer
4. Enclosed/indoor process units
5. Access of emergency equipment
6. Drainage and spill control

$$GPH = 1.0 + \sum_{i=1}^{6} g_i \quad \text{(typically } 1.0 \text{ to } 8.0\text{)}$$

**Step 4 — Calculate Special Process Hazards (SPH):**
Apply 12 specific penalties including toxicity, sub-atmospheric pressure, dust explosion, overpressure, low temperature, **quantity of flammable material**, corrosion, leakage, fired heaters, hot oil systems, rotating equipment.

**Step 5 — Calculate Process Unit Hazard Factor and F&EI:**

$$PUHF = GPH \times SPH$$
$$F\&EI = MF \times GPH \times SPH$$

**Step 6 — Determine Damage Factor (DF):**
Read DF from the Dow nomograph (Table 4-1 of F&EI 4th edition) as a function of F&EI. Example: F&EI = 65 gives $DF \approx 0.18$.

**Step 7 — Compute Consequence Quantities:**

$$R = 0.256 \times F\&EI \quad \text{(ft)}$$
$$A = \pi R^2 \quad \text{(ft}^2\text{)}$$
$$V_{RA} = A \times c_{\text{unit}} \quad \text{(USD)}$$
$$MPPD_0 = V_{RA} \times DF$$

**Step 8 — Apply Loss Control Credit Factors (LCCF):**

$$MPPD_{adj} = MPPD_0 \times LCCF_{\text{process control}} \times LCCF_{\text{material isolation}} \times LCCF_{\text{fire protection}}$$

**Step 9 — Estimate Business Interruption (BI):**
Use standard BI formulas incorporating outage time and variable profit margin.

**(b) Derivation of Radius of Exposure (7 marks):**

Starting from energy considerations: the area of exposure $A$ is the **circular footprint** within which thermal/overpressure effects cause significant secondary damage:

$$A = \pi R^2 \propto E_{\text{released}}$$

where $E_{\text{released}}$ is the total energy released in the worst credible event (pool fire, vapor cloud explosion, BLEVE). For a hydrocarbon, $E_{\text{released}}$ is proportional to the mass $m$ and heat of combustion $\Delta H_c$:

$$E_{\text{released}} = m \cdot \Delta H_c$$

The F&EI itself, by construction, is proportional to this energy through the Material Factor (heat content) and the SPH quantity term:

$$F\&EI \propto E_{\text{released}}$$

Substituting back:

$$\pi R^2 = k_1 \cdot F\&EI \implies R = \sqrt{\dfrac{k_1}{\pi}} \cdot \sqrt{F\&EI}$$

Empirical regression on Dow's incident database showed that a **near-linear fit** (not square-root) best represented the data, because of additional effects like fireball lift-off, blast wave coupling, and shielding. The least-squares regression yielded:

$$\sqrt{\dfrac{k_1}{\pi}} \approx 0.256$$

Hence, in **US customary units**:

$$\boxed{R = 0.256 \times F\&EI \quad \text{[feet]}}$$

In **SI units**, applying the conversion factor $1 \text{ ft} = 0.3048 \text{ m}$:

$$R = 0.256 \times 0.3048 \times F\&EI = 0.078 \times F\&EI \quad \text{[m]}$$

*Verification:* For an F&EI of 128 (extreme hazard), $R = 0.256 \times 128 = 32.8$ ft $\approx 10$ m, which is consistent with the **recommended spacing** of hydrocarbon process units in API RP 752.

---

**Q3(B). A chemical process unit handles acetone. The material factor for acetone is 16. The general process hazard factor is 1.40 and the special process hazard factor is 1.80. The replacement cost per unit area is ₹3,000/m². Calculate: (a) the Fire and Explosion Index, (b) the Radius of Exposure, (c) the Area of Exposure, (d) the Damage Factor (using F&EI = 65 → DF = 0.18), and (e) the Maximum Probable Property Damage (MPPD).** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- Material Factor, $MF = 16$ (acetone)
- General Process Hazard Factor, $GPH = 1.40$
- Special Process Hazard Factor, $SPH = 1.80$
- Replacement cost, $c_{\text{unit}} = \text{₹}3{,}000/\text{m}^2$
- Damage Factor, $DF = 0.18$ (read from Dow chart for F&EI = 65)

**(a) Fire and Explosion Index (2 marks):**

$$F\&EI = MF \times GPH \times SPH$$
$$F\&EI = 16 \times 1.40 \times 1.80$$
$$\boxed{F\&EI = 40.32 \approx 40}$$

**Interpretation:** F&EI = 40 falls in the **"Light"** hazard category (F&EI = 1–50).

**(b) Radius of Exposure (3 marks):**

Using the empirical Dow equation in SI units:

$$R = 0.078 \times F\&EI$$
$$R = 0.078 \times 40.32$$
$$\boxed{R = 3.145 \text{ m}}$$

(Equivalently, in feet: $R = 0.256 \times 40.32 = 10.32$ ft, and $10.32 \times 0.3048 = 3.14$ m — checks out.)

**(c) Area of Exposure (3 marks):**

$$A = \pi R^2$$
$$A = \pi \times (3.145)^2$$
$$A = \pi \times 9.89$$
$$\boxed{A = 31.07 \text{ m}^2}$$

**(d) Damage Factor (2 marks):** Given as $DF = 0.18$ (read from Dow F&EI nomograph at F&EI = 40.32, interpolated to ≈ 0.15–0.18).

**(e) Maximum Probable Property Damage (4 marks):**

**Step 1 — Replacement Value of Area of Exposure:**

$$V_{RA} = A \times c_{\text{unit}} = 31.07 \times 3{,}000$$
$$V_{RA} = \text{₹}93{,}210$$

**Step 2 — Apply Damage Factor:**

$$MPPD = V_{RA} \times DF = 93{,}210 \times 0.18$$
$$\boxed{MPPD = \text{₹}16{,}777.80}$$

**Step 3 — Apply Loss Control Credit Factors (illustrative, if LCCF = 0.85):**

$$MPPD_{adj} = 16{,}777.80 \times 0.85 = \text{₹}14{,}261$$

**Conclusion:** The unit has a light-to-moderate hazard rating; with adequate loss-control credit, the expected property damage is roughly **₹14,000–₹17,000** under the worst credible event.

*Mark split:* (a) 2, (b) 3, (c) 3, (d) 2, (e) 4 = 14 marks.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Do NOT confuse MF and F&EI.** MF is a property of the chemical alone; F&EI is a property of the *unit*. Writing "F&EI = 16" for acetone will get **zero marks** in a 14-mark question.
> 2. **Unit mismatch is the #1 reason for lost marks.** Always explicitly state whether $R$ is in feet or meters. KTU expects **SI units** unless specifically stated.
> 3. **Damage Factor is read from a chart, not computed algebraically.** Show the table reference (Dow F&EI 4th edition, Table 4-1).
> 4. **GPH and SPH are ≥ 1.00 by definition.** A value of $GPH = 0.95$ is physically meaningless and will be marked wrong.
> 5. **Forgetting to apply LCCFs** in Part B leads to an inflated MPPD and a deduction of 2–3 marks.
> 6. **Radius formula 0.256 × F&EI** is for feet; **0.078 × F&EI** is for meters. Marking scheme explicitly penalizes the wrong constant.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- ⭐ The Dow **F&EI = MF × GPH × SPH** is the master equation.
- ⭐ **MF** comes from NFPA 704 ratings; ranges from 0 to 40.
- ⭐ **GPH** has 6 categories; **SPH** has 12 categories. Both ≥ 1.00.
- ⭐ **Process Unit Hazard Factor (PUHF) = GPH × SPH**.
- ⭐ **Radius of Exposure:** $R = 0.256 \times F\&EI$ in **feet**; $R = 0.078 \times F\&EI$ in **meters**.
- ⭐ **Area of Exposure:** $A = \pi R^2$.
- ⭐ **Damage Factor (DF)** is read from the Dow nomograph (0.00 to 1.00).
- ⭐ **MPPD₀ = V_{RA} × DF**; **MPPD_{adj} = MPPD₀ × LCCFs**.
- ⭐ Hazard Severity Bands: **1–50 (Light)**, **51–81 (Moderate)**, **82–95 (Heavy)**, **96–127 (Very Heavy)**, **≥ 128 (Extreme)**.
- ⭐ The 5 Loss Control Credit categories: **Process Control, Material Isolation, Fire Protection** (and historically: training, maintenance).
- ⭐ The Dow index is **plant-layout dependent** (used for unit spacing in API RP 752) and **insurance-grade** (Property All-Risk underwriters use it globally).
- ⭐ The Mond Index and CEI are **modern extensions** that add toxicity and health dimensions.
- ⭐ The F&EI is **empirical**, not derived from first principles — always cite its origin in 1964 (Dow Chemical) and revisions (1976, 1980, 1991, 1994, 1998).
- ⭐ For KTU ESE: remember to **draw the procedural block diagram** in the 14-mark question; it carries 3–4 marks of the total.
- ⭐ For numerical questions: **show all 7 calculation steps** — examiners award partial credit per step.

> **End of Module 4 Notes — Fire and Explosion Hazard Rating of Process Plants; Dow F&EI**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Preliminary Hazard Analysis
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAUT415 - INDUSTRIAL SAFETY ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Preliminary Hazard Analysis

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Preliminary Hazard Analysis (PHA)** is a structured, inductive hazard identification and evaluation technique performed during the early conceptual or design phase of a system, facility, or process. It systematically identifies potential hazards, evaluates their causes, consequences, severity, and probability of occurrence, and recommends preliminary corrective actions or risk mitigation measures before detailed design commitments are made.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, PHA is classified as a **proactive (anticipatory) safety analysis tool** that operates on the principle of *"prevention is better than cure."* It is the first formal hazard analysis conducted in the safety lifecycle, typically after a process concept exists but before detailed engineering is finalized.

The technique was originally developed in **1972** by the **U.S. Army Materiel Command (AMC)** under the military standard **MIL-STD-882** for weapon systems safety. It has since been adopted across process industries, chemical plants, nuclear, aerospace, and construction sectors and is now standardized under international codes such as **IEC 31010:2019 (Risk Management — Risk Assessment Techniques)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Synonyms Used in KTU Exams:** Preliminary Hazard Analysis, PHA, Preliminary Hazard List (PHL), Conceptual Safety Review.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine you are planning a long road trip from Kerala to Delhi. Before packing the car, an experienced traveler sits down with a **notebook and pen** and lists everything that *could* go wrong on the journey:

- *What if the tyre bursts on the highway?* → **Hazard: Tyre blowout**
- *Why might it happen?* → **Cause: Worn-out tread, overloading, pothole impact**
- *What could the result be?* → **Consequence: Loss of vehicle control, collision**
- *How bad could it be?* → **Severity: Catastrophic (fatal risk)**
- *How likely is it?* → **Probability: Occasional**
- *What can we do about it now?* → **Mitigation: Tyre inspection before trip, carry spare, drive within speed limit**

That **mental exercise of writing down what could go wrong, why, how bad, and what to do** — done *before* the trip begins — is exactly what a **Preliminary Hazard Analysis** does for an engineering system. You are **pre-walking the danger paths** so that design changes can be made while they are still cheap to implement.

> [!NOTE]
> **The PHA Mental Model:** "Hazard → Cause → Consequence → Severity + Probability → Risk → Mitigation" — this chain must be completed for every hazardous element in a system.

## 1.3 When is PHA Performed? (Lifecycle Position)

PHA occupies the **front end** of the safety analysis hierarchy. The sequence in the KTU 2024 safety lifecycle is:

| Stage | Analysis Type | Phase |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Preliminary Hazard Analysis (PHA)** | Conceptual / Front-End Design |
| 2 | What-If Analysis | Preliminary Design |
| 3 | Checklist Analysis | Detailed Design |
| 4 | HAZOP / FMEA / FTA / ETA | Detailed Engineering |
| 5 | Quantitative Risk Assessment (QRA) | Operational |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Favourite Question Hook:** *"Why is PHA performed at the conceptual stage and not after construction?"* — The correct answer is that **design changes are cheapest before equipment is purchased and piping is welded**. A single piping reroute during design costs ₹50,000; the same change after commissioning costs ₹50,00,000 (rule of thumb **1:10:100 — 1× in design, 10× in construction, 100× in operation**).

## 1.4 Primary Objectives of PHA

1. **Identify** all credible hazards associated with the system early in its lifecycle.
2. **Evaluate** each hazard's potential consequences on personnel, equipment, environment, and mission.
3. **Determine** the initial risk level (combination of severity and probability).
4. **Recommend** preliminary risk reduction measures (inherent safety, engineering controls, PPE).
5. **Provide** a documented hazard database that feeds downstream analyses (HAZOP, FMEA, SIL).
6. **Establish** a basis for setting safety design criteria before detailed engineering.
7. **Identify** areas requiring more detailed analysis later in the project.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The PHA Methodology — Step-by-Step Logic

A KTU-compliant PHA study follows a **7-step structured procedure**. Each step has a defined input, process, and output.

### Step 1: Define the System Boundaries and Scope
- Identify the **physical boundaries** (battery limits, equipment items) of the system under review.
- Define the **operational envelope** (start-up, normal operation, shutdown, maintenance, emergency).
- Specify the **environmental conditions** (ambient temperature, humidity, altitude, seismic zone).
- Document the **interfaces** with neighbouring units, utilities, and control systems.
- **Output:** System description, P&ID reference list, operating philosophy.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters in KTU answers:** Examiners award marks for stating the boundaries explicitly. A PHA without defined boundaries is considered *invalid* because hazards can be mis-attributed to the wrong unit.

### Step 2: Identify Energy, Material, and Process Hazards
- Enumerate all **forms of energy** present: electrical, mechanical, hydraulic, pneumatic, chemical, thermal, radiation, gravitational (potential), kinetic.
- List all **hazardous materials**: flammables, toxics, reactives, asphyxiants, corrosives.
- Examine **process deviations**: high pressure, high temperature, overfill, dry-out, reverse flow, two-phase flow.
- Consider **external hazards**: natural (flood, earthquake, lightning), adjacent facility, vehicle impact, sabotage.

### Step 3: Determine the Causes of Each Hazard
- Use the mnemonic **"MOCEL"** (Material, Operator, Control, Equipment, Layout) as a brainstorming prompt.
- Apply **guideword-based reasoning**: *No, More, Less, As Well As, Part Of, Reverse, Other Than* (these are the same guidewords used in HAZOP but applied in a coarser, qualitative form during PHA).
- Reference historical incident databases: **eMARS (EU), eRFA (India), ICheme Safety Centre, OSHA, AICHE CCPS**.

### Step 4: Determine the Consequences (Effects)
- For each cause, trace the **chain of events** leading to the worst credible outcome.
- Categorise effects on:
  - **Personnel** (injury, fatality, occupational illness)
  - **Equipment** (damage, downtime, replacement cost)
  - **Environment** (release to air, water, soil)
  - **Mission / Production** (loss of output, contractual penalty)
  - **Public / Community** (off-site impact)

### Step 5: Assign Severity and Probability Categories
- Use the **standard PHA risk matrix** (see Section 2.2 below).
- Severity is rated on a **4-level scale** (Catastrophic, Critical, Marginal, Negligible) per MIL-STD-882.
- Probability is rated on a **5-level scale** (Frequent, Probable, Occasional, Remote, Improbable).

### Step 6: Evaluate the Risk and Decide on Action
- Compute the **Risk Index (RI)** as the product or matrix lookup of Severity × Probability.
- Apply the **As Low As Reasonably Practicable (ALARP)** principle.
- Decide action:
  - **Risk Level 1–2 (Low):** Accept with operating procedures.
  - **Risk Level 3 (Moderate):** Engineering control recommended.
  - **Risk Level 4–5 (High):** Design change mandatory; defer to detailed study (HAZOP / QRA).

### Step 7: Document the PHA Worksheet
- The output is a **PHA worksheet table** with the columns shown in Section 3.4.
- Each line is assigned a unique **PHA number (e.g., PHA-001, PHA-002)** for traceability.
- Recommendations are tracked into the **Action Tracking Register (ATR)** with responsibility and closure date.

## 2.2 The PHA Risk Matrix — KTU Standard (MIL-STD-882 Derived)

The risk index is the heart of PHA scoring. The KTU 2024 syllabus adopts the **5 × 4 matrix** below.

### 2.2.1 Severity Categories (S)

| Level | Category | Description (Personnel Impact) | Equipment / Environmental Impact |
|---|---|---|---|
| **IV** | **Catastrophic** | Fatality or permanent total disability | Loss of entire plant; > ₹10 Cr; irreversible environmental damage |
| **III** | **Critical** | Severe injury, hospitalisation > 30 days; occupational disease | Major equipment damage; ₹1–10 Cr; major release with remediation |
| **II** | **Marginal** | Minor injury, medical treatment, lost-time < 30 days | Moderate damage; ₹10 L–1 Cr; reportable release |
| **I** | **Negligible** | First aid or no injury | Minor damage; < ₹10 L; release contained on-site |

### 2.2.2 Probability Categories (P)

| Level | Category | Qualitative Description | Quantitative Range (per year) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **A** | **Frequent** | Likely to occur repeatedly | $P > 10^{-1}$ |
| **B** | **Probable** | Will occur several times in plant life | $10^{-2} < P \le 10^{-1}$ |
| **C** | **Occasional** | Likely to occur sometime in plant life | $10^{-3} < P \le 10^{-2}$ |
| **D** | **Remote** | Unlikely but possible | $10^{-4} < P \le 10^{-3}$ |
| **E** | **Improbable** | So unlikely it can be assumed it will not occur | $P \le 10^{-4}$ |

### 2.2.3 Risk Matrix (S × P)

| Severity ↓ \ Probability → | A (Frequent) | B (Probable) | C (Occasional) | D (Remote) | E (Improbable) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **IV Catastrophic** | 5 | 5 | 4 | 3 | 2 |
| **III Critical** | 5 | 4 | 3 | 2 | 1 |
| **II Marginal** | 4 | 3 | 2 | 1 | 1 |
| **I Negligible** | 3 | 2 | 1 | 1 | 1 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Rule — Risk Acceptance Levels:**
> - **Risk Index 1 (Low):** Accept; operate with existing controls.
> - **Risk Index 2 (Low-Moderate):** Accept with management notification; review periodically.
> - **Risk Index 3 (Moderate):** Corrective action recommended; ALARP demonstration.
> - **Risk Index 4 (High):** Corrective action mandatory before operation; escalate to detailed study.
> - **Risk Index 5 (Catastrophic):** Operation prohibited until risk reduced; redesign required.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following equations are the high-yield mathematical relations for PHA-based problems.

| # | Formula / Relation | Symbols & Units | Engineering Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $RI = S \times P$ (qualitative lookup) | $RI \in \{1,2,3,4,5\}$ | Risk Index from severity–probability matrix |
| 2 | $S \in \{I, II, III, IV\}$ | Dimensionless ordinal | Severity category |
| 3 | $P \in \{A, B, C, D, E\}$ | Frequency band | Probability category |
| 4 | $R_{residual} = R_{initial} \cdot (1 - E_{control})$ | $R$ = risk, $E_{control} \in [0,1]$ | Risk reduction after control layer |
| 5 | $R = f \times C$ | $f$ = frequency (yr$^{-1}$), $C$ = consequence (₹ or fatalities) | Quantitative risk (QRA basis) |
| 6 | $\text{ALARP} : \frac{dR}{dC_{mit}} = 1$ | Marginal cost = marginal benefit | Risk reduction no longer proportionate to effort |
| 7 | $\text{CCPS LOPA} : R = f_{init} \cdot PFD_{i}$ | $PFD$ = Probability of Failure on Demand | Layer of Protection Analysis (often requested alongside PHA) |
| 8 | $\text{1:10:100 Rule} : C_{design} : C_{construct} : C_{operate} = 1 : 10 : 100$ | Cost-of-change ratio | Justifies early PHA application |

> [!TIP]
> **Note on Markdown:** In the table above, the vertical pipe in set notation (e.g., $f \in [0,1]$) has been replaced with `\in` (set-membership) symbols to preserve table-rendering integrity. Use `\in` or `\mid` whenever a vertical bar is required inside a table cell.

## 2.4 Advantages and Limitations of PHA (High-Yield for KTU)

### 2.4.1 Advantages
- **Early identification** of major hazards before design freeze.
- **Low cost and time** compared to detailed HAZOP/QRA.
- **Effective team-building** tool — brings multi-discipline experts together.
- **Provides input** for downstream studies (HAZOP, SIL classification, emergency planning).
- **Documents** the safety basis for regulatory submissions (PESO, OISD, MoEFCC).
- **Flexible** — applicable to any system, any scale, any industry.

### 2.4.2 Limitations
- **Qualitative** — does not provide numerical risk values; cannot replace QRA.
- **Highly dependent on team experience** — junior teams may miss subtle hazards.
- **Coarse resolution** — cannot detect complex inter-node interactions (use HAZOP for that).
- **Does not address human reliability** in detail.
- **Static** — must be re-validated when the design or operating conditions change (Management of Change — MOC).
- **Cannot capture software / cyber-physical hazards** with same rigour as physical ones.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Mistake:** Students often confuse PHA with HAZOP. The differentiating points are:
> - PHA is **inductive (bottom-up: hazard → consequence)**; HAZOP is **deductive (top-down: process deviation → cause → consequence)**.
> - PHA uses **guidewords loosely**; HAZOP applies **strict guidewords to every node/intent deviation**.
> - PHA is performed at **conceptual stage**; HAZOP at **detailed engineering stage with P&IDs**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Risk Index Derivation — From Frequency × Consequence to the 5×4 Matrix

The qualitative 5×4 matrix is an **abstraction** of the quantitative relation:

$$
R = f \times C
$$

where $f$ is the **frequency of occurrence** (events per year) and $C$ is the **magnitude of consequence** (fatalities or monetary loss). Because PHA is performed before detailed design, neither $f$ nor $C$ is precisely known. The analyst therefore **bins** continuous variables into discrete categories.

### 3.1.1 Binning the Consequence Axis

Consider consequence measured in fatalities. The four severity bins are defined by threshold values $T_{1}, T_{2}, T_{3}, T_{4}$ such that:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Severity I (Negligible)} &: 0 < C \le T_{1} \\
\text{Severity II (Marginal)} &: T_{1} < C \le T_{2} \\
\text{Severity III (Critical)} &: T_{2} < C \le T_{3} \\
\text{Severity IV (Catastrophic)} &: C > T_{3}
\end{aligned}
$$

In a typical KTU problem, the standard thresholds are:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{1} &= 0.1 \;\; \text{(i.e., first aid, no lost time)} \\
T_{2} &= 1.0 \;\; \text{(i.e., single lost-time injury)} \\
T_{3} &= 3.0 \;\; \text{(i.e., multiple hospitalisations)} \\
T_{4} &\to \infty \;\; \text{(i.e., fatality or mass-casualty event)}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.1.2 Binning the Probability Axis

Using the **standard CCPS frequency bands** (per year), the bins are:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{A (Frequent)} &: f > 10^{-1} \;\; \text{(more than once per decade)} \\
\text{B (Probable)} &: 10^{-2} < f \le 10^{-1} \\
\text{C (Occasional)} &: 10^{-3} < f \le 10^{-2} \\
\text{D (Remote)} &: 10^{-4} < f \le 10^{-3} \\
\text{E (Improbable)} &: f \le 10^{-4}
\end{aligned}
$$

The width of each frequency band is **one decade** on a logarithmic scale — a geometric progression with ratio 10.

### 3.1.3 Deriving the Risk Index from $R = f \times C$

For a hazard in band **A** (Frequent, $f \approx 10^{-1}$) and band **IV** (Catastrophic, $C \approx 10$ fatalities expected), the expected risk is:

$$
R = f \times C = 10^{-1} \times 10 = 10^{0} = 1 \;\; \text{fatality per year}
$$

This is assigned the maximum **RI = 5**.

For band **E** (Improbable, $f \le 10^{-4}$) and band **I** (Negligible, $C \le 0.1$):

$$
R = f \times C = 10^{-4} \times 10^{-1} = 10^{-5} \;\; \text{fatalities per year}
$$

This is assigned **RI = 1**.

The matrix is therefore a **logarithmic projection** of continuous risk onto a 5-level qualitative scale, which is why each cell is not a simple product but a calibrated lookup informed by CCPS, MIL-STD-882E, and IEC 61511 guidance.

## 3.2 Worked Example 1 — Liquefied Petroleum Gas (LPG) Bullet Storage

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem (KTU Pattern):** A 100-tonne LPG bullet is to be installed at a new bottling plant. Carry out a PHA for the *bulk storage unit* and rank the top three hazards.

### 3.2.1 Step 1: System Definition

**System:** Vertical bullet, water capacity 100 m³, design pressure 17.2 bar g, design temperature −10 °C to +50 °C, LPG composition ~70 % C₃ / 30 % C₄.

**Boundaries:** Inlet pigtail from road tanker unloading → bullet → vapour return line → outlet pumps → filling shed.

**Operating modes:** Receipt, storage, transfer, maintenance, emergency.

### 3.2.2 Step 2 & 3: Hazard Identification and Cause Determination

A PHA team (Process, Mechanical, Instrument, Safety, Operations) brainstorms the following:

| PHA No. | Hazard | Cause | Consequence |
|---|---|---|---|
| PHA-001 | Vapour cloud explosion (VCE) | Bulletic overfill → liquid LPG release → flash → cloud | Mass-casualty on-site and off-site |
| PHA-002 | Fire impingement on bullet | Adjacent truck fire, no bund wall | BLEVE of bullet → missile ejection |
| PHA-003 | Cold burn to operator | Manual sampling of liquid LPG | Severe frostbite, eye damage |
| PHA-004 | Asphyxiation | Inert blanketing failure → N₂ release in confined shed | Loss of consciousness, fatality |
| PHA-005 | Pump seal leak | Mechanical wear | Flash fire, occupational injury |
| PHA-006 | Foundation failure | Improper soil compaction | Bullet tilt, pipe rupture |
| PHA-007 | Lightning strike | No lightning protection | Direct ignition of vapour cloud |

### 3.2.3 Step 4–6: Severity, Probability, Risk, and Action

Applying the 5×4 matrix to the seven identified hazards:

| PHA No. | Severity (S) | Probability (P) | Risk Index (RI) | Action |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| PHA-001 | IV (Catastrophic) | C (Occasional) | **4** | Install level interlocks (SIL-2), overfill protection, dyke wall |
| PHA-002 | IV (Catastrophic) | D (Remote) | **3** | Install bund wall, water deluge, thermal insulation |
| PHA-003 | III (Critical) | C (Occasional) | **3** | Provide PPE, replace manual sampling with in-line analyser |
| PHA-004 | IV (Catastrophic) | E (Improbable) | **2** | Oxygen monitor, ventilation, confined space entry procedure |
| PHA-005 | III (Critical) | C (Occasional) | **3** | Double mechanical seal, leak detection, planned replacement |
| PHA-006 | IV (Catastrophic) | E (Improbable) | **2** | Geotechnical survey, reinforced foundation, settlement monitor |
| PHA-007 | IV (Catastrophic) | E (Improbable) | **2** | Lightning arrestor as per IS/IEC 62305, earthing grid |

### 3.2.4 Step 7: Top Three Risks

After sorting by Risk Index:

1. **PHA-001 (RI = 4):** Vapour Cloud Explosion due to overfill — **highest priority for redesign**.
2. **PHA-002, PHA-003, PHA-005 (RI = 3):** Fire impingement, cold burn, pump seal leak — all require **engineering controls and procedural mitigation**.
3. **PHA-004, PHA-006, PHA-007 (RI = 2):** Asphyxiation, foundation failure, lightning — **acceptable with administrative controls**.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Trick:** When asked *"how to reduce the risk of PHA-001?"*, the answer must follow the **hierarchy of controls**: Elimination → Substitution → Engineering Control → Administrative → PPE. The strongest answer is **inherent safety** — reduce inventory below the threshold quantity specified in the **Static and Mobile Vessels (SMPV) Rules, 1981** or replace LPG with a less hazardous refrigerant.

## 3.3 Worked Example 2 — Quantitative Verification of a PHA Risk Index

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** A PHA classified a hazard as Severity III (Critical) and Probability C (Occasional). The estimated frequency of the initiating event is $f = 5 \times 10^{-3}$ per year, and the expected fatalities per event are 2. Compute the quantitative risk and verify whether the qualitative RI = 3 is consistent.

### 3.3.1 Solution

Expected fatalities per year:

$$
R = f \times C = (5 \times 10^{-3}) \times 2
$$

$$
R = 10 \times 10^{-3} = 1.0 \times 10^{-2} \;\; \text{fatalities per year}
$$

In KTU practice, the **Individual Risk (IR)** classification is:

| IR (fatalities/yr) | KTU/CCPS Risk Band | Equivalent RI |
|---|---|---|
| $R > 10^{-1}$ | Intolerable | 5 |
| $10^{-2} < R \le 10^{-1}$ | Undesirable | 4 |
| $10^{-4} < R \le 10^{-2}$ | Tolerable with controls (ALARP) | 3 |
| $10^{-6} < R \le 10^{-4}$ | Broadly acceptable | 2 |
| $R \le 10^{-6}$ | Negligible | 1 |

Since $R = 1.0 \times 10^{-2}$ fatalities per year, the quantitative risk falls in the **Tolerable with controls (ALARP)** band, corresponding to **RI = 3**. The qualitative classification in the PHA is therefore **consistent** with the quantitative calculation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Inference Rule:** If a question gives you $f$ and $C$ and asks you to verify the RI, always use the table above as your reference. Do not invent thresholds — the band edges ($10^{-1}, 10^{-2}, 10^{-4}, 10^{-6}$) are **non-negotiable** in any KTU answer.

## 3.4 The Standard PHA Worksheet Format

The PHA output is presented as a table with **seven mandatory columns** per KTU 2024 scheme:

| Column 1 | Column 2 | Column 3 | Column 4 | Column 5 | Column 6 | Column 7 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| PHA No. | Hazard | Cause | Consequence | Severity | Probability | Recommendation / Action |
| PHA-001 | Vapour Cloud Explosion | Overfill of bullet | Mass casualty | IV | C | Install SIL-2 level switch; relief valve; dyke wall |

A complete PHA report for a major project may run from **200 to 2,000** such rows. Each row is a **traceable hazard item** that can be referenced from HAZOP, FMEA, SIL classification, and emergency planning documents.

## 3.5 Relationship of PHA to Other Safety Studies

The PHA's output is the **input** to virtually every downstream study:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{PHA Hazards} &\rightarrow \text{HAZOP Nodes (for detailed deviation analysis)} \\
&\rightarrow \text{FMEA Items (for equipment failure modes)} \\
&\rightarrow \text{SIL Targets (for safety instrumented functions)} \\
&\rightarrow \text{QRA Scenarios (for quantitative risk to public)} \\
&\rightarrow \text{Operating Procedures (for human-task hazards)} \\
&\rightarrow \text{Emergency Plans (for credible major incidents)}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Takeaway:** The PHA is the *parent document* of the safety case. Without a PHA, a HAZOP has no starting list of hazards; without HAZOP, a SIL classification has no basis; without SIL classification, a Safety Instrumented System (SIS) cannot be designed. PHA is therefore the **keystone** of the entire safety engineering effort.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Compilation Safeguard Note

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All Mermaid node identifiers below are pure alphanumeric and prefixed with letters (e.g., `node1`, `phaseA`). Labels are kept as raw uppercase text without markdown bold or italics. No reserved keywords (`end`, `subgraph`, `graph`, `style`) are used as node IDs.

## 4.2 Diagram 1 — The PHA Procedure Flow (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
graph TD
    A1[STEP 1: DEFINE SYSTEM BOUNDARIES] --> B1[STEP 2: IDENTIFY HAZARDS ENERGY MATERIAL PROCESS]
    B1 --> C1[STEP 3: DETERMINE CAUSES USING MOCEL AND GUIDEWORDS]
    C1 --> D1[STEP 4: ASSESS CONSEQUENCES PERSONNEL EQUIPMENT ENVIRONMENT]
    D1 --> E1[STEP 5: ASSIGN SEVERITY S I II III IV]
    D1 --> F1[STEP 6: ASSIGN PROBABILITY P A B C D E]
    E1 --> G1[STEP 7: RISK MATRIX LOOKUP RISK INDEX 1 TO 5]
    F1 --> G1
    G1 --> H1{IS RISK ACCEPTABLE}
    H1 -- YES --> I1[DOCUMENT IN PHA WORKSHEET CLOSE ITEM]
    H1 -- NO --> J1[RECOMMEND MITIGATION INHERENT ENGINEERING ADMIN PPE]
    J1 --> K1[FEED TO DETAILED STUDY HAZOP FMEA QRA]
    K1 --> L1[UPDATE ACTION TRACKING REGISTER]
    I1 --> M1[FINAL PHA REPORT]
    L1 --> M1
    M1 --> N1[RE VALIDATION ON MOC OR DESIGN CHANGE]
```

## 4.3 Diagram 2 — Position of PHA in the Safety Lifecycle

```mermaid
graph LR
    P1[CONCEPTUAL STAGE] --> P2[PHA PRELIMINARY HAZARD ANALYSIS]
    P2 --> P3[PRELIMINARY DESIGN]
    P3 --> P4[WHAT IF ANALYSIS]
    P4 --> P5[CHECKLIST REVIEW]
    P5 --> P6[DETAILED DESIGN STAGE]
    P6 --> P7[HAZOP HAZARD AND OPERABILITY]
    P6 --> P8[FMEA FAILURE MODE EFFECTS ANALYSIS]
    P6 --> P9[FTA FAULT TREE ANALYSIS]
    P6 --> P10[ETA EVENT TREE ANALYSIS]
    P7 --> P11[OPERATIONAL STAGE]
    P8 --> P11
    P9 --> P11
    P10 --> P12[QRA QUANTITATIVE RISK ASSESSMENT]
    P12 --> P11
    P11 --> P13[MANAGEMENT OF CHANGE TRIGGERS RE PHA]
    P13 --> P2
```

## 4.4 Diagram 3 — Risk Index Decision Tree

```mermaid
graph TD
    R1[HAZARD IDENTIFIED] --> R2[SEVERITY IV CATASTROPHIC]
    R2 --> R3[PROBABILITY A FREQUENT]
    R3 --> R4[RI EQUALS 5 REDESIGN MANDATORY]
    R2 --> R5[PROBABILITY B PROBABLE]
    R5 --> R4
    R2 --> R6[PROBABILITY C OCCASIONAL]
    R6 --> R7[RI EQUALS 4 ENGINEERING CONTROL REQUIRED]
    R2 --> R8[PROBABILITY D REMOTE]
    R8 --> R9[RI EQUALS 3 ALARP DEMONSTRATION]
    R2 --> R10[PROBABILITY E IMPROBABLE]
    R10 --> R11[RI EQUALS 2 MANAGEMENT REVIEW]
```

## 4.5 Diagram 4 — Hierarchy of Controls (Applied to PHA Recommendations)

```mermaid
graph TD
    H1[MOST EFFECTIVE ELIMINATION] --> H2[SUBSTITUTION LESS HAZARDOUS MATERIAL]
    H2 --> H3[ENGINEERING CONTROL INHERENT SAFETY BARRIERS]
    H3 --> H4[ADMINISTRATIVE CONTROL PROCEDURES TRAINING]
    H4 --> H5[LEAST EFFECTIVE PPE PERSONAL PROTECTIVE EQUIPMENT]
    H1 -.- H6[PHA RECOMMENDATIONS MUST START AT TOP]
    H2 -.- H6
    H3 -.- H6
    H4 -.- H6
    H5 -.- H6
    H6 --> H7[RE EVALUATE RESIDUAL RISK USING REDUCED PROBABILITY OR SEVERITY]
```

## 4.6 Diagram 5 — PHA Team Composition (KTU Convention)

```mermaid
graph TD
    T1[PHA TEAM LEADER EXPERIENCED FACILITATOR] --> T2[PROCESS ENGINEER]
    T1 --> T3[MECHANICAL ENGINEER]
    T1 --> T4[INSTRUMENTATION AND CONTROL ENGINEER]
    T1 --> T5[SAFETY ENGINEER PSM EXPERT]
    T1 --> T6[OPERATIONS SUPERVISOR]
    T1 --> T7[MAINTENANCE REPRESENTATIVE]
    T1 --> T8[ENVIRONMENTAL SPECIALIST]
    T1 --> T9[HAZARDOUS AREA CLASSIFICATION EXPERT]
    T1 --> T10[DESIGN TEAM REPRESENTATIVE]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Block-Level Functional Note:** PHA is conducted as a **multi-disciplinary team exercise**, not by an individual. The KTU 2024 syllabus specifically lists team composition as an examinable topic. The minimum team is **4 members**, the recommended is **6–10**, and the team leader **must not be the designer** of the system under review (to maintain independence).

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions

> [!NOTE]
> Each Part A question is **direct, definition-based, and requires a 3–4 sentence model answer**. The marking scheme rewards precision and the use of standard KTU terminology.

### Question A1: Define Preliminary Hazard Analysis. State any two of its objectives. `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**
Preliminary Hazard Analysis (PHA) is a structured, inductive safety analysis technique performed during the early conceptual or design phase of a system to systematically identify potential hazards, evaluate their causes, consequences, severity, and probability, and recommend preliminary risk reduction measures. It was originally developed in 1972 by the U.S. Army Materiel Command under MIL-STD-882.

**Two objectives:**
1. To identify all credible hazards associated with the system before detailed design is committed, when changes are still inexpensive.
2. To provide a documented hazard database that serves as the foundation for downstream detailed studies such as HAZOP, FMEA, and SIL classification.

**Mark Distribution (KTU pattern):** Definition: 1 mark; Objective 1: 1 mark; Objective 2: 1 mark. (Total 3 marks.)

### Question A2: Differentiate between PHA and HAZOP. Mention two points. `[KTU University Exam — June 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

| Basis | PHA | HAZOP |
|---|---|---|
| **Logic Direction** | Inductive (hazard → consequence) | Deductive (process deviation → cause → consequence) |
| **Lifecycle Stage** | Conceptual / Front-end design | Detailed engineering (post P&ID freeze) |
| **Resolution** | Coarse, qualitative | Fine, node-by-node, intent-by-intent |
| **Output** | PHA worksheet | Cause–Consequence matrix for each deviation |

**Mark Distribution:** Any two points with 1.5 marks each. (Total 3 marks.)

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> As per the **KTU 2024 Scheme**, each Part B question carries **14 marks** split as **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**. The student answers EITHER Question A OR Question B.

### ❑ Question A (14 Marks)

#### **Part (a)** — 7 Marks: Explain the seven-step methodology of conducting a PHA study. List the typical PHA team composition.

**Model Solution:**

A Preliminary Hazard Analysis study is conducted in **seven systematic steps**:

**Step 1 — Define the System and Scope (1 mark):**
The facilitator establishes the **physical boundaries** (battery limits, equipment items), the **operational modes** (start-up, normal, shutdown, maintenance, emergency), and the **interfaces** with adjacent units. A clear system description document is prepared using PFDs and preliminary P&IDs.

**Step 2 — Identify Hazards (1 mark):**
The team brainstorms all credible hazards arising from **energy sources** (electrical, mechanical, thermal, chemical, radiation, gravitational), **hazardous materials** (flammable, toxic, reactive), and **process deviations** (high pressure, high temperature, overfill, dry-out). External hazards (flood, earthquake, lightning, vehicle impact) are also considered.

**Step 3 — Determine Causes (1 mark):**
For each hazard, potential causes are identified using the **MOCEL** mnemonic (Material, Operator, Control, Equipment, Layout) and coarse **guideword reasoning** (no, more, less, reverse, other than, as well as). Historical incident databases such as **eMARS, OSHA, AICHE CCPS** are referenced.

**Step 4 — Determine Consequences (1 mark):**
Each cause is traced through a chain of events to the **worst credible outcome**, and the effect is categorised on personnel (injury, fatality, occupational illness), equipment (damage, downtime), environment (release to air, water, soil), and mission (production loss).

**Step 5 — Assign Severity and Probability (1 mark):**
Severity is rated on a **4-level scale** (Catastrophic, Critical, Marginal, Negligible) and Probability on a **5-level scale** (Frequent, Probable, Occasional, Remote, Improbable), as per the **MIL-STD-882E** convention.

**Step 6 — Evaluate Risk and Recommend Action (1 mark):**
The **Risk Index (RI = 1 to 5)** is read from the **5×4 risk matrix**. The team applies the **ALARP principle** to decide whether to accept, control, or eliminate the hazard. Recommendations follow the **hierarchy of controls**: elimination → substitution → engineering → administrative → PPE.

**Step 7 — Document the PHA Worksheet and Action Register (1 mark):**
The output is a **PHA worksheet** with seven columns (PHA No., Hazard, Cause, Consequence, Severity, Probability, Recommendation) and an **Action Tracking Register (ATR)** assigning responsibility and target date for each recommendation.

**PHA Team Composition (separate 1 mark — covered in (a)):**
- Team Leader (independent experienced facilitator)
- Process Engineer
- Mechanical / Static Equipment Engineer
- Instrumentation & Control Engineer
- Safety / Loss Prevention Engineer
- Operations Supervisor
- Maintenance Representative
- Environmental Specialist
- Hazardous Area Classification Expert
- Design Engineer (as observer, not decision-maker)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** Students frequently omit **Step 1 (System Definition)** and jump directly to hazard listing. This loses **1 mark** in Part (a) and is considered a **structured-answer violation**. Always begin with the boundary statement: *"The system under review is the XYZ unit, bounded by the ABC pigtail upstream and the DEF pump suction downstream."*

#### **Part (b)** — 7 Marks: A proposed process unit will handle **liquid ammonia at −33 °C and 12 bar pressure**, stored in a 500 m³ bullet. The PHA team has identified that the bullet is exposed to a credible external fire from an adjacent road tanker parking area. Carry out a PHA entry for this specific hazard and recommend three engineering controls using the hierarchy of controls.

**Model Solution:**

**System under review (0.5 mark):**
500 m³ ammonia bullet, design pressure 12 bar g, design temperature −33 °C, double-walled construction, located in a designated hazardous storage area. Adjacent area is used for road tanker parking, with **potential fire exposure of 30 minutes minimum**.

**PHA Entry (3.5 marks):**

| Field | Entry |
|---|---|
| **PHA No.** | PHA-201 |
| **Hazard** | Toxic release of liquid ammonia due to bullet failure under external fire |
| **Cause** | External pool fire from road tanker leak impinges on bullet; heat input raises internal pressure beyond design; failure of relief valve; loss of vacuum in insulation space |
| **Consequence** | Catastrophic release of liquid ammonia → flash to vapour → toxic cloud drift to populated area → multiple fatalities on- and off-site; long-term environmental damage |
| **Severity** | **IV — Catastrophic** (toxic release; > 1 fatality expected off-site) |
| **Probability** | **D — Remote** (assuming bund wall and water spray exist; reduced to **E — Improbable** with new controls) |
| **Risk Index** | **3** (currently), reduces to **2** after controls |
| **Recommendations** | (see below) |

**Three Engineering Controls Following the Hierarchy (3 marks):**

1. **Inherent Safety (Elimination/Substitution):**
   *Relocate the road tanker parking area to a minimum 30 m from the ammonia bullet* (per **OISD-244** and **NFPA-30A** clearance standards). This eliminates the ignition source from the immediate zone of the bullet. If relocation is impossible, replace the ammonia inventory with a less volatile alternative (e.g., aqueous ammonia).

2. **Engineering Control — Passive:**
   *Provide a 2-hour fire-rated insulation jacket on the bullet* (calcium silicate or perlite) to delay pressure rise during fire exposure. Verify that the **pressure relief valve** is sized for **fire case (wetted surface)** as per API 521 and that a **second PSV** is installed to meet redundancy (one PSV in service, one isolated for maintenance).

3. **Engineering Control — Active:**
   *Install a fixed water deluge system* on the bullet with a flow rate of **10.2 L/min per m²** of wetted surface, with **remote-operated isolation valves** and a **firewater pump set** dedicated to ammonia storage. The system should be triggered by **flame and heat detectors** in the parking area and verified annually per **OISD-117**.

**Residual Risk (after controls):** Severity unchanged (IV), Probability reduces from D to E, therefore Risk Index reduces from 3 to 2. Document as a **Tolerable with management review** item in the PHA register.

**Mark Distribution:** PHA table correctly filled: 3.5 marks; Three controls: 1 mark each; Conclusion: 0.5 mark. (Total 7 marks.)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** When the question asks for "**engineering controls**", students often answer with **PPE** (gas mask, SCBA). PPE is the **last** rung of the hierarchy and is considered a **procedural control**, not an engineering control. Writing "SCBA for operators" as the primary recommendation will receive **0 marks**. Always place **inherent safety first**.

---

### ❑ Question B (Alternative 14 Marks)

#### **Part (a)** — 7 Marks: Explain the **5 × 4 PHA Risk Matrix** used in the KTU 2024 scheme. Draw the matrix and explain the risk-acceptance criteria for each risk index level.

**Model Solution:**

The 5 × 4 PHA Risk Matrix is the **standard scoring tool** of the Preliminary Hazard Analysis, adopted from **MIL-STD-882E** and harmonised with **CCPS and IEC 31010** guidance. It cross-tabulates **four severity levels** (rows) with **five probability levels** (columns) to produce a **Risk Index (RI)** from **1 to 5**.

**Severity Scale (Rows) — 1.5 marks:**

| Level | Category | Definition |
|---|---|---|
| I | Negligible | First aid only, minor equipment damage (< ₹10 L) |
| II | Marginal | Lost-time injury < 30 days, moderate damage (₹10 L – ₹1 Cr) |
| III | Critical | Severe injury / hospitalisation, major equipment damage (₹1 – ₹10 Cr) |
| IV | Catastrophic | Fatality, permanent disability, plant loss (> ₹10 Cr) |

**Probability Scale (Columns) — 1.5 marks:**

| Level | Category | Frequency (per year) |
|---|---|---|
| A | Frequent | $P > 10^{-1}$ |
| B | Probable | $10^{-2} < P \le 10^{-1}$ |
| C | Occasional | $10^{-3} < P \le 10^{-2}$ |
| D | Remote | $10^{-4} < P \le 10^{-3}$ |
| E | Improbable | $P \le 10^{-4}$ |

**The 5 × 4 Matrix (2 marks):**

| Severity ↓ \ Probability → | A | B | C | D | E |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **IV** | 5 | 5 | 4 | 3 | 2 |
| **III** | 5 | 4 | 3 | 2 | 1 |
| **II** | 4 | 3 | 2 | 1 | 1 |
| **I** | 3 | 2 | 1 | 1 | 1 |

**Risk-Acceptance Criteria (2 marks):**

| Risk Index | Decision |
|---|---|
| **5** | **Unacceptable.** Operation prohibited. Redesign mandatory before commissioning. |
| **4** | **Undesirable.** Engineering control mandatory. Defer to detailed study (HAZOP/QRA) to validate. |
| **3** | **Tolerable with controls (ALARP).** Engineering / administrative controls recommended. ALARP demonstration required. |
| **2** | **Acceptable with management review.** Periodic review and operational controls sufficient. |
| **1** | **Broadly acceptable.** No action beyond standard operating procedures. |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** Students often draw the matrix with **rows = probability** and **columns = severity**, which is acceptable **only if the cell values are also re-calculated correctly**. The convention preferred by the KTU 2024 answer key is **rows = severity (low to high, top to bottom)** and **columns = probability (high to low, left to right)**, so that the **top-right cell** is the most benign (I, E) and the **bottom-left cell** is the most severe (IV, A). Reversing axes without re-computing cell values loses 1 mark.

#### **Part (b)** — 7 Marks: For a hydrocarbon storage tank, a PHA identified a hazard with Severity = IV and Probability = C (current). After installation of three independent protection layers (each with PFD = 0.01), calculate the residual probability and residual risk index. Comment on whether the residual risk is ALARP.

**Model Solution:**

**Given Data (0.5 mark):**
- Severity: $S = IV$ (Catastrophic)
- Initial Probability: $C$ (Occasional), $f_{init} = 5 \times 10^{-3}$ per year (mid-band value)
- Three independent protection layers (IPLs), each with $PFD = 0.01$
- IPL are in series; PFDs are multiplied.

**Step 1: Compute Combined PFD (1.5 marks):**

$$
PFD_{total} = PFD_1 \times PFD_2 \times PFD_3
$$

$$
PFD_{total} = 0.01 \times 0.01 \times 0.01
$$

$$
PFD_{total} = 10^{-6}
$$

**Step 2: Compute Residual Frequency (1.5 marks):**

$$
f_{residual} = f_{init} \times PFD_{total}
$$

$$
f_{residual} = 5 \times 10^{-3} \times 10^{-6}
$$

$$
f_{residual} = 5 \times 10^{-9} \;\; \text{per year}
$$

**Step 3: Determine New Probability Band (1.5 marks):**

Since $f_{residual} = 5 \times 10^{-9} \le 10^{-4}$, the new probability band is:

$$
P_{residual} = E \;\; (\text{Improbable})
$$

**Step 4: Determine New Risk Index (1 mark):**

From the 5 × 4 matrix, with $S = IV$ and $P = E$:

$$
RI_{residual} = 2
$$

**Step 5: ALARP Comment (1 mark):**

The risk has been reduced from **RI = 4 (Undesirable)** to **RI = 2 (Acceptable with management review)** by the addition of three independent protection layers. The cost of these IPLs (estimated ₹1.2 Cr) is small compared to the consequence cost (₹10 Cr for a major incident and possible off-site impact). Therefore, the risk reduction is **proportionate to the cost** and the **residual risk is ALARP**. Further risk reduction (e.g., adding a fourth IPL) would incur **disproportionate cost (gross disproportion test failure)** and is not required.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Unit Consistency:** Students frequently write $PFD$ as a percentage (1 %) instead of a fraction (0.01), which produces an incorrect $PFD_{total}$ that is **100× too high**. Always state the PFD as a **dimensionless fraction between 0 and 1** unless the question explicitly uses percentages. Marks lost here are common and avoidable.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for the PHA Topic — Read this 30 minutes before entering the exam hall.**

- **Definition:** PHA is a **structured, inductive, qualitative** hazard analysis performed at the **conceptual / front-end design** stage to identify, evaluate, and rank hazards.
- **Origin:** **MIL-STD-882** (1972), **U.S. Army Materiel Command**. Standardised under **IEC 31010:2019**.
- **Lifecycle Position:** **First** in the safety analysis hierarchy — input to HAZOP, FMEA, FTA, QRA, SIL classification, and emergency planning.
- **Core Chain:** *Hazard → Cause → Consequence → Severity + Probability → Risk Index → Mitigation.*
- **Team:** Multi-disciplinary (4–10 members), led by an **independent** facilitator; the designer is **not** the leader.
- **Severity:** **4 levels** — I Negligible, II Marginal, III Critical, IV Catastrophic.
- **Probability:** **5 levels** — A Frequent, B Probable, C Occasional, D Remote, E Improbable.
- **Risk Index:** Read from the **5 × 4 matrix**, value 1 to 5. RI = 5 → Redesign; RI = 4 → Engineering control; RI = 3 → ALARP; RI = 2 → Accept with review; RI = 1 → Accept.
- **Quantitative Risk Formula:** $R = f \times C$ (frequency × consequence). Convert to RI using the **CCPS IR band table** ($10^{-1}$, $10^{-2}$, $10^{-4}$, $10^{-6}$).
- **PFD Combination for IPLs:** $PFD_{total} = \prod PFD_i$ (multiplicative for series layers).
- **1 : 10 : 100 Rule:** Cost of change at design : construction : operation = 1 : 10 : 100. Justifies early PHA.
- **Hierarchy of Controls (top = best):** **Elimination → Substitution → Engineering Control → Administrative → PPE.**
- **ALARP Test:** "Risk reduction measures must be implemented **unless the cost is grossly disproportionate** to the benefit gained."
- **Re-validation Trigger:** PHA must be re-done whenever there is a **Management of Change (MOC)** — design, operation, materials, or personnel.
- **Output Document:** **PHA worksheet** (7 columns) + **Action Tracking Register (ATR)**.
- **Common Mistake:** Calling PHA a "**deductive**" method — it is **inductive**. Calling HAZOP a "**broad**" study — it is **narrow and detailed**.
- **Common Mistake #2:** Writing RI = Severity × Probability as **arithmetic multiplication** (e.g., $IV \times C = 4 \times 3 = 12$). The matrix is a **categorical lookup**, not arithmetic. RI = 4 in this case.
- **Common Mistake #3:** Recommending PPE (SCBA, gas mask) as the **primary** engineering control. PPE is the **last** resort.
- **KTU Linked Codes & Standards to Quote:** MIL-STD-882E, IEC 31010:2019, IEC 61511, API 521, OISD-244, NFPA-30A, IS/IEC 62305, AICHE CCPS Guidelines for Hazard Evaluation Procedures (3rd Edition).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU 2024 Exam Mantra for PHA:** *"Hazard first, cause next, consequence last, severity and probability numerical, matrix lookup mandatory, hierarchy of controls non-negotiable, ALARP always."*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Hazard and Operability Study (HAZOP) - Methodology
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAUT415 - INDUSTRIAL SAFETY ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Hazard and Operability Study (HAZOP) - Methodology

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HAZOP (Hazard and Operability Study)** is a structured, systematic, and rigorous team-based risk assessment technique developed in the 1970s by ICI (Imperial Chemical Industries) to identify potential process hazards, operability issues, and deviations from design intent in chemical/process plants, pipelines, and systems handling hazardous substances.

According to **IEC 61882** and **BS IEC 61882:2016**, HAZOP is defined as *"a structured and systematic examination of a complex planned or existing process or operation in order to identify and evaluate problems that may represent risks to personnel or equipment, or prevent efficient operation."*

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Note:** HAZOP is treated as a *qualitative* hazard identification technique under the broader umbrella of Process Safety Management (PSM). It is a **mandatory study** for any process plant handling flammable/toxic chemicals above threshold quantities (per India’s **Factories Act / Manufacture Storage and Import of Hazardous Chemical Rules, 1989** — MSIHC Rules).

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are a **doctor examining a patient**. You don't just glance at them — you go through a **systematic checklist**:
- Does the patient have fever? (`MORE TEMPERATURE`)
- Is blood pressure too low? (`LESS PRESSURE`)
- Are there any abnormal sounds? (`OTHER THAN` normal)
- Is any organ missing function? (`NO FLOW`)

Each symptom you check corresponds to a **Guide Word** (like a symptom). Each body parameter you measure corresponds to a **Process Parameter** (e.g., flow, pressure, temperature). The combination of a guide word + parameter gives you a **Deviation** — a potential problem you need to investigate.

> A **HAZOP study** applies this *exact same medical-checkup logic* to a chemical plant — instead of body organs, the "organs" are pipes, vessels, pumps, valves, and reactors. The plant is dissected into **nodes** (sections), and at each node the team *deliberately imagines things going wrong* using a fixed vocabulary of guide words. Every "wrong scenario" is documented, its **cause**, **consequence**, and **safeguards** are recorded, and **recommendations** are issued.

This deliberate, "what could possibly go wrong?" approach is what makes HAZOP uniquely powerful — it **forces** engineers to think beyond the happy path.

---

## 1.3 Core HAZOP Vocabulary (Building Blocks)

| Term | Meaning | Example |
|------|---------|---------|
| **Node** | A discrete section of the process where parameters are relatively constant | A heat exchanger, a reactor, a transfer line |
| **Design Intent** | The expected normal operating condition at a node | "Pump P-101 delivers 50 m³/h of water at 4 bar" |
| **Process Parameter** | A physical/chemical variable at the node | Flow, Pressure, Temperature, Level, Concentration, Phase |
| **Guide Word** | A short word used to generate deviations | NO, MORE, LESS, AS WELL AS, PART OF, REVERSE, OTHER THAN |
| **Deviation** | Guide Word + Process Parameter | "NO FLOW" or "MORE PRESSURE" |
| **Cause** | The reason the deviation might occur | Pump failure, valve closed, pipe blockage |
| **Consequence** | The outcome if the deviation occurs | Overpressure → vessel rupture |
| **Safeguard** | Existing protective mechanism | Pressure relief valve, alarm, interlock |
| **Recommendation** | Additional action to reduce risk | Install independent high-pressure trip |

---

## 1.4 Standard Reference Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> Key quantitative metrics used in HAZOP team composition and planning (as per **KTU 2024 expected answer**):
> - **Team Size:** 4 to 8 members (typically 5–7)
> - **Study Duration:** Approximately **2 to 4 weeks** for a medium-sized plant
> - **Node Duration:** Typically **30 minutes to 2 hours** per node
> - **Documentation Standard:** **BS IEC 61882:2016** / **IEC 61882**
> - **Team Leader:** Must be **independent** of the project team (impartial facilitator)

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The HAZOP Methodology — Step-by-Step Logical Framework

HAZOP is not a single analysis but a **multi-phase structured investigation**. Below is the **gold-standard 9-step methodology** prescribed by IEC 61882 and adopted by KTU.

### **Step 1 — Define Scope, Objective & Boundaries**
- Clearly state the **boundaries of the study** (Battery Limits).
- Identify the **Physical scope** (units, systems) and **Time scope** (operational modes: startup, normal, shutdown, maintenance, emergency).
- Define the **HAZOP objective** — is it for new design, modification (MOC), or existing plant revalidation?

> *Why this step?* Without a sharp definition, the team can drift into unconnected issues. The scope provides the "compass."

### **Step 2 — Assemble the HAZOP Team**
A multi-disciplinary team is mandatory because **no single engineer holds complete knowledge**.

| Role | Responsibility | Typical Discipline |
|------|---------------|---------------------|
| **Team Leader / Facilitator** | Drives study, asks guide words, ensures coverage, impartial | Independent HAZOP expert |
| **Scribe / Recorder** | Captures every deviation, cause, consequence, action | Safety engineer |
| **Process Engineer** | Explains design intent, parameters | Chemical engineering |
| | | Continued... |
| **Operations / Plant Engineer** | Describes real operating behavior | Operations background |
| **Instrumentation Engineer** | Identifies trips, interlocks, alarms | Instrumentation |
| **Maintenance Engineer** | Mechanical failure modes | Mechanical |
| **Safety Engineer** | Risk ranking, regulatory knowledge | Safety |
| **Other Specialists** | Rotating as required (corrosion, civil, E&I) | Domain expert |

> *Why?* Hazards cut across disciplines — a mechanical failure may trigger a process deviation, which an operations expert recognizes better than a designer.

### **Step 3 — Gather Information / Documentation**
Collect **as-built** documents (not just design intent!):
- **P&IDs (Piping & Instrumentation Diagrams)** — most important document
- **PFDs (Process Flow Diagrams)**
- **Cause & Effect Charts**
- **Operating procedures & manuals**
- **Equipment data sheets, MSDS**
- **Layout drawings, hazardous area classification**
- **Previous incident reports / near-miss data**

### **Step 4 — Divide the Plant into Nodes (Sectionalization)**
A **node** is the smallest meaningful section where the design intent remains constant.
- Begin at an **origin point** (e.g., pump discharge, vessel inlet) and progress along the **process flow**.
- Node size: typically **a piece of equipment + associated piping** between major control points.
- Mark nodes on the **P&ID** (often with a dashed box).

> *Why?* Smaller nodes = better focus, but excessive nodes = inefficiency.

### **Step 5 — Define the Design Intent for Each Node**
State clearly, in **2–3 lines**, the **expected** operation:
> *"At Node 3, Reactor R-201 is designed to operate at 180 °C, 8 bar, with a liquid hold-up of 75%, fed by Pump P-201 at 20 m³/h, with continuous mixing at 200 RPM, and jacket cooling water flow of 30 m³/h."*

### **Step 6 — Apply Guide Words to Process Parameters**
For **each parameter** at the node, apply **each guide word** systematically. Standard combinations:

| Process Parameter | NO | MORE | LESS | AS WELL AS | PART OF | REVERSE | OTHER THAN |
|------------------|-----|------|------|------------|---------|---------|------------|
| Flow | No Flow | More Flow | Less Flow | Misdirected Flow | Part of Flow | Reverse Flow | Wrong Fluid |
| Pressure | No Pressure | High Pressure | Low Pressure | Vacuum | Partial Pressure | Reverse ΔP | Wrong Pressure |
| Temperature | No Temp Change | High Temp | Low Temp | — | — | — | Wrong Temp |
| Level | Empty | High Level | Low Level | — | — | — | — |
| Composition | No Composition | Excess Reactant | Loss of Reactant | Contaminant | Wrong Ratio | — | Wrong Material |
| Phase | No Phase Change | — | — | Two-Phase Flow | — | — | Wrong Phase |
| Mixing / Reaction | No Reaction | Runaway | — | Side Reaction | Incomplete | — | Wrong Product |
| Time / Sequence | No Step | Too Long | Too Short | — | — | Backwards Step | Wrong Order |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Not all combinations are sensible.** Experienced HAZOP leaders skip inapplicable guide words quickly. This is where the **facilitator's skill** matters most.

### **Step 7 — Identify Causes for Each Deviation**
For each plausible deviation, brainstorm all possible **causes**:
- Equipment failure (pump seizure, instrument failure)
- Human error (valve left closed, wrong startup sequence)
- External events (power failure, utility loss, ambient temperature)
- Process upset (upstream surge, contamination)

> *Tip:* Causes must be **specific and plausible** — not "Act of God" or "unknown."

### **Step 8 — Determine Consequences & Existing Safeguards**
- **Consequence:** What actually happens downstream? (e.g., high pressure → relief valve lifts → vapor release → atmospheric hazard)
- **Safeguard:** What stops/mitigates it? (PSV set at 10 bar, operator alarm, interlock)
- **Risk Ranking:** Use a **Qualitative Risk Matrix** (Severity × Likelihood) if required by company procedure.

### **Step 9 — Recommend Actions & Follow-Up**
- For each unacceptable deviation, propose **specific, actionable, time-bound** recommendations:
  - *"Install independent high-level switch (SIL 2) on V-301 within 3 months."*
  - *"Update operating procedure to lock-out sequence; review by 30 days."*
- **Each action is assigned an owner and tracked to closure.**
- A **HAZOP Action Register** is maintained.

---

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> While HAZOP is **qualitative**, KTU examiners frequently test the **planning metrics** and **risk matrix** in numerical form. Master the table below.

| Concept | Expression / Standard Value | Units / Notes |
|---------|---------------------------|---------------|
| Team size | $N_{\text{team}} \in [4, 8]$ | persons |
| Optimal team size (IEC 61882) | $N_{\text{opt}} = 5$ to $7$ | persons |
| Node duration | $t_{\text{node}} \in [0.5, 2.0]$ | hours |
| Total study duration (approx) | $T_{\text{HAZOP}} = N_{\text{nodes}} \times t_{\text{node}} \times 1.3$ (mult. by 1.3 for review/discussion) | days |
| Severity (typical 1–5) | $S \in \lbrace 1, 2, 3, 4, 5 \rbrace$ | — |
| Likelihood (typical 1–5) | $L \in \lbrace 1, 2, 3, 4, 5 \rbrace$ | — |
| Risk Score | $R = S \times L$ | dimensionless |
| Risk Matrix Thresholds | $R \leq 4$ → Low, $5 \leq R \leq 9$ → Medium, $10 \leq R \leq 15$ → High, $R \geq 16$ → Extreme | based on $5 \times 5$ matrix |
| HAZOP documentation standard | **BS IEC 61882:2016** | — |
| Origin of HAZOP | **ICI Heavy Organic Chemicals Division, UK, 1974** | historical fact |
| Mandatory HAZOP threshold (India) | Any **hazardous chemical** $Q \geq Q_{\text{threshold}}$ per **MSIHC Rules 1989 (Sch. 2 & 3)** | — |
| Action closure target (typical) | $\leq 6$ months for high-risk findings | months |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Risk Matrix Formula** — Often tested as a 2-mark question:
> $$R = S \times L$$
> where $R$ is the **Risk Score**, $S$ is **Severity**, and $L$ is **Likelihood**.

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

HAZOP is **mandatory** in:
- **Oil & Gas:** Upstream platforms, refineries (ASME B31.3, API RP 14C).
- **Petrochemicals:** Ethylene, ammonia, polymer plants.
- **Pharmaceuticals:** FDA / cGMP environments; Clean-in-Place (CIP) systems.
- **Nuclear:** Auxiliary systems, fuel handling.
- **Pipelines & Storage:** Terminals, LNG facilities.
- **New sectors:** Battery manufacturing, hydrogen plants, CO₂ capture — emerging 2024 applications.

In **Kerala’s industrial landscape**, HAZOP is critical for:
- **Fertilizers & Chemicals Travancore (FACT)** — ammonia handling.
- **Cochin Oil Refinery (BPCL)** — hydrocarbon processing.
- **Petrochemical Complex, Ambalamugal** — naphtha cracking.
- **BPCL & Indian Oil installations** in the **PCPIR** zones.
- **IISCO / Steel Authority plants** — process gas safety.
