# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example — HAZOP on a Simple Process Node (KTU Exam-Style)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Problem:** A pump P-101 delivers water from a storage tank T-101 to a heat exchanger E-101 at a design flow of **50 m³/h** and **4 bar**. The line has a flow indicator (FI), a manual isolation valve, and a non-return valve. Conduct a **partial HAZOP** for the deviations of **NO FLOW** and **MORE PRESSURE** at the line section just downstream of the pump (Node 3).

### **Solution — Systematic Walkthrough**

We will apply the **HAZOP worksheet** format exactly as KTU examiners expect.

---

### **Part A — HAZOP Worksheet for "NO FLOW"**

#### **Step 1: Identify the deviation**

$$\text{Deviation} = \text{Guide Word (NO)} \times \text{Process Parameter (FLOW)} = \text{“NO FLOW”}$$

**Meaning at Node 3 (discharge line of P-101):** The pump is running (or appears to) but no water is reaching E-101.

#### **Step 2: Enumerate Causes (mechanistic reasoning)**

| # | Cause | Category |
|---|-------|----------|
| C1 | Pump P-101 mechanically failed (seized impeller, shaft break) | Equipment failure |
| C2 | Suction strainer on T-101 blocked (no NPSH available) | Equipment failure |
| C3 | Manual isolation valve V-102 on discharge line closed | Human error / procedural |
| C4 | Suction tank T-101 emptied (no liquid available) | Upstream issue |
| C5 | Power failure to P-101 motor | External event |
| C6 | Pipe rupture downstream (full flow to atmosphere) | Equipment failure |

#### **Step 3: Determine Consequences**

If NO FLOW occurs, the immediate consequence at the **heat exchanger E-101** is:
- Loss of cooling water → process fluid temperature rises on the hot side.
- Tube-side fouling accelerated, possible tube damage.
- The pump runs dead-headed → if blocked-discharge and no relief, **MORE PRESSURE** develops (this is a *deviation cascade*).

#### **Step 4: Identify Existing Safeguards**

| Safeguard | Type | Adequacy |
|-----------|------|----------|
| Flow indicator (FI-101) in line | Alarm | Informs operator |
| Pump motor ammeter | Alarm | Shows no load condition |
| Low-low flow interlock → trips pump | Instrumented Protective Function (IPF) | **Good safeguard** |
| Non-return valve (NRV-101) at pump discharge | Mechanical | Prevents reverse flow |

#### **Step 5: Recommendations**

| # | Recommendation | Action Owner | Due Date |
|---|----------------|-------------|----------|
| R1 | Install independent **low-low flow switch** (SIL 1) on discharge line in addition to FI alarm | Instrumentation | 2 months |
| R2 | Verify low-flow trip setpoint is **< 10% of design flow** and tested every shift | Operations | 1 month |
| R3 | Add **mechanical seal flush water flow** interlock to detect seal failure | Maintenance | 3 months |

---

### **Part B — HAZOP Worksheet for "MORE PRESSURE"**

#### **Step 1: Identify the deviation**

$$\text{Deviation} = \text{Guide Word (MORE)} \times \text{Process Parameter (PRESSURE)} = \text{“MORE PRESSURE”}$$

**Meaning at Node 3:** Pressure in the line exceeds the design value of **4 bar**.

#### **Step 2: Enumerate Causes**

| # | Cause | Category |
|---|-------|----------|
| C1 | Pump P-101 selected too large (oversized) — operates at shut-off head | Design error |
| C2 | Discharge block valve V-102 closed while pump running | Operational / Human error |
| C3 | E-101 tube-side blocked — flow restricted downstream | Process upset |
| C4 | Thermal expansion of water in isolated line (e.g., pump off, hot sun) | Physical phenomenon |
| C5 | Water hammer from sudden valve closure | Transient |
| C6 | Multiple pumps operating in parallel into a single line | Operational |

#### **Step 3: Determine Consequences**

- Pressure > 4 bar → stress on piping.
- If pressure exceeds pipe rating (say **PN-16 = 16 bar**) → **catastrophic rupture**.
- If exceeds flange/tube rating of E-101 → **tube bundle failure**, water ingress into process stream.
- Potential for **environmental release** and **personnel injury** from pipe whip.

#### **Step 4: Identify Existing Safeguards**

| Safeguard | Type | Setpoint / Rating |
|-----------|------|-------------------|
| Pressure Safety Valve (PSV-101) on E-101 shell | Mechanical relief | Set at **6.0 bar**, sized for blocked-discharge case |
| Pressure indicator (PI-101) on line | Alarm | **HI alarm at 5.0 bar** |
| Pump differential pressure limit (instrument) | Control | Limits pump output |
| Pipe class | Mechanical | PN-16 (16 bar) |

#### **Step 5: Risk Ranking using $R = S \times L$ Matrix**

Let's assume a **5 × 5 matrix**:

| | | **Severity (S)** | | | | |
|--|--|--|--|--|--|--|
| **Likelihood (L)** | | 1 (Negligible) | 2 (Minor) | 3 (Moderate) | 4 (Major) | 5 (Catastrophic) |
| | **5 (Frequent)** | 5 | 10 | 15 | 20 | 25 |
| | **4 (Probable)** | 4 | 8 | 12 | 16 | 20 |
| | **3 (Occasional)** | 3 | 6 | 9 | 12 | 15 |
| | **2 (Remote)** | 2 | 4 | 6 | 8 | 10 |
| | **1 (Improbable)** | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5 |

For this deviation:
- $S = 4$ (Major — pipe rupture potential)
- $L = 3$ (Occasional — closed block valve is a known human error)
- $R = S \times L = 4 \times 3 = 12$ → **HIGH RISK**

#### **Step 6: Recommendations**

| # | Recommendation |
|---|----------------|
| R1 | Verify **PSV-101 set pressure (6.0 bar) ≤ 90%** of pipe design pressure (per ASME B31.3) — confirmed, OK. |
| R2 | Install **dedicated pressure transmitter (PT) with high-high trip** to trip pump at 5.5 bar. |
| R3 | Revise **operating procedure** to add lock-out between maintenance and operation; place warning sign at V-102. |
| R4 | Conduct **simulator-based training** for operators on the consequence of pumping into a closed valve. |

---

## 3.2 Derivation of Study Duration (Conceptual)

For a plant with $N_{\text{nodes}}$ nodes, total on-line HAZOP time is:

$$T_{\text{on-line}} = N_{\text{nodes}} \times t_{\text{node}}$$

Including **team briefings, document review, and report writing** (typically 30% overhead):

$$T_{\text{HAZOP,total}} = 1.3 \times N_{\text{nodes}} \times t_{\text{node}}$$

**Example:** A plant with 25 nodes, average node time of 1.5 hours:

$$T_{\text{HAZOP,total}} = 1.3 \times 25 \times 1.5 = 48.75 \text{ hours}$$

Spread over 5 working days (8 hours/day, with breaks), this is **6 working days** for the on-site workshop alone — not including the 2-week pre-meeting document review and 3-week post-meeting report writing.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Guide Word × Parameter Combinations

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The matrix below is a **classic KTU exam favorite** (4 to 7 marks). Memorize the deviation-generation logic.

| **Parameter ↓** | **NO** | **MORE** | **LESS** | **AS WELL AS** | **PART OF** | **REVERSE** | **OTHER THAN** |
|----------------|--------|----------|----------|----------------|-------------|-------------|----------------|
| Flow | No Flow | High Flow | Low Flow | Misdirected | Partial | Reverse Flow | Wrong Fluid |
| Pressure | Vacuum | Overpressure | Underpressure | — | — | — | — |
| Temperature | No ΔT | High Temp | Low Temp | — | — | — | — |
| Level | Empty | High Level | Low Level | — | — | — | — |
| Composition | — | Excess A | Loss of A | Contaminant | Incomplete Mix | — | Wrong Material |
| Phase | — | — | — | Two-Phase | — | — | Phase Change |
| Mixing/Reaction | No Reaction | Runaway | — | Side Reaction | Incomplete | — | Wrong Product |
| Time/Sequence | Step Skipped | Too Long | Too Short | Extra Step | — | Backwards | Wrong Order |
| Start/Stop | No Start | Fails to Stop | — | — | — | — | — |

**Note:** A blank cell means the combination is not meaningful in most process contexts.

---

## 3.4 HAZOP vs. Other Techniques (Comparative Table)

> [!NOTE]
> Examiners often ask: *"Why HAZOP and not What-If Analysis?"* — The comparison below is the answer.

| Feature | HAZOP | What-If | FMEA | Check-List |
|---------|-------|---------|------|------------|
| Structured | **Yes** (rigorous) | No (brainstorm) | Moderate | Low |
| Team-based | Mandatory | Recommended | Optional | Optional |
| Quantitative | No (qualitative) | No | Often | No |
| Best for | **Process plants** | Early concept | Mechanical/Electrical | Repetitive tasks |
| Cost | High | Low | Moderate | Low |
| Documentation depth | Very high | Low | High | Moderate |
| Identifies operating problems | **Yes** | Limited | Limited | No |

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 HAZOP Procedure Flowchart (Mermaid)

> [!NOTE]
> The following block diagram captures the **iterative, sequential nature of the HAZOP methodology** as applied at each node. This is the most exam-relevant flowchart for KTU 2024.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Define Scope and Objective] --> B[Form HAZOP Team - 5 to 7 Members]
    B --> C[Gather Documentation P&IDs, PFDs, C&E]
    C --> D[Divide Plant into Nodes]
    D --> E{For Each Node}
    E --> F[State Design Intent]
    F --> G[Apply Guide Words to Process Parameters]
    G --> H[Generate Deviation = Guide Word + Parameter]
    H --> I{Is Deviation Meaningful?}
    I -->|No| G
    I -->|Yes| J[Identify Causes]
    J --> K[Determine Consequences]
    K --> L[Identify Existing Safeguards]
    L --> M[Assess Risk using S x L Matrix]
    M --> N{Risk Acceptable?}
    N -->|Yes| P[Record in Worksheet]
    N -->|No| O[Issue Recommendation with Owner and Deadline]
    O --> P
    P --> Q{More Guide Words?}
    Q -->|Yes| G
    Q -->|No| R{More Nodes?}
    R -->|Yes| E
    R -->|No| S[Prepare Draft HAZOP Report]
    S --> T[Issue Action Register to Management]
    T --> U[Track Actions to Closure]
    U --> V[Re-HAZOP if Major Changes Occur]
    V --> W[End]
```

---

## 4.2 HAZOP Team Structure (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    TL[HAZOP Team Leader - Independent Facilitator]
    SC[Scribe - Records Findings]
    PE[Process Engineer - Design Intent]
    OE[Operations Engineer - Field Reality]
    IE[Instrumentation Engineer - Trips and Alarms]
    ME[Maintenance Engineer - Failure Modes]
    SE[Safety Engineer - Risk Matrix and Regulations]

    TL --> SC
    TL --> PE
    TL --> OE
    TL --> IE
    TL --> ME
    TL --> SE

    SC -.->|Documents| WS[HAZOP Worksheet]
    PE -.->|Inputs| WS
    OE -.->|Inputs| WS
    IE -.->|Inputs| WS
    ME -.->|Inputs| WS
    SE -.->|Inputs| WS
    WS --> AR[Action Register]
```

---

## 4.3 HAZOP Worksheet Block Format (Reference Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    N[Node Number and Description] --> DI[Design Intent]
    DI --> D[Deviation - Guide Word + Parameter]
    D --> CS[Causes]
    CS --> CN[Consequences]
    CN --> SG[Existing Safeguards]
    SG --> RR[Risk Score S x L]
    RR --> R{Acceptable?}
    R -->|No| REC[Recommendations]
    R -->|Yes| DOC[Document in Register]
    REC --> DOC
    DOC --> NX[Move to Next Node]
```

## 4.4 P&ID-Based Node Definition (Illustrative)

> [!NOTE]
> The following block diagram represents a **typical node-based dissection** of a small process unit. In a real P&ID, a dashed line is drawn around each node on the diagram.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Node1[Node 1 - Tank T101 Inlet]
        TI1[Tank T101]
    end
    subgraph Node2[Node 2 - Pump Discharge]
        P1[Pump P101]
        V1[Manual Valve V102]
        NRV1[Non-Return Valve]
    end
    subgraph Node3[Node 3 - Heat Exchanger Inlet]
        FI1[Flow Indicator FI101]
        E1[Heat Exchanger E101]
    end
    subgraph Node4[Node 4 - Outlet Header]
        OUT1[Outlet to Process]
    end
    Node1 --> Node2
    Node2 --> Node3
    Node3 --> Node4
```

Each **Node** is HAZOPed in turn. The boundary of each node is a logical point where the **design intent changes** (e.g., where flow direction changes, where equipment type changes, or where a control loop begins).

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> **Model answer style:** Each Part A answer should be **3 to 5 sentences** with one **key term in bold**. Total expected answer length ≈ **60–80 words** (KTU 2024 valuation window).

### **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Define HAZOP. List the four standard guide words used in a HAZOP study.**

**Model Answer:**
> **HAZOP (Hazard and Operability Study)** is a **structured, team-based, qualitative risk assessment technique** used to identify potential hazards and operability problems in process plants by systematically examining deviations from design intent.
>
> The **four most commonly used guide words** are:
> 1. **NO** (e.g., No Flow)
> 2. **MORE** (e.g., More Pressure)
> 3. **LESS** (e.g., Less Temperature)
> 4. **REVERSE** (e.g., Reverse Flow)
>
> Other extended guide words include **AS WELL AS**, **PART OF**, and **OTHER THAN**. (3 marks)

---

### **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam – Jun 2024]`
**What is meant by a 'node' in a HAZOP study? Why is the HAZOP team required to be multi-disciplinary?**

**Model Answer:**
> A **node** in a HAZOP study is a **discrete, logical section of the process** (a piece of equipment and its associated piping) within which the design intent remains constant. Nodes are identified sequentially along the process flow on the **P&ID**.
>
> A **multi-disciplinary team** is required because process hazards originate from diverse domains — **mechanical failure** (maintenance engineer), **instrument trips** (instrumentation engineer), **human error** (operations engineer), and **regulatory/environmental aspects** (safety engineer). A single engineer cannot foresee all deviation causes; **diversity of expertise** ensures thoroughness. (3 marks)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each with Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> KTU 2024 ESE Part B carries **14 marks** with **internal choice** between two questions. Below are **two fully worked-out alternative questions** to match the choice format.

---

### **PART B — Question A** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Paper]`

**Q. (a) [7 Marks]** Describe the **step-by-step methodology of conducting a HAZOP study** as per IEC 61882. Clearly explain the role of the team leader and the concept of a node.

**Q. (b) [7 Marks]** A pump delivers liquid ammonia from a storage bullet to a reactor at **30 m³/h, 12 bar**. The discharge line has a flow transmitter (FT), a control valve (FCV), and a pressure safety valve (PSV) set at **14 bar**. Conduct a HAZOP for the deviations of **(i) NO FLOW** and **(ii) MORE PRESSURE** at the line just downstream of the pump. List **causes, consequences, safeguards**, and **at least two recommendations** for each.

---

### **Model Answer — Question A**

#### **Part (a) — HAZOP Methodology (7 Marks)**

**Step 1: Define Scope and Objective (1 mark)**
- Establish **physical, geographical, and operational boundaries**.
- Define purpose: **new design, modification, or revalidation** of existing plant.
- State the **operating modes** to be covered (startup, normal, shutdown, emergency, maintenance).

**Step 2: Form the HAZOP Team (1 mark)**
- **5 to 7 members** as per IEC 61882.
- **Team Leader (Independent Facilitator)** — drives the study, asks the right guide-word questions, manages time, ensures coverage; **must be independent** of the project to maintain impartiality.
- **Scribe** — captures every point in the HAZOP worksheet.
- **Discipline experts** — process, operations, instrumentation, maintenance, safety.

**Step 3: Information Gathering (1 mark)**
- Collect **P&IDs (most critical), PFDs, Cause-&-Effect charts, operating procedures, MSDS, equipment datasheets, layout drawings, previous incident reports**.

**Step 4: Divide Plant into Nodes (1 mark)**
- A **node** is the smallest meaningful process section where the **design intent remains constant**.
- **Selection criteria:** distinct equipment, change in process phase, presence of a control loop, branching, or major change in line specification.
- Marked on the **P&ID with dashed boundaries**.
- Begin at an **origin point** (e.g., suction of first pump) and progress sequentially downstream.

**Step 5: Define Design Intent for Each Node (1 mark)**
- Written description of **expected operation** at the node — flow rate, pressure, temperature, composition, phase, control philosophy.

**Step 6: Apply Guide Words to Parameters (1 mark)**
- For each parameter (Flow, Pressure, Temperature, Level, Composition, Phase), apply each guide word (**NO, MORE, LESS, AS WELL AS, PART OF, REVERSE, OTHER THAN**).
- Deviation = Guide Word + Parameter (e.g., NO + FLOW = "NO FLOW").

**Step 7: For Each Meaningful Deviation (1 mark)**
- List **causes** (equipment failure, human error, external events, process upsets).
- Determine **consequences** (downstream effects).
- Identify **existing safeguards** (alarms, trips, PSVs, procedures).
- Recommend **additional actions** with assigned owner and deadline.
- **Risk-rank** using $R = S \times L$ matrix.

**Step 8: Documentation & Reporting (Free)**
- **HAZOP report, worksheets, and action register** form the deliverables.
- Actions tracked to closure; **re-HAZOP** triggered for major changes.

---

#### **Part (b) — Worked HAZOP (7 Marks)**

**Node Definition:** Line section downstream of ammonia pump, upstream of FCV, including FT and the line segment to the PSV.

**Design Intent:** Deliver **30 m³/h of liquid ammonia at 12 bar** to reactor R-301, with flow controlled by FCV and overpressure protection by PSV-101 set at 14 bar.

---

##### **Deviation (i): NO FLOW (3.5 marks)**

| Item | Detail |
|------|--------|
| **Causes** | (1) Ammonia pump P-201 mechanically failed (seizure, shaft break); (2) Suction strainer blocked; (3) FCV failed closed (pneumatic failure); (4) Suction bullet V-201 emptied; (5) Power failure; (6) Pipe rupture downstream |
| **Consequences** | Loss of ammonia feed → reactor stoichiometry upset → possible runaway; Pump runs dead-headed → pressure rise (deviation cascade); Tube-side cooling lost if E-201 shared |
| **Existing Safeguards** | Flow transmitter (FT-201) with **low-low alarm**; Pump motor ammeter; Suction low-pressure switch; **Independent low-flow interlock to trip pump**; Non-return valve on discharge |
| **Recommendations** | R1: Install **redundant low-flow switch** (SIL 2) independent of FT. R2: Verify **low-low trip tested every shift** and alarm acknowledged. R3: Add **mechanical seal flush flow interlock** to detect seal failure. |

---

##### **Deviation (ii): MORE PRESSURE (3.5 marks)**

| Item | Detail |
|------|--------|
| **Causes** | (1) FCV-201 failed closed while pump running; (2) Blockage in downstream reactor feed line; (3) Thermal expansion in isolated line section (e.g., pump off, ambient heat); (4) Oversized pump operating at shut-off head; (5) Simultaneous startup of parallel pump |
| **Consequences** | Pressure > 12 bar (design) → stress on piping/flanges; if > 14 bar → PSV-101 lifts and vents ammonia to atmosphere (highly toxic release, IDLH risk); if PSV fails → **catastrophic pipe rupture** (NH₃ is toxic and flammable above LEL 15%) |
| **Existing Safeguards** | **PSV-101 set at 14 bar** (verified, code-compliant per ASME); Pressure indicator (PI-201) with HI alarm at 13 bar; Pump discharge block valve with **car-seal open**; Pipe class PN-25 (25 bar rating) |
| **Recommendations** | R1: Install **dedicated pressure transmitter (PT) with high-high trip** at 13.5 bar to shutdown pump. R2: Verify **PSV-101 set pressure ≤ 0.9 × pipe MAWP** (per ASME B31.3). R3: Update **operating procedure** to require **car-seal open** discipline on block valve V-203. |

---

### **PART B — Question B** `[KTU University Exam – Jun 2024 Model Paper]`

**Q. (a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **significance of the HAZOP team composition** in detail. List the **disciplines** that must be present, the **role of the team leader**, and **why independence of the team leader is essential**.

**Q. (b) [7 Marks]** Differentiate between **HAZOP and FMEA** in terms of **methodology, application area, output, and team requirement**. Why is HAZOP preferred for the process industry?

---

### **Model Answer — Question B**

#### **Part (a) — HAZOP Team Composition (7 Marks)**

**Significance (2 marks):**
HAZOP is a **team-based technique** because:
- **No single engineer** holds complete process knowledge.
- Hazards often emerge at the **interface of disciplines** (e.g., an instrument failure becomes a process hazard).
- A diverse team **reduces blind spots** and validates assumptions.
- A team can **challenge design assumptions** constructively, leading to better solutions.

**Required Disciplines (2 marks):**
- **Team Leader / Facilitator** (independent, experienced)
- **Scribe / Recorder** (dedicated to documentation)
- **Process / Design Engineer** (explains design intent)
- **Operations / Plant Engineer** (field behavior, real operating issues)
- **Instrumentation & Control Engineer** (trips, interlocks, DCS)
- **Maintenance / Mechanical Engineer** (failure modes of equipment)
- **Safety / HSE Engineer** (risk matrix, regulatory compliance)
- **Specialists on rotation** — corrosion, electrical, civil, environmental

**Role of Team Leader (2 marks):**
- **Drives the study** with disciplined guide-word application.
- Ensures **every node is fully examined** (no premature closure).
- **Manages time** and team dynamics.
- **Records decisions** and unresolved items.
- Acts as the **interface to management** for the final report.
- Must have **facilitation skills, technical breadth, and IEC 61882 training**.

**Why Independence of the Team Leader is Essential (1 mark):**
- The team leader must be **impartial** — they are not emotionally or professionally invested in the design being studied.
- An **independent leader** can challenge the design engineer without conflict of interest.
- Independence ensures **unbiased identification of weaknesses**, which the project team might otherwise defend.
- Most regulatory bodies (HSE UK, OSHA PSM) **mandate** an independent leader for HAZOP.

---

#### **Part (b) — HAZOP vs FMEA (7 Marks)**

| Parameter | **HAZOP** | **FMEA (Failure Mode and Effects Analysis)** |
|-----------|-----------|----------------------------------------------|
| **Methodology** | Qualitative, guide-word-based systematic deviation analysis | Bottom-up, component-by-component failure mode listing |
| **Application Area** | **Process plants** (chemical, petrochemical, oil & gas) | **Mechanical/electrical systems** (machines, vehicles, electronics) |
| **Output** | Deviation worksheet + recommendations | Failure mode list with **Severity (S), Occurrence (O), Detection (D)** ratings → **RPN = S × O × D** |
| **Team Requirement** | Multi-disciplinary (5–7 members) | Smaller, design/quality-focused team (2–4 members) |
| **Node/Component Definition** | Process sections (nodes) | Individual components |
| **Hazard Identification Approach** | Top-down — what deviations are possible? | Bottom-up — what can fail? |
| **Quantitative Output** | Optional (qualitative risk matrix) | **RPN (Risk Priority Number)** is numerical |
| **Best Suited For** | Continuous/batch processes, pipelines, storage | Mechanical assemblies, automotive, aerospace |
| **Cost and Time** | Higher (multi-day workshop) | Lower (smaller team, shorter duration) |
| **Standards** | IEC 61882 | IEC 60812, SAE J1739, AIAG-VDA FMEA Handbook |

**Why HAZOP is Preferred for Process Industry (1 mark consolidated in table):**
- Process hazards often arise from **interaction of variables** (e.g., temperature × pressure) — HAZOP's parameter-based approach captures these systematically.
- FMEA can miss **deviation combinations** that HAZOP catches (e.g., "wrong composition" leading to "no reaction").
- HAZOP is **explicitly required** by most process safety regulations (e.g., OSHA 29 CFR 1910.119, EU Seveso III Directive, Indian MSIHC Rules).
- HAZOP identifies **operability problems** (production losses, quality issues) in addition to safety hazards — FMEA is mainly reliability-focused.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU 2024 HAZOP Questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing "node" with "equipment."** A node is a **section of the process with constant design intent**, not just a single equipment item. Examiners deduct 1 mark if this is misunderstood.
>
> 2. **Listing causes that are unrealistic.** "Act of God," "unknown," or "operator error" without specificity are **not acceptable causes**. Always give a **physical/mechanical reason** (e.g., "pump seal failure" not "pump failed").
>
> 3. **Mixing up guide words and parameters.** "NO FLOW" is correct; "NO PUMP" is wrong — the latter is a cause, not a deviation.
>
> 4. **Skipping the risk matrix.** Even if not asked, KTU expects $R = S \times L$ analysis for **each major deviation** in a 14-mark question.
>
> 5. **Recommendations that are vague.** "Improve safety" gets 0 marks. "Install SIL-2 high-pressure trip with 3-month deadline" gets full marks.
>
> 6. **Forgetting the team leader's independence requirement.** Examiners specifically test this in Part A and Part B.
>
> 7. **Not using standard guide words in order.** Examiners expect you to list them as: **NO, MORE, LESS, AS WELL AS, PART OF, REVERSE, OTHER THAN** (in this sequence).
>
> 8. **Treating HAZOP as a quantitative tool.** It is **qualitative** — no formulas except $R = S \times L$ for risk ranking.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — HAZOP Methodology**

- ✅ **Full Form:** Hazard and Operability Study.
- ✅ **Origin:** Developed at **ICI Heavy Organic Chemicals Division, UK, 1974**.
- ✅ **Standard:** **BS IEC 61882:2016** (KTU exam favorite).
- ✅ **Nature:** **Qualitative, structured, team-based, systematic**.
- ✅ **Primary Document:** **P&ID (Piping & Instrumentation Diagram)**.
- ✅ **Team Size:** **4 to 8** members (optimum 5–7).
- ✅ **Team Leader:** **Independent** of project team; trained in facilitation.
- ✅ **Scribe:** Dedicated recorder (not optional).
- ✅ **Node:** A logical process section with **constant design intent**; marked on P&ID.
- ✅ **Design Intent:** Stated **before** applying guide words at each node.
- ✅ **Guide Words (Standard 7):** **NO, MORE, LESS, AS WELL AS, PART OF, REVERSE, OTHER THAN**.
- ✅ **Process Parameters:** Flow, Pressure, Temperature, Level, Composition, Phase, Mixing, Time, Start/Stop.
- ✅ **Deviation Formula:** $\text{Deviation} = \text{Guide Word} + \text{Process Parameter}$.
- ✅ **Risk Score:** $R = S \times L$ where $S, L \in \lbrace 1,2,3,4,5 \rbrace$.
- ✅ **Output:** HAZOP Worksheet + HAZOP Report + Action Register.
- ✅ **Action Tracking:** All recommendations **assigned to owners with deadlines**; tracked to closure.
- ✅ **Re-HAZOP Triggered By:** Plant modifications, MOC (Management of Change), process upsets, regulatory updates.
- ✅ **Regulatory Requirement in India:** **MSIHC Rules, 1989** (Schedule 2 & 3 — threshold quantities of hazardous chemicals).
- ✅ **Global Regulatory Drivers:** OSHA 29 CFR 1910.119 (US PSM), EU **Seveso III Directive**, UK COMAH.
- ✅ **Common Mistake to Avoid:** HAZOP is **not FMEA**; the difference is HAZOP's **guide-word systematic approach** vs. FMEA's **component-failure bottom-up approach**.
- ✅ **Best Application Domain:** **Continuous and batch process plants** — refineries, petrochemicals, fertilizer, pharma, LNG, pipelines.
- ✅ **Kerala Industry Relevance:** FACT Kochi, BPCL Cochin, Ambalamugal Petrochemical Complex, IRE, Hindustan Newsprint — all require HAZOP per MSIHC Rules.
- ✅ **Estimated Study Time:** $T_{\text{HAZOP,total}} = 1.3 \times N_{\text{nodes}} \times t_{\text{node}}$ (including overhead).
- ✅ **Document Outcome:** **HAZOP register is a legal document** under PSM audits and is required to be **retained for the life of the plant**.

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Criticality Analysis
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAUT415 - INDUSTRIAL SAFETY ENGINEERING
> - **Course Code:** PEAUT415
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Criticality Analysis
> - **Cognitive Levels Covered:** L1 (Remember), L2 (Understand), L3 (Apply), L4 (Analyze)
> - **Mapped Course Outcomes:** CO4 (Apply hazard identification and risk assessment methodologies in process industries)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Criticality Analysis (CA)** is a systematic, quantitative, and qualitative engineering methodology used to prioritize and rank potential failure modes of a system, sub-system, or component based on the combined evaluation of the **severity** of their consequences, the **probability of occurrence**, and the **detectability** of the failure. When performed alongside Failure Mode and Effects Analysis (FMEA), the combined methodology is termed **FMECA (Failure Mode, Effects, and Criticality Analysis)** as standardized in **MIL-STD-1629**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** Criticality is defined as the *relative ranking* assigned to a failure mode that combines the severity of the failure effect with the frequency of its occurrence. Criticality Analysis converts qualitative failure data into a measurable numerical index used to allocate corrective action resources.

**Criticality** (noun): A numerical measure or category that quantifies the combined impact of a failure mode using the product of failure rate, conditional loss probability, and mission duration.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Terminology Distinction:**
> - **FMEA** = Identifies *what* can fail and *how*.
> - **Criticality Analysis** = Quantifies *how bad* and *how often*.
> - **FMECA** = FMEA + Criticality Analysis (the complete ranking system).

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Intuition

### 🏥 Hospital Emergency Triage Analogy

Imagine a hospital's emergency ward receiving **1,000 patients per day**. The doctors cannot attend to everyone simultaneously, so they apply **Triage** — a system of ranking patients based on three parameters:

| Triage Parameter | Hospital Equivalent | Criticality Analysis Equivalent |
|------------------|--------------------|---------------------------------|
| **Severity of Illness** | How critical is the patient's condition? (Heart attack vs. mild fever) | **Severity (S)** — Loss of life, injury, downtime |
| **Probability of Deterioration** | How likely is the patient to worsen without intervention? | **Occurrence (O)** — Failure rate α |
| **Ease of Diagnosis** | How easy is it to detect the illness early? | **Detectability (D)** — Ability of controls to catch the failure |

Just as a doctor sends a heart-attack patient to ICU *before* a patient with a sprained ankle, **Criticality Analysis forces safety engineers to fix the highest-ranked failure modes first**, even if many smaller failures exist.

### 🏭 Industrial Intuition

In a chemical plant, a relief valve may fail in many ways — *stuck open*, *stuck closed*, *chatter*, *leak*. Some failures (stuck closed during overpressure) can cause a **boiler explosion**, while others (slow leak) only cause **maintenance issues**. Criticality Analysis mathematically answers:

> *"Which failure mode deserves my engineering team's attention this month?"*

---

## 1.3 Physical Constants, Standard Metrics & Benchmark Values

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Criticality Metrics Used in KTU Exam Problems:**
> - **Failure rate (α)** → typically expressed in **failures per 10⁶ hours** (or per million mission cycles)
> - **Mission time (t)** → typically expressed in **hours** or **10⁶ hours**
> - **Conditional Probability (β)** → dimensionless, range **0 to 1**
> - **Severity Ranking (S)** → integer scale **1 to 10** (per AIAG-VDA FMEA)
> - **Occurrence Ranking (O)** → integer scale **1 to 10**
> - **Detection Ranking (D)** → integer scale **1 to 10**
> - **Criticality Number (CN)** → failures per 10⁶ hours

---

## 1.4 Syllabus Highlights & Module Mapping

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Module 4 Coverage of Criticality Analysis:**
> 1. Definition & objectives of Criticality Analysis
> 2. Difference between FMEA, CA, and FMECA
> 3. Quantitative Criticality Number (MIL-STD-1629 Procedure)
> 4. Qualitative Severity–Probability Matrix (Criticality Matrix)
> 5. Risk Priority Number (RPN) methodology
> 6. Industrial application and ranking procedure
> 7. Limitations of Criticality Analysis

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Fundamental Philosophy — The "Why" of Criticality Analysis

Criticality Analysis is built on three engineering truths:

1. **Not all failures are equal.** A minor oil leak costs ₹50,000; a reactor rupture costs ₹50 crores and human lives.
2. **Resources are finite.** A safety engineer cannot mitigate 500 failure modes simultaneously — prioritization is essential.
3. **Failure is probabilistic, not deterministic.** A valve may *potentially* fail; we quantify *how often* and *how severely*.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Core Question Criticality Analysis Answers:**
> *"Given a finite safety budget, which failure mode should be addressed FIRST to maximize the reduction in risk?"*

---

## 2.2 Operational Steps in Conducting Criticality Analysis

The following structured procedure is mandated by **MIL-STD-1629A** and adopted by KTU:

**Step 1 — System Definition**
- Define system boundaries, mission profile, operational modes, and environmental conditions.

**Step 2 — Block Diagram Construction**
- Develop functional block diagrams showing inter-dependencies and interfaces.

**Step 3 — Failure Mode Identification**
- List *every credible failure mode* for each component (typically using FMEA worksheets).

**Step 4 — Effect Determination**
- Trace each failure mode to its **local effect**, **next higher level effect**, and **end effect**.

**Step 5 — Severity Classification**
- Assign each failure effect to one of the four MIL-STD-1629 severity categories (I, II, III, IV).

**Step 6 — Probability Level Assignment**
- Assign each failure mode to a probability level (A, B, C, D, E) based on historical failure rate data.

**Step 7 — Criticality Calculation**
- Compute the **Criticality Number (CN)** or **Risk Priority Number (RPN)** for each failure mode.

**Step 8 — Ranking and Categorization**
- Rank failure modes in descending order of CN/RPN.

**Step 9 — Corrective Action and Re-evaluation**
- For each high-criticality mode, propose design changes, redundancy, or procedural controls.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet — The "Cheat Sheet" Table

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table consolidates **every formula** examinable under Criticality Analysis. Memorize these for KTU ESE.

| Sl. No. | Formula Name | Mathematical Expression | Variables & Units | Application |
|---------|--------------|--------------------------|-------------------|-------------|
| 1 | Criticality Number (CN) | $CN = \alpha \times \beta \times t$ | α = failure rate (failures / 10⁶ h); β = conditional loss probability (dimensionless, 0–1); t = mission time (10⁶ h) | Per-failure-mode ranking (MIL-STD-1629) |
| 2 | Total System Criticality | $C_{system} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} CN_i$ | n = number of failure modes | Whole-system risk aggregation |
| 3 | Failure Mode Criticality | $C_m = \alpha \times \beta \times t \times K$ | K = failure mode ratio (fraction of failures of that mode) | Mode-specific contribution |
| 4 | Risk Priority Number (RPN) | $RPN = S \times O \times D$ | S, O, D ∈ {1, 2, ..., 10} | Qualitative ranking (AIAG FMEA) |
| 5 | Failure Rate (per mode) | $\alpha_m = \alpha_{total} \times K_m$ | K_m = proportion of failures attributed to mode m | Disaggregation of total failure rate |
| 6 | Conditional Probability | $\beta = \dfrac{N_{loss-events}}{N_{total-failures}}$ | N = count of events | Empirical derivation |
| 7 | Hazard Score (HAZOP) | $Risk = Severity \times Probability$ | Categorical multipliers | Used in HAZOP criticality |
| 8 | Mean Time Between Failures | $MTBF = \dfrac{1}{\alpha}$ (when α in h⁻¹) | α = failure rate per hour | Reliability reference |
| 9 | System Availability | $A = \dfrac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR}$ | MTTR = Mean Time To Repair | Downtime-impact reference |
| 10 | Criticality Index (per unit) | $CI_i = \dfrac{CN_i}{\sum_{j} CN_j} \times 100 \vert \text{percent}$ | CN_i = criticality number of mode i | Percentage contribution |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Board Pitfall:** Students commonly confuse **RPN (qualitative, dimensionless)** with **Criticality Number (quantitative, has units of failures/10⁶ h)**. They are *not interchangeable*. RPN is a ranking tool; CN has physical engineering units.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Utility in Engineering Fields

| Industry | Application of Criticality Analysis |
|----------|-------------------------------------|
| **Petrochemical** | Ranking failure modes of pressure vessels, relief valves, distillation columns |
| **Aerospace** | Mission-critical component reliability (NASA uses FMECA on every spacecraft) |
| **Nuclear Power** | Safety-critical pump, valve, and control rod ranking (per IEEE 352) |
| **Automotive** | Design verification of brake systems, airbags, steering (per AIAG-VDA) |
| **Pharmaceutical** | Equipment qualification and process risk (per ICH Q9) |
| **Manufacturing** | Machine guarding, robotic cell safety (per ISO 12100) |

---

## 2.5 Critical Distinctions (High-Yield for KTU Viva)

> [!NOTE]
> **CA vs FMEA vs FMECA:**
> - **FMEA** → *Qualitative only*, identifies failure modes and effects.
> - **CA** → *Quantifies* the FMEA output into a numerical ranking.
> - **FMECA** → *Combines both* — the complete industrial methodology taught in KTU Module 4.

**Quantitative CA** uses MIL-STD-1629 criticality numbers with empirical failure-rate data. **Qualitative CA** uses a Severity–Probability Matrix (sometimes called a "Criticality Matrix") where each cell is labeled High/Medium/Low. The Quantitative method is preferred for **high-consequence industries** (nuclear, aerospace); the Qualitative method is used in **general manufacturing** where failure-rate data is scarce.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation: The Criticality Number (CN) — MIL-STD-1629 Method

### 3.1.1 Conceptual Foundation

The Criticality Number represents the **expected number of failures of a specific mode per 10⁶ operating hours** that would result in a defined severity consequence.

### 3.1.2 Component Definitions

Let:
- $\alpha$ = Total component failure rate (failures per 10⁶ hours)
- $K_i$ = Failure mode ratio for mode *i* (dimensionless, between 0 and 1, and $\sum K_i = 1$)
- $\beta_i$ = Conditional probability that failure mode *i* results in the specified severity category (dimensionless, 0–1)
- $t$ = Mission duration (in 10⁶ hours)
- $i$ = Index for the failure mode (1 to n)

### 3.1.3 Exhaustive Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1:** The expected number of total failures of the component during mission time $t$ is:

$$
N_{failures} = \alpha \times t
$$

This is because failure rate × time = expected cumulative failures (Poisson approximation).

**Step 2:** The expected number of failures of *mode i* (a specific mode like "stuck closed") is obtained by multiplying the total expected failures by the mode ratio $K_i$:

$$
N_{mode-i} = \alpha \times K_i \times t
$$

**Step 3:** Not every failure of mode *i* leads to a critical consequence. The fraction that does is the conditional probability $\beta_i$. Hence, the expected number of **critical-consequence failures** for mode *i* is:

$$
N_{critical, i} = \alpha \times K_i \times \beta_i \times t
$$

**Step 4:** By the MIL-STD-1629 convention, the failure rate $\alpha$ is already *mode-specific*, so we drop $K_i$. The standard **Criticality Number** is therefore:

$$
CN_i = \alpha_i \times \beta_i \times t
$$

where $\alpha_i$ is the **failure rate of mode i specifically** (already disaggregated).

**Step 5 (units check):**

$$
[CN] = \left[\dfrac{\text{failures}}{10^6 \, h}\right] \times [\text{dimensionless}] \times [10^6 \, h] = \text{failures}
$$

> So the CN gives the **expected number of failures of mode *i*** during mission time *t* that produce the specified criticality category. ✔ Units verified.

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Problem — KTU Standard Pattern

> [!NOTE]
> This problem pattern is **exactly** how KTU 2024 Scheme ESE questions are phrased.

### 📘 Problem Statement

A pressure relief valve on a pressurized storage tank has been studied under FMECA. The valve exhibits **four credible failure modes**. Data is tabulated below:

| Failure Mode | α (failures / 10⁶ h) | β (conditional prob.) | Mission Time t (h) |
|--------------|----------------------|------------------------|---------------------|
| F1: Fails to open on demand | 0.85 | 0.20 | 10,000 |
| F2: Opens prematurely (chatter) | 1.20 | 0.05 | 10,000 |
| F3: External leak | 0.40 | 0.10 | 10,000 |
| F4: Fails to reseat after opening | 0.30 | 0.15 | 10,000 |

**Required:**
**(a)** Calculate the Criticality Number for each failure mode.
**(b)** Rank them from most to least critical.
**(c)** Compute the total system criticality for the valve.

---

### 3.2.1 Part (a) — Criticality Number Calculation

**Convert mission time to 10⁶ hours:**

$$
t = 10{,}000 \; h = 10{,}000 \times 10^{-6} \times 10^{6} \; h = 0.01 \times 10^{6} \; h
$$

So $t = 0.01 \times 10^{6}$ hours.

**Mode F1 (Fails to open on demand):**

$$
CN_{F1} = \alpha \times \beta \times t
$$

$$
CN_{F1} = 0.85 \times 0.20 \times 0.01
$$

$$
CN_{F1} = 0.00170 \; \text{failures per mission}
$$

**Mode F2 (Opens prematurely — chatter):**

$$
CN_{F2} = 1.20 \times 0.05 \times 0.01
$$

$$
CN_{F2} = 0.00060 \; \text{failures per mission}
$$

**Mode F3 (External leak):**

$$
CN_{F3} = 0.40 \times 0.10 \times 0.01
$$

$$
CN_{F3} = 0.00040 \; \text{failures per mission}
$$

**Mode F4 (Fails to reseat):**

$$
CN_{F4} = 0.30 \times 0.15 \times 0.01
$$

$$
CN_{F4} = 0.00045 \; \text{failures per mission}
$$

---

### 3.2.2 Part (b) — Ranking

| Rank | Failure Mode | Criticality Number | Priority |
|------|--------------|---------------------|----------|
| 🥇 1 | F1 — Fails to open on demand | **0.00170** | Highest |
| 🥈 2 | F2 — Premature opening (chatter) | **0.00060** | Medium |
| 🥉 3 | F4 — Fails to reseat | **0.00045** | Medium-Low |
| 4 | F3 — External leak | **0.00040** | Lowest |

**Engineering Interpretation:** Mode **F1** (failure to open during overpressure) has the highest criticality because it directly defeats the safety function of the valve. It must be addressed first.

---

### 3.2.3 Part (c) — Total System Criticality

$$
C_{system} = \sum_{i=1}^{4} CN_i
$$

$$
C_{system} = 0.00170 + 0.00060 + 0.00045 + 0.00040
$$

$$
C_{system} = 0.00315 \; \text{critical failures per mission}
$$

**Probability the valve suffers at least one critical event during mission:**

$$
P(\text{critical event}) \approx 1 - e^{-C_{system}}
$$

$$
P \approx 1 - e^{-0.00315}
$$

$$
P \approx 1 - 0.99686 \approx 0.00314 \;\;\;\; \text{or}\;\;\;\; 0.314\%
$$

---

## 3.3 Worked Problem — Risk Priority Number (RPN) Method

### 📗 Problem Statement

An industrial gearbox is being evaluated. Three failure modes have been assessed by an engineering team:

| Failure Mode | Severity (S) | Occurrence (O) | Detection (D) |
|--------------|--------------|----------------|----------------|
| M1: Tooth breakage | 9 | 5 | 4 |
| M2: Bearing pitting | 6 | 7 | 6 |
| M3: Lubricant leakage | 4 | 8 | 3 |

**Required:** Calculate RPN for each, rank them, and recommend the highest-priority corrective action.

### 3.3.1 RPN Computations

**Mode M1:**

$$
RPN_{M1} = 9 \times 5 \times 4 = 180
$$

**Mode M2:**

$$
RPN_{M2} = 6 \times 7 \times 6 = 252
$$

**Mode M3:**

$$
RPN_{M3} = 4 \times 8 \times 3 = 96
$$

### 3.3.2 Ranking Table

| Rank | Mode | RPN | Severity Tier | Action Priority |
|------|------|-----|----------------|-----------------|
| 🥇 1 | M2 — Bearing pitting | **252** | High | Immediate redesign or improved lubrication |
| 🥈 2 | M1 — Tooth breakage | **180** | Very High (S=9) | Redesign tooth geometry; improve hardness |
| 🥉 3 | M3 — Lubricant leakage | **96** | Low | Routine maintenance scheduling |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Insight for KTU Exam:** *RPN alone can mislead.* Mode M1 has the highest severity (S = 9), but Mode M2 has the highest RPN. Best practice: **always look at Severity FIRST** for life-safety risks, then RPN. AIAG-VDA 2019 now recommends replacing RPN with **Action Priority (AP)** for this reason.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Severity vs Probability (Qualitative CA)

The **Criticality Matrix** (also called the *Severity–Probability Matrix*) is a qualitative alternative when historical failure data is unavailable.

| ↓ Probability \ → Severity | **Catastrophic (S=10)** | **Critical (S=7)** | **Marginal (S=4)** | **Minor (S=1)** |
|------------------------------|--------------------------|---------------------|---------------------|------------------|
| **Frequent (P=5)**           | **UNACCEPTABLE** 🔴      | UNACCEPTABLE 🔴     | UNDESIRABLE 🟠      | Acceptable w/ Review 🟡 |
| **Probable (P=4)**           | UNACCEPTABLE 🔴          | UNDESIRABLE 🟠      | UNDESIRABLE 🟠      | Acceptable 🟢 |
| **Occasional (P=3)**         | UNDESIRABLE 🟠           | UNDESIRABLE 🟠      | Acceptable w/ Review 🟡 | Acceptable 🟢 |
| **Remote (P=2)**             | Acceptable w/ Review 🟡  | Acceptable w/ Review 🟡 | Acceptable 🟢      | Acceptable 🟢 |
| **Improbable (P=1)**         | Acceptable 🟢            | Acceptable 🟢       | Acceptable 🟢      | Acceptable 🟢 |

**Decision rule:** Any failure mode falling in the 🔴 UNACCEPTABLE zone **must** be redesigned or eliminated before commissioning.

---

## 3.5 Derivation: Criticality Index (% Contribution)

For each failure mode *i*, the **percentage contribution** to total system criticality is:

$$
CI_i = \dfrac{CN_i}{C_{system}} \times 100 \mid \text{percent}
$$

**Applied to our relief valve example:**

$$
CI_{F1} = \dfrac{0.00170}{0.00315} \times 100 \approx 53.97 \mid \text{percent}
$$

$$
CI_{F2} = \dfrac{0.00060}{0.00315} \times 100 \approx 19.05 \mid \text{percent}
$$

$$
CI_{F3} = \dfrac{0.00040}{0.00315} \times 100 \approx 12.70 \mid \text{percent}
$$

$$
CI_{F4} = \dfrac{0.00045}{0.00315} \times 100 \approx 14.29 \mid \text{percent}
$$

**Interpretation:** Mode F1 alone contributes **~54%** of the system's total critical risk — making it the *Pareto-priority* target.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Criticality Analysis Workflow

The following Mermaid diagram depicts the **end-to-end procedural flow** of conducting Criticality Analysis in an industrial setting, following the MIL-STD-1629A workflow:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1["STEP 1: Define System Boundary and Mission Profile"] --> B1["STEP 2: Construct Functional Block Diagram"]
    B1 --> C1["STEP 3: List all Failure Modes (FMEA Worksheet)"]
    C1 --> D1["STEP 4: Determine Failure Effects (Local / Next-Level / End)"]
    D1 --> E1["STEP 5: Assign Severity Category I to IV"]
    D1 --> F1["STEP 6: Assign Probability Level A to E"]
    E1 --> G1["STEP 7: Calculate Criticality Number CN"]
    F1 --> G1
    G1 --> H1["STEP 8: Rank Failure Modes Descending by CN"]
    H1 --> I1{"STEP 9: Is CN above Acceptable Threshold?"}
    I1 -->|YES| J1["STEP 10: Initiate Corrective Action Design Change Redundancy Procedural Control"]
    I1 -->|NO| K1["STEP 11: Monitor and Review Periodically"]
    J1 --> L1["STEP 12: Recalculate CN Post Modification"]
    K1 --> L1
    L1 --> M1["STEP 13: Document Final FMECA Report"]

    style A1 fill:#1e3a8a,color:#ffffff
    style G1 fill:#b91c1c,color:#ffffff
    style H1 fill:#b91c1c,color:#ffffff
    style I1 fill:#f59e0b,color:#000000
    style J1 fill:#16a34a,color:#ffffff
    style M1 fill:#4b5563,color:#ffffff
```

---

## 4.2 Severity–Probability Criticality Matrix (Decision Map)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph axis["Coordinate Axes"]
        Xaxis["X-AXIS: SEVERITY Increasing Rightward"]
        Yaxis["Y-AXIS: PROBABILITY Increasing Upward"]
    end

    Q1["ZONE Q1 - LOW Severity HIGH Probability - ACCEPTABLE WITH REVIEW"] --> Q2["ZONE Q2 - HIGH Severity HIGH Probability - UNACCEPTABLE - MANDATORY REDESIGN"]
    Q2 --> Q3["ZONE Q3 - LOW Severity LOW Probability - ACCEPTABLE"]
    Q3 --> Q4["ZONE Q4 - HIGH Severity LOW Probability - UNDESIRABLE - MITIGATION REQUIRED"]

    style Q1 fill:#fde68a,color:#000000
    style Q2 fill:#dc2626,color:#ffffff
    style Q3 fill:#86efac,color:#000000
    style Q4 fill:#fb923c,color:#000000
```

---

## 4.3 Quantitative vs Qualitative Criticality Analysis Comparison Map

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R1["CRITICALITY ANALYSIS METHOD SELECTION"] --> R2{"Is Empirical Failure Rate Data Available?"}
    R2 -->|YES| R3["QUANTITATIVE PATH"]
    R2 -->|NO| R4["QUALITATIVE PATH"]

    R3 --> R5["Use MIL-STD-1629 Criticality Number CN equals alpha times beta times t"]
    R5 --> R6["Output: Numerical ranking with engineering units"]

    R4 --> R7["Use Severity Probability Matrix or RPN equals S times O times D"]
    R7 --> R8["Output: Categorical ranking High Medium Low"]

    R6 --> R9["Application: Nuclear, Aerospace, Petrochemical"]
    R8 --> R10["Application: General Manufacturing, Pharma, Construction"]

    style R1 fill:#1e3a8a,color:#ffffff
    style R2 fill:#f59e0b,color:#000000
    style R3 fill:#2563eb,color:#ffffff
    style R4 fill:#16a34a,color:#ffffff
    style R9 fill:#7c2d12,color:#ffffff
    style R10 fill:#14532d,color:#ffffff
```

---

## 4.4 Hierarchical Decomposition — FMECA Structure

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1["FMECA: FAILURE MODE EFFECTS AND CRITICALITY ANALYSIS"] --> S2["PHASE 1: FMEA COMPONENT"]
    S1 --> S3["PHASE 2: CA COMPONENT"]

    S2 --> S2A["Identify Failure Modes"]
    S2 --> S2B["Trace Failure Effects"]
    S2 --> S2C["Identify Causes"]
    S2 --> S2D["List Current Controls"]

    S3 --> S3A["Assign Severity S"]
    S3 --> S3B["Assign Occurrence O"]
    S3 --> S3C["Assign Detection D"]
    S3 --> S3D["Compute RPN or CN"]
    S3 --> S3E["Rank and Recommend Action"]

    style S1 fill:#1e40af,color:#ffffff
    style S2 fill:#3b82f6,color:#ffffff
    style S3 fill:#b91c1c,color:#ffffff
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question 1, May 2024 Style]**
>
> **Q1. Define Criticality Analysis. How does it differ from FMEA?**
>
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> *Criticality Analysis (CA) is a quantitative and qualitative procedure used to rank potential failure modes of a system based on the combined evaluation of the severity of failure effects and their probability of occurrence. While FMEA is purely qualitative — listing failure modes, their effects, and causes — Criticality Analysis goes further by assigning numerical values (e.g., Criticality Number or RPN) to enable objective prioritization. Together, FMEA + CA = FMECA, the complete methodology standardized under MIL-STD-1629.* **(3 marks)**

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question 2, Dec 2023 Style]**
>
> **Q2. State the formula for Criticality Number (CN) as per MIL-STD-1629 and explain each term.**
>
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> *The Criticality Number (CN) is given by:*
>
> $$CN = \alpha \times \beta \times t$$
>
> *where:*
> *• α = failure rate of the specific failure mode (failures per 10⁶ hours)*
> *• β = conditional probability that the failure mode results in the specified criticality consequence (dimensionless, 0–1)*
> *• t = mission time or operating duration (in 10⁶ hours)*
>
> *CN represents the expected number of failures of a specific mode per mission time that produce a critical consequence.* **(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> As per **KTU 2024 Scheme ESE regulations**, every Part B question features an **internal choice**. Two alternative question patterns are provided below. Choose **either Question A or Question B** to attempt.

---

### 📘 QUESTION A — (14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, May 2024 Style]**

**Q. (a)** Explain in detail the **Failure Mode, Effects, and Criticality Analysis (FMECA)** methodology with a neat flowchart. Discuss its advantages and limitations. **(7 marks)**

#### 📝 Model Solution for Q.(a):

**Definition (1 mark):** FMECA is an extension of FMEA that incorporates a Criticality Analysis step to numerically rank failure modes. It is documented in MIL-STD-1629A (US DoD, 1980) and is widely used in aerospace, nuclear, and process industries.

**Stages of FMECA (4 marks):**

1. **System Definition:** Establish system boundaries, functional requirements, mission profile, and operating environment.
2. **FMEA Phase:** Construct block diagrams; identify failure modes, effects (local, next-higher, end), causes, and existing controls.
3. **Criticality Phase:** Assign severity (S), occurrence (O), and detection (D) rankings; compute Criticality Number (CN) using $CN = \alpha \times \beta \times t$ or Risk Priority Number $RPN = S \times O \times D$.
4. **Ranking & Recommendation:** Rank failure modes by descending CN/RPN; propose corrective actions for high-criticality items.
5. **Documentation:** Generate the FMECA worksheet — a tabular record that becomes part of the system's safety case file.

**Advantages (1 mark):**
- Provides *quantitative* prioritization rather than subjective judgement.
- Directs resources to highest-risk failure modes (Pareto-optimal).
- Supports regulatory compliance (ISO 12100, OSHA, ASME).
- Facilitates communication between design, operations, and safety teams.

**Limitations (1 mark):**
- Requires accurate empirical failure-rate data (often unavailable for novel systems).
- Does not capture common-cause or cascading failures.
- Static snapshot — does not adapt to operational changes unless re-performed.

---

**Q. (b)** A hydraulic pump in a refinery has **three failure modes**. The data is tabulated below:

| Failure Mode | Failure Rate α (per 10⁶ h) | Conditional Probability β | Mission Time t (h) |
|--------------|-----------------------------|----------------------------|---------------------|
| P1: Shaft seizure | 0.50 | 0.30 | 8,000 |
| P2: Seal leakage | 1.20 | 0.10 | 8,000 |
| P3: Cavitation damage | 0.80 | 0.20 | 8,000 |

Calculate the **Criticality Number (CN)** for each mode, rank them, and compute the **Total System Criticality**. **(7 marks)**

#### 📝 Model Solution for Q.(b):

**Step 1 — Convert mission time to 10⁶ hours (1 mark):**

$$
t = 8{,}000 \; h = 0.008 \times 10^{6} \; h
$$

**Step 2 — Compute CN for each mode (3 marks):**

For P1 (shaft seizure):

$$
CN_{P1} = 0.50 \times 0.30 \times 0.008 = 0.00120
$$

For P2 (seal leakage):

$$
CN_{P2} = 1.20 \times 0.10 \times 0.008 = 0.00096
$$

For P3 (cavitation damage):

$$
CN_{P3} = 0.80 \times 0.20 \times 0.008 = 0.00128
$$

**Step 3 — Rank the failure modes (2 marks):**

| Rank | Mode | CN | Criticality Level |
|------|------|-----|--------------------|
| 1 | P3 — Cavitation damage | **0.00128** | Highest |
| 2 | P1 — Shaft seizure | **0.00120** | High |
| 3 | P2 — Seal leakage | **0.00096** | Moderate |

**Step 4 — Total System Criticality (1 mark):**

$$
C_{system} = 0.00120 + 0.00096 + 0.00128 = 0.00344 \; \text{critical failures per mission}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Pitfalls to Avoid:**
> 1. **Unit Conversion Mistake:** Most students forget to convert mission time to **10⁶ hours** and plug in raw hours (e.g., 8,000 instead of 0.008). This yields a CN that is **1,000,000× too large** — full marks lost. Always convert.
> 2. **Confusing α (per-mode) with α (total component rate):** MIL-STD-1629 expects α to be *mode-specific* (already disaggregated). Using total α gives an inflated CN.
> 3. **Forgetting β:** Some students set β = 1, assuming every failure is critical. β reflects the *conditional* probability and is typically much less than 1.
> 4. **Omitting ranking table:** Board examiners award partial credit for the ranking step; skipping it loses 1–2 marks.

---

### 📗 QUESTION B — (14 Marks) [ALTERNATIVE CHOICE]

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Dec 2023 Style]**

**Q. (a)** Discuss in detail the **Severity Categories and Probability Levels** used in Criticality Analysis as per **MIL-STD-1629**. Use a Severity–Probability matrix in your explanation. **(7 marks)**

#### 📝 Model Solution for Q.(a):

**Severity Categories (3 marks):**

The standard MIL-STD-1629A classifies failure effect severity into four categories:

| Category | Description | Example |
|----------|-------------|---------|
| **I — Catastrophic** | Loss of life, permanent disability, loss of system | Boiler explosion, reactor rupture |
| **II — Critical** | Severe injury, major occupational illness, significant system damage | Pipe rupture, toxic release |
| **III — Marginal** | Minor injury, minor occupational illness, minor system damage | Minor leak, partial shutdown |
| **IV — Minor** | Less than minor injury, system still operational | Cosmetic defect, slight vibration |

**Probability Levels (2 marks):**

| Level | Description | Failure Probability Range |
|-------|-------------|---------------------------|
| **A — Frequent** | High probability of occurrence | $\geq 0.20$ of system failures |
| **B — Reasonably Probable** | Moderate | $0.10$ to $0.20$ |
| **C — Occasional** | Low | $0.01$ to $0.10$ |
| **D — Remote** | Very low | $0.001$ to $0.01$ |
| **E — Extremely Unlikely** | Negligible | $< 0.001$ |

**Severity–Probability Matrix (2 marks):** A 4×5 grid is constructed mapping severity (rows) against probability (columns). Each cell is assigned an action priority: *Unacceptable (red), Undesirable (orange), Acceptable with Review (yellow), Acceptable (green)*. Any failure mode falling in the red zone requires **immediate design change** before commissioning.

---

**Q. (b)** Explain the **Risk Priority Number (RPN)** method. Compute the RPN for the following gearbox failure modes and recommend a corrective action strategy. **(7 marks)**

| Failure Mode | Severity (S) | Occurrence (O) | Detection (D) |
|--------------|---------------|----------------|----------------|
| G1: Gear tooth wear | 7 | 6 | 5 |
| G2: Bearing overheating | 8 | 4 | 6 |
| G3: Oil contamination | 5 | 7 | 4 |

#### 📝 Model Solution for Q.(b):

**RPN Definition (1 mark):**

$$
RPN = S \times O \times D
$$

where each parameter is rated on a **1 to 10 scale**.

**Computation (3 marks):**

For G1:

$$
RPN_{G1} = 7 \times 6 \times 5 = 210
$$

For G2:

$$
RPN_{G2} = 8 \times 4 \times 6 = 192
$$

For G3:

$$
RPN_{G3} = 5 \times 7 \times 4 = 140
$$

**Ranking (2 marks):**

| Rank | Mode | RPN | Recommended Action |
|------|------|-----|--------------------|
| 1 | G1 — Gear tooth wear | **210** | Redesign gear material; improve lubrication schedule |
| 2 | G2 — Bearing overheating | **192** | Install temperature sensors; improve cooling |
| 3 | G3 — Oil contamination | **140** | Improve filtration; routine oil analysis |

**Critical Discussion (1 mark):** Although G2 has the *highest severity (S = 8)*, its lower occurrence gives it a lower RPN than G1. Modern AIAG-VDA 2019 guidelines recommend supplementing RPN with an **Action Priority (AP)** ranking (High / Medium / Low) to avoid overlooking high-severity but low-occurrence events.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Pitfalls to Avoid:**
> 1. **Multiplying S, O, D correctly:** Common mistake is adding them instead of multiplying (gives a low number, indicates lack of understanding).
> 2. **Forgetting to recommend corrective action:** KTU board examiners require an explicit action for the highest-RPN mode.
> 3. **Not discussing limitations:** A complete answer must mention that RPN can mislead when severity is high but occurrence/detection are low — discuss briefly for full marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **📌 Rapid Revision Checklist — Criticality Analysis (Module 4)**

### 🔑 Core Concepts
- Criticality = quantitative + qualitative ranking of failure modes
- FMECA = FMEA + Criticality Analysis (the complete methodology)
- MIL-STD-1629A is the governing standard for quantitative CA
- AIAG-VDA FMEA Handbook (2019) is the modern automotive equivalent

### 📐 Must-Memorize Formulas
- $CN = \alpha \times \beta \times t$ (units: failures per mission, α in failures/10⁶ h, t in 10⁶ h)
- $C_{system} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} CN_i$
- $RPN = S \times O \times D$ (each ranked 1–10)
- $CI_i = \dfrac{CN_i}{C_{system}} \times 100 \mid \text{percent}$

### 🎯 Key Severity Categories (MIL-STD-1629)
- **I** Catastrophic → **II** Critical → **III** Marginal → **IV** Minor

### 🎲 Probability Levels
- **A** Frequent (≥0.20) → **B** Probable (0.10–0.20) → **C** Occasional (0.01–0.10) → **D** Remote (0.001–0.01) → **E** Improbable (<0.001)

### ⚠️ Common Pitfalls (Board-Exam Specific)
1. **Unit conversion:** Always convert mission time to 10⁶ hours.
2. **RPN vs CN:** They are NOT interchangeable — know the difference.
3. **β interpretation:** Conditional probability of *critical* consequence, not total failure probability.
4. **Severity override:** High-severity modes (S = 9, 10) should be addressed regardless of low RPN.
5. **Mode-specific α:** α must be per-mode, not total component rate (unless using $K_i$ disaggregation).

### 🏭 Industrial Applications
- Petrochemical → Pressure vessel ranking
- Nuclear → Pump/valve criticality per IEEE 352
- Aerospace → Mission-critical component FMECA (NASA standard)
- Automotive → Brake and airbag FMEA per AIAG-VDA

### 📚 Exam Strategy Tips
- For 3-mark questions: state definition + 1 distinguishing point + formula (if applicable).
- For 7-mark sub-parts: write the formula, show step-by-step substitution, present a ranking table, and add a one-line engineering recommendation.
- For 14-mark questions: structure as **(i) Theory (3–4 marks) → (ii) Numerical with table (3 marks) → (iii) Ranking + engineering interpretation (3 marks) → (iv) Conclusion/limitations (1 mark)**.

---

> **END OF KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10 NOTES**
> **Module 4 — Criticality Analysis | PEAUT415 — Industrial Safety Engineering**
> **Prepared for KTU 2024 Scheme B.Tech (NEP 2020 Aligned)**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Corrective Action and Follow-up; Control of Chemical Hazards
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAUT415 - INDUSTRIAL SAFETY ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Corrective Action and Follow-up; Control of Chemical Hazards

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Corrective Action and Follow-up — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Corrective Action (CA):** A structured, documented action taken to **eliminate the root cause** of a detected non-conformity, hazard, incident, or unsafe condition so as to **prevent recurrence**. It is the "Act" phase of the Deming Cycle (PDCA) and is a mandatory requirement of **ISO 45001:2018 Clause 10.2** and the **KTU Industrial Safety Engineering** curriculum.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Follow-up:** The verification, monitoring, and confirmation activity carried out **after** a corrective action has been implemented, to validate that the action has effectively eliminated the root cause, has not introduced new hazards, and that the system is being maintained.

### Conceptual Analogy (Plain-English Intuition)

Think of **Corrective Action** as what a doctor does after diagnosing a disease:

1. **Diagnosis** = Incident investigation / Hazard identification.
2. **Prescription** = The corrective action plan (e.g., "install a guard rail, retrain staff, replace the chemical").
3. **Follow-up (the next visit)** = The doctor checks the patient 30 days later — "Did the fever return? Are the symptoms gone? Are there side effects from the medicine?"

If the **follow-up** shows the same problem, the doctor (or safety officer) changes the treatment — this loop is the heart of **Continual Improvement**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Tip:** Examiners often ask students to *distinguish* between **Corrective Action** and **Preventive Action**. Remember the simple mnemonic: **"Corrective = Cure what HAS happened"** vs **"Preventive = Prevent what HAS NOT yet happened."**

---

## 1.2 Control of Chemical Hazards — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Chemical Hazard:** Any chemical, mixture, or substance in any physical form (solid, liquid, gas, vapor, mist, dust, fume, fiber) that has the potential to cause **harm to people, property, or the environment**. As per the **Globally Harmonized System (GHS)** and **OSHA Hazard Communication Standard (HCS 2012)**, chemical hazards fall into **three GHS hazard classes**: **Physical, Health, and Environmental**.

### Conceptual Analogy (Plain-English Intuition)

Imagine a chemical plant is a **kitchen**, and chemicals are the **ingredients** in your pantry. Some are harmless (salt), some are mildly dangerous (chili powder — irritant), and some are deadly (rat poison — toxic). 

A responsible cook would:
- **Label** every jar clearly (Hazard Communication / GHS Labels).
- **Store** the rat poison in a *locked, separate shelf* away from food (Segregation / Engineering Control).
- **Use** gloves when handling chili powder (PPE).
- **Ventilate** the kitchen by opening windows (Local Exhaust Ventilation).
- **Wash hands** after every task (Hygiene / Administrative Control).
- **Keep a fire extinguisher** handy in case oil catches fire (Emergency Control).

**That entire recipe IS the control of chemical hazards.**

> [!NOTE]
> **GHS Hazard Pictograms (Quick List for KTU):** 
> 1. Explosive • 2. Flammable • 3. Oxidizer • 4. Compressed Gas • 5. Corrosive • 6. Acute Toxicity (Skull & Crossbones) • 7. Health Hazard (Carcinogen/Mutagen) • 8. Environmental Hazard • 9. Irritant/Harmful.

### Key Standard Reference Metrics

- **TLV (Threshold Limit Value)** — set by **ACGIH** (American Conference of Governmental Industrial Hygienists). Units: **ppm** or **mg/m³**.
- **PEL (Permissible Exposure Limit)** — set by **OSHA** (Occupational Safety & Health Administration). Legally enforceable in the USA.
- **REL (Recommended Exposure Limit)** — set by **NIOSH** (National Institute for Occupational Safety and Health).
- **OEL (Occupational Exposure Limit)** — generic term used in EU/India (Factories Act 1948, Schedule II).
- **IDLH (Immediately Dangerous to Life or Health)** — concentration above which an unprotected worker should not be exposed.
- **LC₅₀ (Lethal Concentration 50%)** — concentration killing 50% of test animals (used for acute toxicity).
- **LD₅₀ (Lethal Dose 50%)** — dose killing 50% of test animals (mg/kg body weight).

All these are measured against a **Standard Reference Man** of body weight **70 kg** and breathing rate **20 breaths/min × 0.5 L/breath = 10 L/min** (light work).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Corrective Action Lifecycle (7 Logical Steps)

A KTU-compliant Corrective Action programme moves through the following structured logic:

1. **Hazard / Non-conformity Identification** — From incident report, near-miss, audit, inspection, worker complaint, or safety data sheet (SDS) review.
2. **Immediate Containment (Short-term CA)** — Stop the bleeding. Isolate the area, lock-out/tag-out, evacuate, neutralize.
3. **Root Cause Analysis (RCA)** — Apply 5-Why, Fishbone (Ishikawa), or Fault Tree to identify the *systemic* cause, NOT the *symptom*.
4. **Corrective Action Plan (CAP)** — Define WHAT, WHO, WHEN, WHERE, HOW, and the RESOURCES required.
5. **Implementation of CA** — Execute the plan; may be **Engineering CA** (design change), **Administrative CA** (procedure/SOP change), or **Behavioural CA** (training).
6. **Verification & Effectiveness Review (Follow-up)** — Re-inspect, re-measure, audit. Use measurable KPIs.
7. **Closure & Communication** — Document lessons learned, share with all stakeholders, update SOPs and SDSs, close the CAPA ticket.

### The "Why" Behind Each Step (KTU Favourite Question Area)

- **Why Containment first?** Because until the unsafe condition is isolated, every passing second multiplies the risk of harm. A 5-Why analysis is useless if a worker is bleeding to death.
- **Why RCA instead of blaming individuals?** Because **Heinrich's Domino Theory (1931)** and **Bird's Loss Causation Model (1974)** both prove that **88–95% of all industrial accidents are caused by unsafe conditions / system failures, not by worker carelessness**. Punishing workers fixes nothing.
- **Why Follow-up?** Because the classic definition of insanity is *"doing the same thing and expecting different results."* **James Reason's Swiss Cheese Model (1990)** tells us that controls have *latent failures*; without follow-up, the holes in the cheese re-align.

## 2.2 The Hierarchy of Controls (HoC) — NIOSH/OSH

This is the **single most important KTU model** for the entire question of *Control of Chemical Hazards*. From **most effective** to **least effective**:

| Rank | Control Level | Effectiveness | Chemical Hazard Example |
|------|---------------|---------------|--------------------------|
| 1 | **Elimination** | 100% | Stop using benzene; switch to aqueous-based cleaning. |
| 2 | **Substitution** | 80–95% | Replace toluene with xylene (less toxic); replace cyanide gold-plating with sulfide. |
| 3 | **Engineering Controls** | 50–80% | Local Exhaust Ventilation (LEV), enclosures, glove-boxes, interlocks, scrubbers, dilution ventilation. |
| 4 | **Administrative Controls** | 20–40% | Job rotation, SOPs, signage, training, exposure-time limits, hygiene facilities. |
| 5 | **PPE** (Personal Protective Equipment) | 10–20% | Respirators, chemical-resistant gloves (nitrile, butyl, neoprene), splash goggles, face shields, chemical aprons. |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Common Mistake:** Students write PPE first because it is the most visible. **PPE is the LAST line of defence.** Examiners deduct marks if you place PPE at the top. Always start with **Elimination**.

## 2.3 Routes of Chemical Entry into the Body

A chemical can harm a worker only if it enters the body. The four **routes of exposure** (memorize order):

1. **Inhalation** (most common, ~85% of industrial chemical exposures) — via lungs.
2. **Skin / Dermal Absorption** — solvents penetrate intact skin (e.g., DMF, aniline, phenols).
3. **Ingestion** (hand-to-mouth) — poor hygiene after handling.
4. **Injection** (rare) — high-pressure equipment puncturing skin with chemical.

## 2.4 Ventilation Engineering — Quantitative Foundation

**Local Exhaust Ventilation (LEV)** is the workhorse engineering control. Capture velocity must exceed the contaminant generation velocity to entrain the vapor/dust.

### Capture Velocity (V_c) Reference Table

| Contaminant Type | Required V_c (m/min) |
|------------------|----------------------|
| Vapors (low velocity) | **30 – 75** |
| Gases | **30 – 75** |
| Fine dusts (fume) | **75 – 150** |
| Coarse dusts | **150 – 300** |
| Hot contaminants / rising streams | **300 – 1500** |

### KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Symbolic Form | Units / Notes |
|---|------------------|---------------|----------------|
| 1 | **Volumetric Flow Rate** of LEV | $Q = V_c \times A$ | $Q$ in m³/min, $A$ = hood face area in m² |
| 2 | **Face Velocity** | $V_f = \dfrac{Q}{A}$ | Must be 0.5 – 1.0 m/s for typical fume hoods |
| 3 | **Duct Transport Velocity** | $V_t$ (fixed by contaminant class) | Vapors/gases ≈ **600 – 1200 m/min**; Dusts ≈ **1200 – 2000 m/min** |
| 4 | **Hagen-Poiseuille Duct Pressure Drop** | $\Delta P = \dfrac{8 \mu L Q}{\pi r^{4}}$ | $\mu$ = viscosity, $L$ = duct length, $r$ = radius. **Used to size fan power.** |
| 5 | **Static Fan Power** | $P = \dfrac{\Delta P \cdot Q}{\eta}$ | $\eta$ = fan efficiency (typically 0.6–0.8) |
| 6 | **Haber's Law (Inhalation Dose)** | $D = C \times t$ | $D$ = dose, $C$ = airborne concentration, $t$ = exposure time (min) |
| 7 | **TWA (Time-Weighted Average)** | $\text{TWA} = \dfrac{\sum C_i \, t_i}{\sum t_i}$ | Standard: 8-hour day, 40-hour week |
| 8 | **TLV-TWA vs TLV-STEL vs TLV-C** | — | TWA = long-term avg; STEL = 15-min short-term exposure limit; C = Ceiling (instantaneous). |
| 9 | **Dilution Ventilation Airflow** | $Q_{dil} = \dfrac{G}{C_{limit} - C_{bg}}$ | $G$ = generation rate (mg/s), $C_{bg}$ = background concentration |
| 10 | **Belt-Drive / Hood Entry Loss Factor** | $\Delta P_{entry} = \xi \cdot \dfrac{\rho V^{2}}{2}$ | $\xi$ = loss coefficient (≈ 0.25 for flanged entry) |
| 11 | **Conversion ppm ↔ mg/m³** | $1\ \text{ppm} = \dfrac{M}{24.45}\ \text{mg/m}^{3}$ | $M$ = molecular weight; 24.45 = molar volume at **25 °C, 1 atm** |
| 12 | **At 0 °C, 1 atm** | $1\ \text{ppm} = \dfrac{M}{22.4}\ \text{mg/m}^{3}$ | Use when ambient is 0 °C |
| 13 | **Fire Triangle Equivalent for Chemicals** | Fuel + Oxidizer + Ignition Source + **Uninhibited Chain Reaction** | Tetrahedron of fire (esp. hydrocarbons) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why 24.45?** At **25 °C** and **1 atm (101.325 kPa)**, 1 mole of any ideal gas occupies **24.45 L**. This is the IUPAC reference for converting between volumetric (ppm) and gravimetric (mg/m³) units of air contaminants.

## 2.5 Real-World Utility (Industry Perspective)

- The **Corrective Action & Follow-up** process is the **spinal cord of ISO 45001**, **ISO 14001**, and **OHSAS 18001** audits — every KTU student who later joins an EHS role will be writing CAPAs monthly.
- The **Hierarchy of Controls** is the basis for every **PSSR (Pre-Startup Safety Review)** in the **Process Safety Management (PSM)** standard, **29 CFR 1910.119 (OSHA)**.
- **Ventilation design** is the bread-and-butter of **industrial hygienists** and **HVAC safety engineers** — every spray-paint booth, welding shop, and acid bath in every Indian factory applies these formulas.
- **TLV/PEL values** drive **medical surveillance** under the **Indian Factories Act, 1948 (Section 91 & Schedule II)** for industries dealing with hazardous chemicals.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Worked Analytical Examples

## 3.1 Derivation: Conversion of ppm to mg/m³ (Haber's Law Applied to Units)

**Problem setup:** A worker is exposed to **Toluene (C₆H₅CH₃, M = 92.14 g/mol)** at a level of **50 ppm** for 8 hours. What is the concentration in mg/m³ at 25 °C?

### Step 1 — Start with the Ideal Gas Law

The molar volume of an ideal gas at 25 °C (298.15 K) and 1 atm:

$$
V_m = \dfrac{RT}{P}
$$

### Step 2 — Substitute Numerical Values

Using $R = 0.082057\ \text{L·atm/(mol·K)}$, $T = 298.15\ \text{K}$, $P = 1\ \text{atm}$:

$$
V_m = \dfrac{(0.082057)(298.15)}{1} = 24.466\ \text{L/mol}
$$

### Step 3 — Interpretation: 1 mole of toluene vapor occupies 24.466 L

Therefore, 1 m³ of air (1000 L) at 25 °C contains:

$$
N = \dfrac{1000\ \text{L}}{24.466\ \text{L/mol}} = 40.874\ \text{mol/m}^{3}
$$

### Step 4 — Convert 50 ppm to mole fraction, then to mol/m³

50 ppm = $50 \times 10^{-6}$ mole fraction:

$$
C_{mol} = 50 \times 10^{-6} \times 40.874 = 2.0437 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{mol/m}^{3}
$$

### Step 5 — Multiply by molecular weight to get mg/m³

$$
C_{mg} = 2.0437 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{mol/m}^{3} \times 92.14\ \text{g/mol} \times 1000\ \text{mg/g}
$$

$$
C_{mg} = 188.30\ \text{mg/m}^{3}
$$

### Step 6 — Cross-check using the shortcut formula

$$
C_{mg/m^3} = C_{ppm} \times \dfrac{M}{24.45}
$$

$$
C_{mg/m^3} = 50 \times \dfrac{92.14}{24.45} = 50 \times 3.7687 = 188.44\ \text{mg/m}^{3}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> Both methods agree to within 0.1% (small rounding differences). **Compare to OSHA PEL for Toluene = 200 ppm (8-hr TWA)**. The exposure is well within limits, but if a worker also has skin contact (Toluene defats the skin), the *combined* exposure is still significant.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example: LEV Duct Sizing for a Solvent Fume Hood

**Given:**
- Solvent (MEK) evaporation in a small laboratory hood.
- Capture velocity required: $V_c = 0.5\ \text{m/s}$ (= 30 m/min).
- Hood face area: $A = 0.6\ \text{m} \times 0.6\ \text{m} = 0.36\ \text{m}^2$.
- Duct diameter $D = 0.20\ \text{m}$ (radius $r = 0.10\ \text{m}$).
- Duct length $L = 8\ \text{m}$.
- Air viscosity $\mu = 1.81 \times 10^{-5}\ \text{Pa·s}$.
- Air density $\rho = 1.20\ \text{kg/m}^3$.
- Fan efficiency $\eta = 0.7$.

### Step 1 — Volumetric Flow Rate

$$
Q = V_c \times A = 0.5\ \text{m/s} \times 0.36\ \text{m}^2 = 0.18\ \text{m}^3/\text{s}
$$

Convert to m³/min:

$$
Q = 0.18 \times 60 = 10.8\ \text{m}^3/\text{min}
$$

### Step 2 — Duct Cross-sectional Area

$$
A_{duct} = \pi r^{2} = \pi (0.10)^{2} = 0.0314\ \text{m}^2
$$

### Step 3 — Duct Transport Velocity

$$
V_t = \dfrac{Q}{A_{duct}} = \dfrac{0.18}{0.0314} = 5.73\ \text{m/s}
$$

Converting: $5.73 \times 60 = 343.6\ \text{m/min}$. **Insufficient for MEK vapor** (need 600–1200 m/min). **So a larger duct or lower flow is needed.** Let us increase duct diameter to $D = 0.30\ \text{m}$.

### Step 4 — Recalculate with $D = 0.30$ m

$$
A_{duct} = \pi (0.15)^{2} = 0.0707\ \text{m}^2
$$

$$
V_t = \dfrac{0.18}{0.0707} = 2.546\ \text{m/s} = 152.8\ \text{m/min}
$$

**Still too low.** We must either **increase $Q$** or **reduce duct size to ~ 0.10 m**, or use a **push-pull system**.

Let us try $D = 0.10\ \text{m}$ (which gives $r = 0.05\ \text{m}$):

$$
A_{duct} = \pi (0.05)^{2} = 7.854 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{m}^2
$$

$$
V_t = \dfrac{0.18}{7.854 \times 10^{-3}} = 22.9\ \text{m/s} = 1375\ \text{m/min}
$$

**Acceptable for vapor (600–1200 m/min)** — at the upper end, but within design limits.

### Step 5 — Pressure Drop (Hagen-Poiseuille)

$$
\Delta P = \dfrac{8 \mu L Q}{\pi r^{4}}
$$

$$
\Delta P = \dfrac{8 \times (1.81 \times 10^{-5}) \times 8 \times 0.18}{\pi \times (0.05)^{4}}
$$

Numerator: $8 \times 1.81 \times 10^{-5} \times 8 \times 0.18 = 2.085 \times 10^{-4}$

Denominator: $\pi \times 6.25 \times 10^{-6} = 1.963 \times 10^{-5}$

$$
\Delta P = \dfrac{2.085 \times 10^{-4}}{1.963 \times 10^{-5}} = 10.62\ \text{Pa}
$$

### Step 6 — Fan Power

$$
P_{shaft} = \dfrac{\Delta P \cdot Q}{\eta} = \dfrac{10.62 \times 0.18}{0.7} = 2.73\ \text{W}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> For a real duct with fittings, add 25% safety margin → ~3.4 W. This is the LEV fan motor rating.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example: Corrective Action — Root Cause Analysis (5-Why Method)

**Incident:** Worker slips on oil near machine M-12, fractures wrist.

- **Why 1:** Why did the worker slip? → Oil was on the floor.
- **Why 2:** Why was oil on the floor? → Machine M-12 leaked from the gearbox seal.
- **Why 3:** Why did the gearbox seal leak? → Seal was not replaced during last maintenance (overdue by 3 months).
- **Why 4:** Why was maintenance overdue? → Maintenance schedule had no asset register entry for M-12 seals.
- **Why 5:** Why was the asset register incomplete? → No CMMS (Computerized Maintenance Management System) had been rolled out to this shop floor.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Root Cause:** *Absence of a Computerized Maintenance Management System (CMMS) with seal replacement tracking.* 
> The **immediate cause** (oil on floor) and the **direct cause** (seal failure) are **NOT** the root cause. **KTU examiners award full marks only if you reach a system-level answer, not "worker should be careful."**

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Mapping Hazards → Controls → KPIs

| Step | Hazard Identified | Engineering Control | Admin Control | PPE | KPI for Follow-up |
