|------|-------------------|--------------------|---------------|------|-------------------|
| 1 | Toluene vapor | LEV with carbon filter | SOP + medical surveillance | Half-mask respirator (NIOSH N95 not sufficient) | TWA < 50 ppm |
| 2 | HF acid splash | Eyewash within 10 m + drip trays | Buddy system + HF training | Butyl gloves, face shield | 100% eyewash-tested weekly |
| 3 | Chlorine gas leak | Gas detector + auto-shutoff valve | Emergency drill quarterly | SCBA | Detector calibration 100% |
| 4 | Asbestos dust | Full enclosure + HEPA | Restricted access + sign-in | P3 respirator + decon suit | Air < 0.1 f/cc |
| 5 | Cyanide (NaCN) | Closed reactor + scrubber | Two-person rule for handling | Full chemical apron + SCBA | HCN in air < 1 ppm |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Corrective Action & Follow-up Loop (Block Architecture Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[ID1 Hazard or Incident Detected] --> B[ID2 Immediate Containment Short Term CA]
    B --> C[ID3 Root Cause Analysis 5 Why or Fishbone]
    C --> D[ID4 Corrective Action Plan CAP]
    D --> E[ID5 Implement CA Engineering Admin or Behavioural]
    E --> F[ID6 Verification and Effectiveness Review]
    F --> G{ID7 Effective}
    G -->|Yes| H[ID8 Close CAPA Update SOPs and SDSs]
    G -->|No| C
    H --> I[ID9 Continual Improvement Feed Back to PDCA Act Phase]
    I --> A
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The arrow from **G → C** is the "failed verification" loop — if the CA is ineffective, the system *automatically* re-enters Root Cause Analysis. This is the essence of **ISO 45001 Clause 10.2 — Continual Improvement**.

---

## 4.2 Hierarchy of Controls — Mermaid Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    L1[LEVEL 1 ELIMINATION Remove the Hazard] --> L2[LEVEL 2 SUBSTITUTION Replace with Less Hazardous]
    L2 --> L3[LEVEL 3 ENGINEERING CONTROLS LEV Enclosure Interlocks]
    L3 --> L4[LEVEL 4 ADMINISTRATIVE CONTROLS SOP Training Signage]
    L4 --> L5[LEVEL 5 PPE Respirator Gloves Goggles Apron]
    L5 --> L0[WORKER]
    L1 --> L0
    L2 --> L0
    L3 --> L0
    L4 --> L0
```

> [!NOTE]
> The arrows show that **all five levels** ultimately reach the worker, but with *decreasing effectiveness*. Notice the inverted pyramid intuition — the **broad base (PPE)** is the *weakest*, while the **apex (Elimination)** is the *strongest*. KTU often asks students to draw this diagram — keep it in your answer script.

---

## 4.3 Routes of Chemical Entry into the Human Body

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph EX[EXPOSURE SOURCE]
        S1[Vapor]
        S2[Liquid Splash]
        S3[Dust]
        S4[Pressurized Jet]
    end
    subgraph RT[ROUTES OF ENTRY]
        R1[INHALATION 85 percent]
        R2[DERMAL ABSORPTION]
        R3[INGESTION Hand to Mouth]
        R4[INJECTION High Pressure]
    end
    subgraph ORG[TARGET ORGANS]
        T1[Lungs Blood Brain]
        T2[Liver Kidney Skin]
        T3[GI Tract]
        T4[Local Tissue Muscle]
    end
    S1 --> R1
    S2 --> R1
    S2 --> R2
    S3 --> R1
    S3 --> R2
    S3 --> R3
    S4 --> R4
    R1 --> T1
    R2 --> T2
    R3 --> T3
    R4 --> T4
```

---

## 4.4 Local Exhaust Ventilation (LEV) System — Block Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[HAZARD SOURCE e.g. Solvent Tank] --> B[HOOD OR ENCLOSURE Capture Velocity Vc]
    B --> C[DUCT Transport Velocity Vt]
    C --> D[AIR CLEANER Filter Scrubber or Cyclone]
    D --> E[FAN Motor with Drive]
    E --> F[STACK Discharge Height above Roofline]
    B -.Velocity Check.-> G[VELOMETER FACE VELOCITY READOUT]
    C -.Velocity Check.-> G
    D -.Pressure Drop.-> H[MAGNEHELIC GAUGE delta P]
    E -.Power.-> I[MOTOR LOAD METER]
    F --> J[SAFE ATMOSPHERIC DISCHARGE]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The dotted arrows in this diagram represent **measurement points** for the **Follow-up** phase. **KTU design question:** A LEV system is only "corrective action complete" when **all four** monitoring points (face velocity, duct velocity, pressure drop, motor load) are logged weekly for 6 consecutive months and trending within ±10% of design values.

---

## 4.5 CAPA Process — 7-Step Block Flow (KTU Favourite)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[STEP 1 IDENTIFY Non Conformity] --> P2[STEP 2 CONTAIN Isolate]
    P2 --> P3[STEP 3 INVESTIGATE 5 Why or Fishbone]
    P3 --> P4[STEP 4 PLAN Corrective Action with SMART Targets]
    P4 --> P5[STEP 5 IMPLEMENT Engineering Admin or Training]
    P5 --> P6[STEP 6 VERIFY Effectiveness Audit and Re Measure]
    P6 --> P7[STEP 7 CLOSE Documentation and Communication]
    P7 --> P1
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Question]**
*Define "Corrective Action" and "Follow-up" in the context of an Occupational Health and Safety Management System. State ONE difference between corrective action and preventive action.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Corrective Action (1.5 marks):** A documented action taken to eliminate the **root cause** of a detected non-conformity, hazard, or incident so as to prevent recurrence. It addresses problems that **have already occurred** and is mandated under **ISO 45001:2018, Clause 10.2**.
- **Follow-up (1 mark):** The verification activity performed **after** the corrective action has been implemented, to confirm its effectiveness, sustainability, and absence of unintended side effects.
- **Difference (0.5 mark):** Corrective Action cures an *existing* problem; Preventive Action anticipates and forestalls a *potential* problem. (Mnemonic: Corrective = Cure; Preventive = Pre-empt.)

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Retest Pattern]**
*List the FIVE levels of the Hierarchy of Controls in their correct order of effectiveness (most effective first). Name the engineering control used to remove solvent vapors from a workplace.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **Elimination** — 1 mark
2. **Substitution** — included
3. **Engineering Controls** — included
4. **Administrative Controls** — included
5. **Personal Protective Equipment (PPE)** — 1 mark

(All five in correct order for 2 marks total; the **engineering control** for solvent vapors is **Local Exhaust Ventilation (LEV)** with a vapour scrubber or activated-carbon adsorber for 1 mark.)

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Module Internal Choice Questions

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 4 Pattern]**

**(a) (7 Marks)** Explain in detail the **seven-step Corrective Action and Follow-up process** as required by **ISO 45001:2018 Clause 10.2**. State clearly how *root cause analysis* differs from *blaming the worker* and illustrate your answer with a worked 5-Why example for a chemical-burn incident involving Sodium Hydroxide (NaOH).

**(b) (7 Marks)** A worker in a metal-finishing shop is exposed to **Chromic Acid (CrO₃, M = 100 g/mol)** mist. The 8-hr TWA measured is **0.08 mg/m³**. The OSHA PEL is **0.1 mg/m³ (TWA-TLV)**. The shop is **40 m × 25 m × 6 m**. The **Local Exhaust Ventilation (LEV)** has a hood face area of **0.5 m²** and a duct diameter of **0.15 m**. Determine:
- (i) Whether the exposure exceeds the PEL.
- (ii) The required volumetric flow rate for a capture velocity of **1.0 m/s**.
- (iii) The duct transport velocity achieved.
- (iv) Whether the duct transport velocity is acceptable for acid mist (assume required = **1200 m/min**).
- (v) Recommend THREE corrective actions.

---

### Model Solution — Question A

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks Model Answer

| # | Step | Explanation | Marks |
|---|------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | **Identification of non-conformity** | From incident report, near-miss, audit, worker complaint, inspection. | 1 |
| 2 | **Immediate containment** | Isolate the area; first-aid; neutralize NaOH spill with dilute acetic acid; lock-out machine. | 1 |
| 3 | **Root cause analysis** | 5-Why, Fishbone (Ishikawa 6M: Man, Machine, Material, Method, Measurement, Mother-Nature), or Fault Tree. | 1 |
| 4 | **Corrective action plan (CAP)** | SMART goals; WHAT, WHO, WHEN, WHERE, HOW, RESOURCES. | 1 |
| 5 | **Implementation** | Engineering / Administrative / Behavioural. | 1 |
| 6 | **Follow-up (Verification)** | Re-measure, re-audit, KPI monitoring, observe 30-90 days. | 1 |
| 7 | **Closure & continual improvement** | Update SOPs, communicate lessons, file CAPA. | 0.5 |
| | **Why RCA ≠ Blame** | Heinrich's Domino Theory shows 88% accidents are system-induced, not worker-induced. Blame fixes nothing. | 0.5 |

**Worked 5-Why Example (included for full marks):**
- **Incident:** Worker suffers chemical burn to forearm from NaOH splash.
- **Why 1:** NaOH splashed on arm. → *No face shield worn.*
- **Why 2:** Worker had no face shield. → *PPE not enforced in that section.*
- **Why 3:** Why was PPE not enforced? → *No supervisor present during late shift.*
- **Why 4:** Why no supervisor? → *Shift roster was short-staffed for 2 weeks.*
- **Why 5:** Why short-staffed? → *HR had frozen recruitment 3 months earlier due to cost-cut.*
- **Root Cause:** *HR cost-cutting without safety risk assessment; no automated PPE-detection interlock.*

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks Model Solution

**(i) Exposure vs PEL**

Given: $C_{measured} = 0.08\ \text{mg/m}^{3}$, PEL = $0.1\ \text{mg/m}^{3}$.

Since $0.08 < 0.1$, the **exposure is within the PEL.** However, Cr(VI) is a known **human carcinogen (GHS Category 1A)**, so **ALARP** (As Low As Reasonably Practicable) principle still demands reduction. (1 mark)

**(ii) Volumetric Flow Rate**

$$
Q = V_c \times A = 1.0\ \text{m/s} \times 0.5\ \text{m}^{2} = 0.5\ \text{m}^{3}/\text{s}
$$

Converting: $Q = 0.5 \times 60 = 30\ \text{m}^{3}/\text{min}$ (1 mark)

**(iii) Duct Transport Velocity**

Duct radius $r = 0.075\ \text{m}$. Duct cross-section:

$$
A_{duct} = \pi (0.075)^{2} = 1.767 \times 10^{-2}\ \text{m}^{2}
$$

$$
V_t = \dfrac{Q}{A_{duct}} = \dfrac{0.5}{1.767 \times 10^{-2}} = 28.3\ \text{m/s} = 1698\ \text{m/min}
$$

(1.5 marks)

**(iv) Acceptability**

Required: 1200 m/min. Achieved: 1698 m/min. **Acceptable** (within 1200–2000 m/min range for acid mist). (1 mark)

**(v) Three Corrective Actions** (1.5 marks — 0.5 each)

1. **Engineering:** Add a **second LEV hood** near the chromic acid bath and install a **mist-eliminator (demister pad)** in the duct to capture sub-micron CrO₃ droplets.
2. **Administrative:** Implement a **medical surveillance programme** per **OSHA 29 CFR 1910.1026** — annual nasal/skin examination and urine chromium testing. Update SOP to enforce **PPE checklist** before entry.
3. **PPE:** Upgrade PPE from splash goggles to a **full-face chemical shield + acid-vapor respirator (NIOSH P100 with acid-gas cartridge)**. Provide **butyl-rubber gloves (0.4 mm)** instead of nitrile.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:**
> - **Do NOT** confuse *duct transport velocity* with *capture velocity*. They are different — capture is at the hood face, transport is inside the duct.
> - **Do NOT** skip the units conversion. Most students lose 1–2 marks by writing $Q = 0.5$ without converting to m³/min.
> - **Do NOT** conclude "exposure is safe" just because it is below PEL. **Cr(VI) is carcinogenic; always invoke ALARP.**
> - For root cause analysis, **do not stop at "worker carelessness."** That is an *immediate cause*, not a *root cause*. Examiners give **zero** marks for a blame-based answer.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — [Alternative Internal Choice]**

**(a) (7 Marks)** Discuss in detail the **Hierarchy of Controls** for chemical hazards. With the aid of a neat sketch, explain the operation of a **Local Exhaust Ventilation (LEV)** system. List **FIVE** design checks an EHS officer must perform during **Follow-up** of an LEV system.

**(b) (7 Marks)** A storage shed contains **five chemicals**:
- (i) Acetone (Flammable, BP 56 °C)
- (ii) Concentrated Sulphuric Acid (Oxidizer + Corrosive)
- (iii) Sodium Hypochlorite (Oxidizer)
- (iv) Ammonium Nitrate (Oxidizer + Explosive when heated)
- (v) Toluene (Flammable, BP 110 °C)

Explain the **incompatibility matrix** that dictates their storage. Outline the **correct segregation plan** using distance, containment, and cabinet principles. List the **GHS pictogram** for each.

---

### Model Solution — Question B (Key Points)

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks Model Answer

**Hierarchy of Controls (5 levels, 2 marks):**
Elimination → Substitution → Engineering → Administrative → PPE.

**LEV System Operation (3 marks):**
- **Capture** at the hood (velocity ≥ 0.5 m/s for vapor).
- **Transport** through duct at 600–2000 m/min.
- **Cleaning** via scrubber, cyclone, or HEPA filter.
- **Discharge** via fan to a safe stack above roofline.
- **Diagram:** Replicate the **Section 4.4 Mermaid diagram** by hand (use a hood → duct → filter → fan → stack flowchart).

**Five Follow-up Design Checks (2 marks — 0.4 each):**
1. **Face velocity** at the hood with a vane anemometer.
2. **Duct transport velocity** using a pitot tube.
3. **Filter pressure drop** with a magnehelic gauge (replace if ΔP > 250 Pa for typical bag filter).
4. **Fan motor amperage** to detect bearing wear.
5. **Stack discharge opacity** (Ringelmann test) and **discharge concentration** below local PCB standards.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks Model Answer

**Incompatibility Matrix (3 marks):**

| Chemical | Acetone | H₂SO₄ | NaOCl | NH₄NO₃ | Toluene |
|----------|:-------:|:-----:|:-----:|:-------:|:-------:|
| Acetone | — | ✗ | ✗ | ✗ | ✗ |
| H₂SO₄ | ✗ (fire) | — | ✗ (Cl₂ gas) | ✗ (explosive) | ✗ (fire) |
| NaOCl | ✗ (fire) | ✗ | — | ✗ (NCl₃) | ✗ |
| NH₄NO₃ | ✗ | ✗ | ✗ | — | ✗ |
| Toluene | ✗ | ✗ | ✗ | ✗ | — |

All five chemicals are **mutually incompatible** with at least one other in the shed. (Acetone and Toluene are *both* Class IB flammables, so they can be co-stored, but **not with the oxidizers** H₂SO₄, NaOCl, NH₄NO₃.)

**Correct Segregation Plan (3 marks):**
1. **Cabinet 1 (Yellow, Flammable, FM-approved):** Acetone + Toluene together (compatible). Store ≤ 60 L.
2. **Cabinet 2 (Polyethylene, Corrosive cabinet with vent):** H₂SO₄ only, on **lowest shelf**, in secondary containment tray.
3. **Cabinet 3 (Locked, Oxidizer, with Explosion-Proof electrical):** NaOCl + NH₄NO₃, separated by a **fire-resistant wall (≥ 2-hour rating)** from the flammable cabinet.
4. **Minimum separation distance** between flammable and oxidizer cabinets: **6 m** (per **NFPA 30**).
5. **Ventilation:** Mechanical exhaust at **6 air-changes/hour**, floor-level for H₂SO₄ (heavier vapor), ceiling for acetone/toluene.

**GHS Pictograms (1 mark — 0.2 each):**
- Acetone: **Flame**
- H₂SO₄: **Corrosion + Flame over Circle (Oxidizer)**
- NaOCl: **Flame over Circle (Oxidizer) + Corrosion**
- NH₄NO₃: **Flame over Circle (Oxidizer) + Explosive (unstable)**
- Toluene: **Flame + Health Hazard + Exclamation Mark**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning (Question B):**
> - **Never** co-store **H₂SO₄ + NaOCl** — it generates **chlorine gas** (used in WWI!). Examiners love this question.
> - **Never** co-store **NH₄NO₃ + any fuel** — the **Beirut 2020 explosion** is a classic real-world example.
> - For storage questions, the order of the cabinet is not enough — students lose marks by **not mentioning the secondary containment tray and ventilation rate**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **LAST-MINUTE KTU REVISION BULLET POINTS — memorize these before entering the exam hall:**

- ✅ **Corrective Action** ≠ **Preventive Action** — Cure vs Pre-empt.
- ✅ **ISO 45001 Clause 10.2** mandates: Document non-conformity → Investigate root cause → Plan CA → Implement → Verify effectiveness → Update OH&S risks & opportunities.
- ✅ **Hierarchy of Controls** mnemonic: **"Every Safe Admin Protects People"** → **E**limination, **S**ubstitution, **E**ngineering, **A**dministrative, **P**PE.
- ✅ **5-Why** requires reaching a **systemic, third-party answer** — never stop at the worker.
- ✅ **4 Routes of entry**: Inhalation (85%), Dermal, Ingestion, Injection.
- ✅ **Conversion formula:** $\text{mg/m}^{3} = \text{ppm} \times \dfrac{M}{24.45}$ (at 25 °C).
- ✅ **Haber's Law:** $D = C \times t$ — Dose is product of concentration and time.
- ✅ **GHS has 9 pictograms** — Flammable, Corrosive, Toxic, Explosive, Oxidizer, Compressed Gas, Health Hazard, Environment, Irritant.
- ✅ **LEV** must satisfy: Capture velocity + Transport velocity + Filter ΔP + Stack discharge height.
- ✅ **Acid + Hypochlorite → Chlorine gas** (lethal).
- ✅ **NH₄NO₃ + Heat/Contamination → Explosive** (Beirut, Texas City 1947).
- ✅ **OSHA PEL** is legally enforceable; **TLV** is advisory; **REL** is NIOSH recommendation; **WEEL** is Workplace Environmental Exposure Level.
- ✅ **Follow-up** KPIs: TWA ppm, TLV-C, TLV-STEL, filter ΔP, LEV face velocity, SCBA cylinder pressure, eyewash-test logs.
- ✅ **PDCA** = Plan → Do → Check → Act. Corrective action is the **Act** phase.
- ✅ **James Reason's Swiss Cheese Model** justifies why multiple layers of control + Follow-up are essential.
- ✅ **ALARP** — As Low As Reasonably Practicable — especially for carcinogens like **Cr(VI), Benzene, Asbestos**.
- ✅ **Storage cabinets:** Yellow = Flammable, Blue = Pesticide, White = Acid, Green = Alkali, Red = Flammable Liquid (older coding).
- ✅ **Glove compatibility chart** — Nitrile (oils, fuels), Butyl (ketones, esters), Neoprene (acids, bases), Viton (chlorinated solvents). Wrong glove = NO protection.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Hazardous Properties of Chemicals; Material Safety Data Sheets (MSDS)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAUT415 - INDUSTRIAL SAFETY ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Hazardous Properties of Chemicals; Material Safety Data Sheets (MSDS)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Hazardous Properties of Chemicals — Formal Definition

A **hazardous chemical** is any substance (solid, liquid, or gas) that, by virtue of its intrinsic physical, chemical, toxicological, or environmental properties, has the potential to cause harm to human life, property, livestock, or the ecological balance of the surroundings during industrial handling, processing, transportation, storage, or disposal.

As per the **Occupational Safety and Health Administration (OSHA) Hazard Communication Standard (HCS) — 29 CFR 1910.1200**, hazardous chemicals are classified based on their inherent dangerous characteristics, which are broadly grouped into **four primary hazard categories**:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Four Pillars of Chemical Hazard Classification (OSHA HCS / GHS Framework)**
> 1. **Physical Hazards** — Properties related to flammability, reactivity, explosivity, and oxidation.
> 2. **Health Hazards** — Properties causing acute or chronic toxicity, carcinogenicity, mutagenicity, or reproductive toxicity.
> 3. **Environmental Hazards** — Properties that pose threats to aquatic life, atmosphere (Ozone Depletion Potential), and soil.
> 4. **Target Organ & Systemic Hazards** — Specific systemic toxicity to liver, kidney, CNS, blood, etc.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

**Analogy — "The Dangerous Toolbox":**
Imagine a mechanic's toolbox. Most tools (hammers, wrenches) are useful but can hurt you if mishandled. Now imagine a toolbox that contains tools that are *poisoned, explosive, or corrosive* — each tool is still useful, but the consequences of misuse are catastrophic.

- The **hammer** = harmless if used carefully (a benign chemical like water).
- The **acid bottle** = corrodes skin and metals (a corrosive chemical like Sulfuric Acid, H₂SO₄).
- The **gasoline can** = catches fire instantly (a flammable chemical like Acetone).
- The **radioactive badge** = invisible long-term harm (a chronic toxicant like Benzene).

Just as a responsible mechanic would label each dangerous tool, the **MSDS** is the universal "instruction manual" that tells workers and emergency responders exactly what kind of danger each chemical poses and how to handle it safely.

## 1.3 What is a Material Safety Data Sheet (MSDS)?

An **MSDS** — also referred to as **SDS (Safety Data Sheet)** under the modern **GHS (Globally Harmonized System of Classification and Labelling of Chemicals)** framework — is a standardized, multi-section document that provides comprehensive information about the properties, hazards, protective measures, and emergency procedures related to a particular chemical substance or mixture.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Terminology Distinction:**
> - **MSDS (Material Safety Data Sheet)** — Older terminology, used prior to GHS adoption (still widely used in industry).
> - **SDS (Safety Data Sheet)** — Modern GHS-compliant terminology, contains 16 standardized sections.
> - The two terms are used interchangeably in industrial practice, but technically **SDS** is the current standard under **GHS Rev. 9 (2021)** adopted by OSHA, EU CLP, and Indian **IS 18018:2021**.

### 1.3.1 Purpose of MSDS
The MSDS serves four critical stakeholder groups:
- **Workers** — to understand hazards and use PPE correctly.
- **Employers / Safety Officers** — to conduct risk assessments and implement controls.
- **Emergency Responders** — firefighters, HazMat teams, and medical personnel.
- **Transporters & Regulators** — to comply with **ADR, IMDG, IATA, RID** regulations for hazardous goods transport.

## 1.4 The Four Fundamental Physical Hazard Properties

### 1.4.1 Flammability
A chemical is flammable if it can ignite easily and burn rapidly in air under normal conditions. Key metrics include:
- **Flash Point** — Lowest temperature at which a liquid gives off sufficient vapor to form an ignitable mixture with air near its surface. Units: °C or °F.
- **Fire Point** — Lowest temperature at which a liquid gives off sufficient vapor to sustain continuous combustion.
- **Auto-Ignition Temperature (AIT)** — Minimum temperature at which a substance ignites spontaneously without an external ignition source.

**Classification (GHS Flammable Liquids):**
| Category | Flash Point | Boiling Point |
|----------|-------------|---------------|
| **1** | < 23 °C | ≤ 35 °C |
| **2** | < 23 °C | > 35 °C |
| **3** | ≥ 23 °C and ≤ 60 °C | Any |
| **4** | > 60 °C and ≤ 93 °C | Any |

### 1.4.2 Reactivity / Instability
A reactive chemical undergoes violent chemical change (often exothermic) when subjected to shock, pressure, heat, or contact with another substance. Examples: organic peroxides, nitroglycerin, sodium metal in water.

### 1.4.3 Explosivity
A chemical explodes when it undergoes a rapid, self-propagating oxidation/reduction reaction producing gas, heat, and pressure at extremely high velocity. **Detonation velocity** can exceed **9000 m/s** (e.g., TNT ≈ 6900 m/s, RDX ≈ 8750 m/s).

### 1.4.4 Oxidizing Ability
Oxidizers (e.g., Potassium Permanganate KMnO₄, Hydrogen Peroxide H₂O₂, Nitric Acid HNO₃) supply oxygen and can cause or intensify the combustion of other materials.

## 1.5 The Three Core Health Hazard Properties

### 1.5.1 Acute Toxicity
- **LD₅₀ (Lethal Dose 50%)** — The single oral/dermal dose of a substance that kills **50% of a test animal population** (usually rats) within a specified time (typically 14 days). Unit: **mg/kg body weight**.
- **LC₅₀ (Lethal Concentration 50%)** — The concentration in air that kills 50% of test animals over a specified exposure time (usually 4 hours). Unit: **ppm (parts per million)** or **mg/m³**.

### 1.5.2 Chronic Toxicity
Long-term low-level exposure effects:
- **Carcinogenicity** — Cancer-causing (e.g., Benzene → Leukemia, Asbestos → Mesothelioma).
- **Mutagenicity** — DNA damage (e.g., Formaldehyde).
- **Reproductive Toxicity** — Affects fertility or fetal development (e.g., Lead, Mercury).
- **Sensitization** — Allergic response on repeated exposure (e.g., Isocyanates).

### 1.5.3 Corrosivity / Irritation
- **Corrosive** — Causes visible destruction of living tissue (skin corrosion) or metals. pH ≤ 2 or ≥ 11.5 typically indicates corrosivity.
- **Irritant** — Causes reversible inflammation of skin, eyes, or respiratory tract.

## 1.6 NFPA 704 Diamond — The Universal Hazard Label

The **National Fire Protection Association (NFPA) 704** standard presents a "fire diamond" with four colored quadrants rated 0–4, providing at-a-glance hazard information:

- **Blue (Left)** — Health Hazard
- **Red (Top)** — Flammability Hazard
- **Yellow (Right)** — Instability / Reactivity
- **White (Bottom)** — Special Hazards (OX = Oxidizer, W = Water-reactive, SA = Simple Asphyxiant, COR = Corrosive, BIO = Biological)

**Rating Scale:** **0** = No hazard; **1** = Slight; **2** = Moderate; **3** = High / Serious; **4** = Deadly / Extreme.

> [!NOTE]
> **Example:** Hydrogen peroxide (concentrated, 91%) is labelled NFPA 704 as **3-0-3-OX**, meaning high health hazard, no flammability, high reactivity, and a strong oxidizer.

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Detailed Taxonomy of Hazardous Chemical Properties

To understand MSDS deeply, a structured taxonomy is essential. The classification follows the **GHS (Globally Harmonized System)** of the United Nations, which is internationally recognized and forms the basis of the Indian **IS 18018:2021** standard.

### 2.1.1 Physical Hazard Sub-Classes (GHS — 16 Classes)
- Explosives
- Flammable Gases, Liquids, Solids, and Aerosols
- Oxidizing Gases, Liquids, and Solids
- Gases Under Pressure (Compressed, Liquefied, Refrigerated, Dissolved)
- Self-Heating Substances
- Pyrophoric Liquids and Solids
- Self-Reactive Substances
- Organic Peroxides
- Substances Corrosive to Metals
- Water-Reactive / Emitting Flammable Gases

### 2.1.2 Health Hazard Sub-Classes (GHS — 10 Classes)
- Acute Toxicity (Oral, Dermal, Inhalation)
- Skin Corrosion / Irritation
- Serious Eye Damage / Eye Irritation
- Respiratory or Skin Sensitization
- Germ Cell Mutagenicity
- Carcinogenicity
- Reproductive Toxicity
- Specific Target Organ Toxicity — Single Exposure (STOT-SE)
- Specific Target Organ Toxicity — Repeated Exposure (STOT-RE)
- Aspiration Hazard

### 2.1.3 Environmental Hazard Sub-Classes (GHS)
- Hazardous to the Aquatic Environment (Acute & Chronic)
- Hazardous to the Ozone Layer (ODS — Ozone Depletion Substances)

## 2.2 Quantitative Toxicological Metrics

### 2.2.1 Threshold Limit Value (TLV)
Established by the **American Conference of Governmental Industrial Hygienists (ACGIH)**, TLV represents the airborne concentration of a substance to which nearly all workers may be repeatedly exposed day after day without adverse health effects.

**Three Sub-Categories of TLV:**
1. **TLV-TWA (Time-Weighted Average)** — 8-hour workday, 40-hour workweek average exposure.
2. **TLV-STEL (Short-Term Exposure Limit)** — 15-minute average, not to exceed 4 times per day, with at least 60 minutes between exposures.
3. **TLV-C (Ceiling Limit)** — Absolute upper limit that should never be exceeded.

### 2.2.2 Permissible Exposure Limit (PEL)
Set by **OSHA**, legally enforceable in the USA. PELs are typically more conservative (lower) than TLVs.

### 2.2.3 Immediately Dangerous to Life or Health (IDLH)
The maximum airborne concentration from which a worker could escape within **30 minutes** without experiencing any irreversible health effects or impairing escape. Set by **NIOSH**.

### 2.2.4 Lethal Concentration / Dose Calculations

The **Haber's Law** (or **Concentration × Time (Ct) Product**) is fundamental:

$$C^n \times t = k$$

Where:
- **C** = Concentration of the toxic gas (ppm or mg/m³)
- **t** = Exposure time (minutes or hours)
- **n** = Toxicological exponent (typically 1 for most gases; n = 1 for Haber's law; n > 1 for cumulative toxicants)
- **k** = Toxicological constant specific to the chemical

For most industrial hygiene applications, **Haber's Law** assumes n = 1:

$$C \times t = k$$

This means the toxic effect is proportional to the product of concentration and exposure time. Doubling either doubles the toxic effect.

## 2.3 Probit Analysis for Quantifying Toxic Gas Hazards

The **Probit Equation** (Probability Unit) is used to estimate the percentage of population affected by a given toxic load. The standard form is:

$$Pr = a + b \ln(C^n \times t)$$

Where:
- **Pr** = Probit (Probability Unit)
- **a, b** = Regression constants specific to the chemical
- **C** = Concentration (ppm or mg/m³)
- **t** = Exposure duration (minutes)
- **n** = Exponent (typically 1)

**Conversion from Probit to Percentage:**

$$\text{Percentage} = 50 \times \left[1 + \text{erf}\left(\frac{Pr - 5}{\sqrt{2}}\right)\right]$$

Where **erf** is the Gaussian error function. The percentage of population experiencing the effect is read from standard probit conversion tables.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Parameter** | **Symbol / Formula** | **Units** | **Application** |
|---------------|----------------------|-----------|-----------------|
| Lethal Dose 50% | $\text{LD}_{50}$ | mg/kg | Single oral/dermal dose killing 50% test subjects |
| Lethal Concentration 50% | $\text{LC}_{50}$ | ppm or mg/m³ | Airborne concentration killing 50% over exposure period |
| Haber's Law | $C \times t = k$ | ppm·min | Linear dose-response toxic load |
| General Ct | $C^n \times t = k$ | ppmⁿ·min | Non-linear dose-response (n ≠ 1) |
| Probit | $Pr = a + b \ln(C^n \cdot t)$ | dimensionless | Probability of toxic effect |
| Vapor Density | $\text{VD} = \dfrac{M_{\text{vapor}}}{M_{\text{air}}}$ | ratio (air = 1) | Determines if gas rises (VD < 1) or sinks (VD > 1) |
| Boiling Point | $T_b$ | °C or K | Indicator of volatility |
| Vapor Pressure | $P_{\text{vap}}$ | mmHg or Pa | Evaporation rate indicator |
| Evaporation Rate | $\text{ER} = \dfrac{P_{\text{substance}}}{P_{\text{butyl acetate}}}$ | ratio (BuAc = 1) | Relative volatility |
| Flash Point | $T_{\text{flash}}$ | °C | Fire hazard classification |
| Auto-Ignition Temp | $T_{\text{AIT}}$ | °C | Spontaneous combustion threshold |
| LEL / UEL | Lower / Upper Explosive Limit | % by volume | Flammability range in air |
| Exposure Limit (TWA) | $\text{TLV-TWA}$ | ppm or mg/m³ | 8-hr safe exposure average |
| Ceiling Limit | $\text{TLV-C}$ | ppm or mg/m³ | Never-exceed threshold |
| STEL | $\text{TLV-STEL}$ | ppm or mg/m³ | 15-min short-term peak |
| IDLH | $\text{IDLH}$ | ppm | 30-min escape-safe level |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Utility of Haber's Law in Engineering Safety:**
> The Ct product is the cornerstone of Emergency Response Planning Guidelines (ERPGs) and Temporary Emergency Exposure Limits (TEELs). For example, **Ammonia (NH₃)** has an IDLH of **300 ppm**. Using Haber's Law: a worker exposed to 150 ppm for 60 minutes has the same toxic load as exposure to 300 ppm for 30 minutes. This allows safety engineers to design evacuation procedures, ventilation rates, and PPE specifications.

## 2.5 MSDS — The 16 Standardized Sections (GHS / SDS Format)

The modern **16-section SDS** (as per GHS Rev. 9, OSHA HCS 2012, and Indian IS 18018) is structured as follows:

| **Section** | **Title** | **Content Summary** |
|-------------|-----------|---------------------|
| 1 | Identification | Product name, manufacturer, emergency contact, recommended uses |
| 2 | Hazard(s) Identification | GHS classification, signal word, pictograms, hazard statements |
| 3 | Composition / Ingredients | Chemical identity, CAS number, concentration, impurities |
| 4 | First-Aid Measures | Inhalation, skin, eye, ingestion — symptoms, immediate action |
| 5 | Fire-Fighting Measures | Suitable extinguishing media, special hazards, firefighting PPE |
| 6 | Accidental Release | Spill/leak response, containment, decontamination, PPE |
| 7 | Handling and Storage | Safe practices, incompatibilities, storage conditions |
| 8 | Exposure Controls / PPE | TLV/PEL/IDLH, engineering controls, respiratory & skin PPE |
| 9 | Physical & Chemical Properties | Boiling point, melting point, density, vapor pressure, pH, solubility |
| 10 | Stability & Reactivity | Reactivity, conditions to avoid, incompatible materials, decomposition products |
| 11 | Toxicological Information | LD₅₀, LC₅₀, routes of exposure, symptoms, carcinogenicity (IARC/NTP/OSHA) |
| 12 | Ecological Information | Aquatic toxicity, bioaccumulation, persistence, mobility |
| 13 | Disposal Considerations | Waste treatment methods, contaminated packaging |
| 14 | Transport Information | UN number, shipping name, hazard class, packing group, marine pollutant |
| 15 | Regulatory Information | TSCA, REACH, Indian CWC, GHS compliance |
| 16 | Other Information | Date of preparation/revision, key references, disclaimer |

> [!NOTE]
> **Practical Exam Tip:** KTU examiners frequently ask students to list **all 16 sections of SDS** or to elaborate on **Sections 2, 4, 8, and 11** (Hazard Identification, First-Aid, Exposure Controls, and Toxicology). Master these four sections thoroughly.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Analytical Derivation — Probit Equation for Toxic Gas Lethality

The Probit equation is derived from the assumption that the percentage of population affected follows a **log-normal (Gaussian) distribution** of tolerance to a toxic load.

### 3.1.1 Derivation Steps

**Step 1:** Define the toxic load (Z) as:

$$Z = C^n \times t$$

This represents the cumulative dose received by an individual. For most industrial toxic gases (NH₃, Cl₂, HCl, CO), the exponent **n = 1** (Haber's Law). For cumulative toxins (heavy metals, radionuclides), **n > 1**.

**Step 2:** The natural logarithm of the toxic load is taken as the independent variable because toxicological tolerance data is typically log-normally distributed:

$$\ln(Z) = \ln(C^n \times t) = n \ln(C) + \ln(t)$$

**Step 3:** The Probit (Pr) is a linear transformation of the cumulative normal distribution. A Probit value of **5.0** corresponds to **50%** of the population; **Pr = 4.0** corresponds to ~16%; **Pr = 6.0** corresponds to ~84%.

**Step 4:** The linear Probit model is:

$$Pr = a + b \cdot \ln(C^n \times t)$$

where **a** and **b** are empirical regression coefficients determined by exposing test animals to multiple dose-response levels and fitting a least-squares regression.

### 3.1.2 Worked Example — Ammonia (NH₃) Toxicity

**Given:** For Ammonia gas, the probit equation coefficients are **a = −35.9** and **b = 1.85** (with n = 1). A community is exposed to **NH₃ concentration of 2000 ppm for 30 minutes**. Estimate the percentage of fatalities.

**Step-by-step Solution:**

**Step 1 — Calculate toxic load Z:**

$$Z = C^n \times t = (2000)^1 \times 30 = 60{,}000 \; \text{ppm·min}$$

**Step 2 — Calculate natural logarithm:**

$$\ln(Z) = \ln(60{,}000) = 11.0021$$

**Step 3 — Apply the Probit equation:**

$$Pr = a + b \cdot \ln(C^n \times t)$$

$$Pr = -35.9 + 1.85 \times 11.0021$$

$$Pr = -35.9 + 20.3539$$

$$Pr = -15.5461$$

Since **Pr < 0**, this means the toxic load is **insignificantly low** to cause fatality. The result is essentially a **0% fatality** scenario.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This example demonstrates that even seemingly high concentrations (2000 ppm) for short durations (30 min) may not cause mass fatalities if the chemical has a high toxicological threshold, as is the case with ammonia (IDLH = 300 ppm; fatality requires prolonged exposure at much higher levels).

### 3.1.3 Revised Example — Chlorine (Cl₂) Toxicity

**Given:** For Chlorine gas, probit coefficients are **a = −19.27** and **b = 3.13**. Exposure to **30 ppm for 30 minutes**.

**Step 1 — Calculate Z:**

$$Z = 30 \times 30 = 900 \; \text{ppm·min}$$

**Step 2 — Calculate ln(Z):**

$$\ln(900) = 6.8024$$

**Step 3 — Apply Probit equation:**

$$Pr = -19.27 + 3.13 \times 6.8024$$

$$Pr = -19.27 + 21.2915$$

$$Pr = 2.0215$$

**Step 4 — Convert Probit to Percentage using the standard probit-to-percentage table:**

For **Pr = 2.0**, the corresponding percentage is **~2.3%**.

Therefore, approximately **2.3%** of the exposed population is expected to suffer the defined effect (lethality, in this case).

| **Chemical** | **CAS No.** | **NFPA 704 Rating** | **TLV-TWA (ppm)** | **IDLH (ppm)** | **LC₅₀ (4-hr rat, ppm)** | **Physical State** | **GHS Pictogram** |
|--------------|-------------|---------------------|-------------------|----------------|--------------------------|---------------------|-------------------|
| Ammonia (NH₃) | 7664-41-7 | 3-1-0 | 25 | 300 | 2000 | Gas | GHS04, GHS05, GHS06, GHS09 |
| Chlorine (Cl₂) | 7782-50-5 | 3-0-0 | 0.5 | 10 | 293 | Gas | GHS04, GHS05, GHS06, GHS09 |
| Benzene (C₆H₆) | 71-43-2 | 2-3-0 | 0.5 (carcinogen) | 500 | 13700 | Liquid | GHS02, GHS07, GHS08 |
| Sulfuric Acid (H₂SO₄) | 7664-93-9 | 3-0-2 | 0.05 mg/m³ | 15 mg/m³ | 510 mg/m³ | Liquid | GHS05 |
| Acetone (C₃H₆O) | 67-64-1 | 1-3-0 | 500 | 2500 | 50100 | Liquid | GHS02, GHS07 |
| Hydrogen Peroxide (30%) | 7722-84-1 | 2-0-3-OX | 1 | 75 | 1418 ppm | Liquid | GHS03, GHS05, GHS07 |
| Carbon Monoxide (CO) | 630-08-0 | 2-4-0 | 25 | 1200 | 1807 | Gas | GHS02, GHS04, GHS06, GHS08 |
| Toluene (C₇H₈) | 108-88-3 | 2-3-0 | 20 | 500 | 12500 | Liquid | GHS02, GHS07, GHS08 |

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Analytical Matrix:**
> - The **NFPA 704 rating** is read as **Health-Flammability-Reactivity**, with special hazards in subscript.
> - **TLV-TWA** values for known carcinogens (Benzene) and irritants (H₂SO₄) are extremely low, indicating strict control requirements.
> - **LC₅₀** values are inversely related to chemical toxicity — a lower LC₅₀ indicates a more toxic substance.

## 3.3 Step-by-Step Derivation — Vapor Density and Ventilation Design

Vapor density is critical for determining whether a leaked gas will accumulate at floor level (requiring low-level ventilation) or rise to the ceiling (requiring high-level ventilation).

**Step 1 — Definition:**

$$\text{Vapor Density (VD)} = \frac{\text{Molar mass of vapor}}{\text{Molar mass of air}}$$

Molar mass of air ≈ **28.97 g/mol** (often rounded to **29 g/mol**).

**Step 2 — Worked Example — Carbon Disulfide (CS₂):**

Molar mass of CS₂ = 12.01 + (2 × 32.07) = 76.15 g/mol

$$\text{VD} = \frac{76.15}{28.97} = 2.63$$

Since **VD > 1**, CS₂ vapor is **2.63 times heavier than air**. In the event of a leak, the vapor will **sink and accumulate at floor level** and in low-lying areas (pits, trenches, basements). This has critical safety implications:

- **Ventilation inlets** must be at **high level** (roof).
- **Ventilation exhausts** must be at **low level** (floor).
- **Gas detectors** must be installed at **floor level**, not ceiling.

**Step 3 — Contrast with Methane (CH₄):**

Molar mass of CH₄ = 16.04 g/mol

$$\text{VD} = \frac{16.04}{28.97} = 0.55$$

Since **VD < 1**, methane rises and accumulates near the ceiling. Detectors and ventilation must be **ceiling-mounted**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Application — LNG/LPG Safety:**
> Liquefied Petroleum Gas (LPG, C₃H₈/C₄H₁₀ blend) has a vapor density of ~1.5–2.0. This is why **LPG detectors in domestic and industrial settings are mounted at floor level**. Contrast this with **LNG (Methane, CH₄)**, where detectors are mounted at the ceiling. The infamous **Bhopal Gas Tragedy (1984)** involved **Methyl Isocyanate (MIC, CH₃NCO)** with VD ≈ 1.4, which accumulated at ground level and caused thousands of fatalities in the low-lying slums surrounding the Union Carbide plant.

## 3.4 Step-by-Step Derivation — Lower Explosive Limit (LEL) Safety Margin

The **Lower Explosive Limit (LEL)** is the minimum concentration of a vapor in air below which propagation of a flame does not occur on contact with a source of ignition.

**Step 1 — Identification of LEL and UEL:**

For Acetone: LEL = **2.5%** (by volume in air); UEL = **12.8%**.

**Step 2 — Calculation of safe operating concentration (typically 10% of LEL):**

$$C_{\text{safe}} = 0.10 \times \text{LEL}$$

$$C_{\text{safe, acetone}} = 0.10 \times 2.5\% = 0.25\%$$

**Step 3 — Converting to mg/m³ for industrial hygiene applications:**

Using the formula:

$$C_{\text{mg/m}^3} = C_{\text{ppm}} \times \frac{M}{24.45} \quad \text{(at 25 °C, 1 atm)}$$

For Acetone (M = 58.08 g/mol):

$$C_{\text{mg/m}^3} = 2500 \; \text{ppm} \times \frac{58.08}{24.45} = 2500 \times 2.375 = 5938 \; \text{mg/m}^3$$

This represents the maximum safe acetone concentration (10% LEL) below which the atmosphere cannot support combustion.

```mermaid
            Y1[REACTIVITY\n0 Stable\n1 Mild Change\n2 Violent\n3 Shock Detonate\n4 Detonate]:::reac
        end
        subgraph BOTTOM [BOTTOM WHITE QUADRANT]
            W1[SPECIAL\nOX Oxidizer\nW Water Reactive\nSA Asphyxiant\nCOR Corrosive\nBIO Biological\nRAD Radioactive]:::spec
        end
    end

    classDef flam fill:#E74C3C,color:#FFF,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
    classDef hlth fill:#3498DB,color:#FFF,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
    classDef reac fill:#F1C40F,color:#000,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
    classDef spec fill:#FFFFFF,color:#000,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
```


# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define LD₅₀ and LC₅₀. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**Model Answer:**

**LD₅₀ (Lethal Dose 50%):** It is the single dose (administered orally or dermally) of a substance that is expected to kill **50% of a test animal population** (usually albino rats) under controlled laboratory conditions within a specified period, conventionally **14 days**. It is expressed in **mg per kg of body weight (mg/kg)**. A lower LD₅₀ value indicates a more toxic substance.

**LC₅₀ (Lethal Concentration 50%):** It is the concentration of a substance in air (or water) to which exposure for a defined duration (typically 4 hours) is expected to kill **50% of test animals**. It is expressed in **ppm (parts per million)** for gases and vapors, or **mg/m³** for particulates. Like LD₅₀, a lower LC₅₀ indicates higher toxicity.

> [!NOTE]
> Both LD₅₀ and LC₅₀ are statistically derived from probit analysis of dose-response curves, accounting for inter-species variability and applying uncertainty factors (typically 10× for animal-to-human extrapolation) when setting occupational exposure limits.

### Question 2: List the four quadrants of the NFPA 704 hazard rating diamond with their colors. [KTU University Exam — June 2023]

**Model Answer:**

The **NFPA 704 Standard System** for the Identification of the Hazards of Materials for Emergency Response uses a four-quadrant diamond, each quadrant color-coded:

1. **Blue (Left quadrant)** — **Health Hazard** — Rating 0 to 4 indicating severity of health effects.
2. **Red (Top quadrant)** — **Flammability Hazard** — Rating 0 to 4 based on flash point and ignition properties.
3. **Yellow (Right quadrant)** — **Instability / Reactivity Hazard** — Rating 0 to 4 based on thermal/chemical instability.
4. **White (Bottom quadrant)** — **Special Hazards** — Letter symbols for specific hazards (OX, W, SA, COR, BIO, RAD).

The **rating scale (0–4)** is interpreted as: **0** = No hazard; **1** = Slight; **2** = Moderate; **3** = High/Serious; **4** = Deadly/Extreme.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (14 Marks) [Internal Choice Option 1]

**Q. (a) Explain in detail the various hazardous properties of chemicals used in industries, classifying them under physical, health, and environmental hazards with suitable examples. [7 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

Industrial chemicals exhibit a wide range of hazardous properties. These are systematically classified into three primary categories:

**1. Physical Hazards:**

Physical hazards relate to a chemical's ability to undergo physical changes that result in fire, explosion, or pressure-related incidents.

- **Flammability** — Chemicals that readily ignite and burn. *Example: Acetone (Flash Point −20 °C), Methanol (Flash Point 11 °C).* GHS Categories 1–4 classify flammable liquids.
- **Explosivity** — Chemicals that undergo rapid decomposition releasing gas, heat, and pressure. *Example: TNT, RDX, Ammonium Nitrate (ANFO), Nitroglycerin.*
- **Reactivity / Instability** — Chemicals that react violently with water, air, or other chemicals. *Example: Sodium metal (reacts explosively with water), Acetylene (unstable above 2 bar gauge).*
- **Oxidizing Ability** — Chemicals that supply oxygen and intensify combustion. *Example: Potassium Permanganate (KMnO₄), Hydrogen Peroxide (H₂O₂), Nitric Acid (HNO₃).*
- **Compressed Gases** — Gases stored under high pressure pose projectile and asphyxiation risks. *Example: Oxygen cylinders, Liquid Nitrogen, LPG.*

**2. Health Hazards:**

Health hazards affect human biological systems through acute or chronic exposure.

- **Acute Toxicity** — Rapid harmful effects from a single short-term exposure. Quantified by **LD₅₀** (oral/dermal) and **LC₅₀** (inhalation). *Example: Hydrogen Cyanide (HCN) has LC₅₀ (30 min) of ~100 ppm — extremely toxic.*
- **Chronic Toxicity** — Long-term low-level exposure effects:
  - **Carcinogenicity** — *Example: Benzene → Leukemia; Asbestos → Mesothelioma; Formaldehyde → Nasopharyngeal cancer.*
  - **Mutagenicity** — *Example: Ethylene Oxide, ionizing radiation.*
  - **Reproductive Toxicity** — *Example: Lead causes reduced fertility and fetal neurological damage.*
- **Corrosivity** — Causes irreversible destruction of skin tissue (corrosion) or metals. pH < 2 or > 11.5 typically indicates corrosivity. *Example: Concentrated Sulfuric Acid (H₂SO₄), Sodium Hydroxide (NaOH), Hydrofluoric Acid (HF).*
- **Sensitization** — Allergic reactions upon repeated exposure. *Example: Isocyanates (asthma), Nickel salts (contact dermatitis).*
- **Target Organ Toxicity** — Specific damage to organs:
  - **Neurotoxicants** — *Lead, Mercury, Manganese, n-Hexane.*
  - **Hepatotoxicants** — *Carbon Tetrachloride (CCl₄), Vinyl Chloride.*
  - **Nephrotoxicants** — *Cadmium, certain halogenated solvents.*
  - **Hematotoxicants** — *Benzene, Carbon Monoxide (CO).*

**3. Environmental Hazards:**

- **Aquatic Toxicity** — *Example: Mercury, Organochlorine pesticides (DDT), Polycyclic Aromatic Hydrocarbons (PAHs).* Quantified by **LC₅₀ for fish (96-hr)** and **EC₅₀ for Daphnia (48-hr)**.
- **Ozone Depletion Potential (ODP)** — *Example: Chlorofluorocarbons (CFCs), Halons.* ODP of CFC-11 = 1.0 (reference).
- **Global Warming Potential (GWP)** — *Example: CO₂ (GWP = 1), Methane (GWP = 25), SF₆ (GWP = 23500).*
- **Soil Contamination & Bioaccumulation** — *Example: Persistent Organic Pollutants (POPs) like DDT, PCBs.*

**Q. (b) What is MSDS? Explain any eight important sections of a Material Safety Data Sheet in detail. [7 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition of MSDS (Material Safety Data Sheet):**

A Material Safety Data Sheet (MSDS), now technically termed **Safety Data Sheet (SDS)** under the GHS framework, is a **standardized 16-section document** that provides comprehensive information about a chemical's identity, hazards, protective measures, and emergency response procedures. It is a legal requirement under **OSHA HCS (29 CFR 1910.1200)**, **REACH (EU 1907/2006)**, and **Indian IS 18018:2021** for manufacturers and importers of hazardous chemicals.

**Eight Important Sections Explained:**

**Section 1 — Identification:**
Contains the product name (as labeled), manufacturer's/supplier's name, address, emergency phone number, recommended use, and restrictions on use. *Example: "Acetone, CAS 67-64-1, manufactured by XYZ Chemicals Ltd., Emergency Contact +91-XXXXX."*

**Section 2 — Hazard(s) Identification:**
Includes GHS classification, **signal word** ("Danger" for severe, "Warning" for moderate), **hazard statements** (H-codes like H225 = Highly Flammable Liquid), and **precautionary statements** (P-codes), along with the nine standardized **GHS pictograms** (flame, skull, exclamation mark, etc.).

**Section 3 — Composition / Information on Ingredients:**
Lists chemical ingredients, their **CAS (Chemical Abstracts Service) numbers**, concentrations, and any impurities or stabilizers. CAS numbers are universal unique identifiers (e.g., Benzene CAS 71-43-2).

**Section 4 — First-Aid Measures:**
Describes immediate medical response for exposure via **inhalation, skin contact, eye contact, and ingestion**. Includes symptoms/effects (acute and delayed) and indication of need for immediate medical attention.

**Section 5 — Fire-Fighting Measures:**
Specifies **suitable extinguishing media** (water spray, foam, CO₂, dry chemical), special hazards arising from the chemical (e.g., toxic combustion products), and **protective equipment for firefighters** (SCBA, full bunker gear).

**Section 6 — Accidental Release Measures:**
Covers personal precautions, **PPE for spill responders**, emergency procedures, methods for **containment** (dikes, absorbents), and cleanup techniques. *Example: For a mercury spill, use a mercury vacuum, not a regular vacuum.*

**Section 7 — Handling and Storage:**
Outlines safe handling practices (ventilation, no smoking, grounding), **incompatible materials** (e.g., oxidizers away from flammables), and storage conditions (temperature, container type, segregation).

**Section 8 — Exposure Controls / Personal Protective Equipment:**
Lists **TLV-TWA, TLV-STEL, TLV-C, PEL, and IDLH** values. Specifies **engineering controls** (fume hoods, ventilation) and **PPE** (gloves, respirators, eye protection, protective clothing) with standards (e.g., NIOSH N95, EN 374 chemical-resistant gloves).

### Question B (14 Marks) [Internal Choice Option 2]

**Q. (a) Define the terms TLV-TWA, TLV-STEL, TLV-C, PEL, and IDLH. Explain the significance of each in industrial hygiene monitoring. [7 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

**TLV-TWA (Threshold Limit Value — Time-Weighted Average):**
The time-weighted average concentration for a **conventional 8-hour workday and 40-hour workweek** to which nearly all workers may be repeatedly exposed without adverse health effects. *Example: Toluene TLV-TWA = 20 ppm.*
**Significance:** Used for chronic exposure assessment. Average concentration must be calculated as:

$$C_{\text{TWA}} = \frac{\sum C_i \times t_i}{\sum t_i}$$

where Cᵢ and tᵢ are concentration and time at each work phase.

**TLV-STEL (Short-Term Exposure Limit):**
The **15-minute time-weighted average** exposure that should not be exceeded at any time during the workday, even if the 8-hour TWA is within limits. Maximum **4 STEL periods per day** with at least **60 minutes** between them.
**Significance:** Protects against acute effects from short-term peak exposures (e.g., solvent vapors during transfer operations).

**TLV-C (Ceiling Limit):**
The **absolute concentration that must never be exceeded**, even instantaneously.
**Significance:** Applied to fast-acting chemicals where any brief overexposure can cause immediate harm. *Example: Hydrogen Chloride (HCl) TLV-C = 2 ppm.*

**PEL (Permissible Exposure Limit):**
The legally enforceable limit set by **OSHA (USA)** under 29 CFR 1910.1000. PELs are typically more conservative (lower values) than TLVs. PEL-TWA values were largely adopted from the 1968 ACGIH TLV list.
**Significance:** Non-compliance with PELs is a legal violation under US occupational law. India follows similar standards under the **Factories Act 1948 and State Factory Rules**.

**IDLH (Immediately Dangerous to Life or Health):**
The maximum airborne concentration from which a worker could escape within **30 minutes** without experiencing any irreversible health effects or impairment of escape ability. Set by **NIOSH**.
**Significance:** Determines the level above which **supplied-air respirators (SCBA)** are mandatory. Also defines the conditions for confined space entry rescue planning.

**Q. (b) With the help of a Probit equation, estimate the percentage of population likely to experience lethal effects if exposed to Chlorine (Cl₂) at a concentration of 50 ppm for 20 minutes. Given: a = −19.27, b = 3.13, n = 1. [7 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

**Given Data:**
- Chemical: Chlorine (Cl₂)
- Concentration, C = 50 ppm
- Exposure time, t = 20 minutes
- Probit constants: a = −19.27, b = 3.13
- Exponent: n = 1

**Step 1 — State the Probit equation:**

$$Pr = a + b \cdot \ln(C^n \times t)$$

**Step 2 — Calculate the toxic load (Z):**

$$Z = C^n \times t = 50^1 \times 20 = 1000 \; \text{ppm·min}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the natural logarithm:**

$$\ln(Z) = \ln(1000) = 6.9078$$

**Step 4 — Substitute into the Probit equation:**

$$Pr = -19.27 + 3.13 \times 6.9078$$

$$Pr = -19.27 + 21.6214$$

$$Pr = 2.3514$$

**Step 5 — Convert Probit to Percentage using the Probit-to-Percentage table:**

For Pr = 2.35, the corresponding percentage is approximately **2.6%** (interpolating between Pr = 2.3 → 2.3% and Pr = 2.4 → 2.9%).

**Final Answer:** Approximately **2.6%** of the exposed population is estimated to suffer lethal effects from exposure to 50 ppm Chlorine for 20 minutes.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the natural logarithm** — Students often write "log" but mean "ln" (natural log to base *e*). The probit equation uses **ln**, not log₁₀. This single error cascades through the entire calculation and loses 4–5 marks.
> 2. **Unit inconsistency in Z** — Concentration must be in **ppm** and time in **minutes** (or consistently in any other unit). Mixing units (e.g., ppm with seconds) loses 1 mark.
> 3. **Probit-to-Percentage conversion** — KTU students frequently forget to convert the probit value to a percentage using the standard probit table. Always include this final conversion step.
> 4. **MSDS Section Recall** — In the 14-mark question, students often list the 16 sections but fail to **elaborate** on 8 of them. Examiners require at least 2–3 lines of description per section.
> 5. **NFPA 704 Confusion** — Many students confuse the **Blue** and **Red** quadrants. Memorize: **Blue (Left) = Health, Red (Top) = Flammability, Yellow (Right) = Reactivity, White (Bottom) = Special.**
> 6. **Vapor Density Interpretation** — Many students incorrectly state that gases with VD > 1 are "lighter than air" — the correct interpretation is they are **heavier** than air and settle at low levels.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 4: Hazardous Chemical Properties & MSDS**

### Key Definitions
- **Hazardous Chemical:** Any substance with intrinsic property to cause harm to life, property, or environment.
- **MSDS/SDS:** Standardized 16-section document (GHS compliant) detailing chemical hazards, handling, and emergency response.
- **LD₅₀:** Lethal dose killing 50% of test population (mg/kg).
- **LC₅₀:** Lethal concentration killing 50% over exposure period (ppm or mg/m³).
- **Flash Point:** Lowest temperature at which a liquid emits ignitable vapor.
- **LEL/UEL:** Lower/Upper Explosive Limits (% vapor in air).
- **Vapor Density:** Molar mass of vapor / Molar mass of air (28.97 g/mol).
- **TLV-TWA:** 8-hr average safe exposure.
- **TLV-STEL:** 15-min short-term exposure limit.
- **TLV-C:** Absolute ceiling never to be exceeded.
- **IDLH:** 30-min escape-safe concentration.

### Key Formulas
- **Haber's Law:** $C \times t = k$
- **Probit Equation:** $Pr = a + b \cdot \ln(C^n \times t)$
- **TWA Calculation:** $C_{\text{TWA}} = \frac{\sum C_i t_i}{\sum t_i}$
- **Vapor Density:** $\text{VD} = M_{\text{vapor}} / M_{\text{air}}$
- **LEL Safety Margin:** $C_{\text{safe}} = 0.10 \times \text{LEL}$

### Key Classification Standards
- **GHS** (Globally Harmonized System) — United Nations standard.
- **NFPA 704** — 4-quadrant hazard diamond (Blue/Red/Yellow/White).
- **OSHA HCS (29 CFR 1910.1200)** — USA standard.
- **REACH (EC 1907/2006)** — EU standard.
- **IS 18018:2021** — Indian GHS standard.
- **IARC, NTP, ACGIH** — Carcinogen classification agencies.

### GHS Pictogram Categories (9 total)
- **GHS01** — Explosive
- **GHS02** — Flammable
- **GHS03** — Oxidizing
- **GHS04** — Compressed Gas
- **GHS05** — Corrosive
- **GHS06** — Acute Toxicity (Skull)
- **GHS07** — Harmful / Irritant
- **GHS08** — Health Hazard (Carcinogen)
- **GHS09** — Environmental Hazard

### SDS 16 Sections — Must-Know Order
Identification → Hazards → Composition → First Aid → Fire → Spill → Storage → PPE → Properties → Reactivity → Toxicology → Ecology → Disposal → Transport → Regulation → Other.

### Historical Lessons
- **Bhopal Disaster (1984):** MIC leak — 3000+ deaths; MSDS deficiencies exposed.
- **Flixborough Disaster (1974):** Cyclohexane vapor explosion — NFPA and OSHA frameworks developed subsequently.
- **Minamata Disease (Japan, 1956):** Methylmercury poisoning — strict MSDS for heavy metals enforced.

### Practical Field Application
- Always check **Section 8 (PPE)** and **Section 2 (Pictograms)** before handling.
- Confirm **Section 14 (Transport Info)** for hazardous goods shipment.
- In emergencies, immediately refer to **Section 4 (First Aid)** and **Section 5 (Fire Fighting)**.
- For storage, strictly follow **Section 7** and **Section 10 (Incompatibilities)**.
